questionText:
The diagram shows a flow chart of the reaction of a human body to an increase in temperature.

Fill in the three blank steps on the flow chart.

[Flow chart showing: Body temperature rises → (blank) → Nerve impulses travel to blood vessels / Nerve impulses travel to sweat glands → Sweat produced on skin / Excess blood flow to skin → Heat loss from blood flow through skin / (blank) → Body temperature falls / Body temperature falls]
diagramCaption: Flow chart showing the negative feedback loop for thermoregulation in humans with three blank steps to be filled in.
sampleAnswer:
1. Thermoreceptors in the skin detect the temperature change and send nerve impulses to the hypothalamus (control centre).
2. The hypothalamus sends nerve impulses to the blood vessels and sweat glands.
3. Evaporation of sweat from the skin removes heat energy from the body.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Complete the table to show the differences between somatic and germ-line mutations.

| | Somatic mutation | Germ-line mutation |
|---|---|---|
| Location | | |
| Effect on offspring | | |
| Example | | |
diagramCaption: Table with rows for Location, Effect on offspring, and Example, comparing somatic and germ-line mutations.
sampleAnswer:
Somatic mutation: Location = Body cells (not gametes). Effect on offspring = Not passed to offspring. Example = Mutation in skin cell DNA leading to skin cancer.

Germ-line mutation: Location = Sex cells/gametes only. Effect on offspring = May be passed to offspring. Example = Mutation in sex cell leading to haemophilia.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how educational programs can be effective in reducing the incidence of non-infectious diseases. Support your answer with examples.
sampleAnswer: Public education programs can raise awareness of the risk of exposure to various harmful environmental agents. For example UV radiation can cause melanoma and tobacco smoke increases the risk of lung cancer. Programs like 'Slip, Slop, Slap, Seek, Slide' educate people about sun protection, while 'Quit' campaigns address smoking. As a result of the programs, people can alter their behaviour to reduce their exposure to harmful situations. For example not everyone can avoid the sun in their daily lives but the program encourages them to wear a hat, shirt and sunscreen so that exposure to UV radiation is reduced. This reduces the risk of melanoma.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the loss of biodiversity that may result from TWO biotechnologies used in agriculture.
sampleAnswer:
Biodiversity refers to the variety of species present in an ecosystem and also the genetic diversity of the species present.

Genetic Engineering has allowed modified corn such as Bt corn to be produced. Bt corn has a gene from Bacillus thuringiensis incorporated into its genome. The Bt gene allows the corn to produce a toxin that kills caterpillars when they eat it, thus reducing the number of caterpillars present in the ecosystem. This may reduce the food available for species that feed on them, thus affecting food chains and food webs, potentially reducing the number of species that can survive and therefore biodiversity.

Artificial Insemination in agriculture involves collecting semen from desirable male animals such as bulls. Semen can be frozen, stored and transported to other locations and can be used to inseminate many females in order to produce multiple offspring with desirable traits. This results in a loss of biodiversity as one male has many offspring. All the offspring will be genetically half siblings so will have reduced genetic variation.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A human karyotype that shows evidence of chromosomal mutation is shown.

Identify the evidence of chromosomal mutation in the karyotype.
diagramCaption: A human karyotype showing chromosomes arranged in pairs, with evidence of a chromosomal mutation (only one sex chromosome present).
sampleAnswer: There is only one copy of the sex chromosome (monosomy of the X chromosome, indicating Turner syndrome - 45,X).

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how cell division and fertilisation could lead to the production of this karyotype.
sampleAnswer: If homologous chromosomes in Meiosis I (or sister chromatids in Meiosis II) do not separate correctly (non-disjunction), one gamete will end up with 2 copies of the same chromosome and another gamete will end up without a copy of this chromosome. After fertilisation, the gamete with no copy of the sex chromosome has joined with a normal gamete to produce an individual with only one sex chromosome.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The map shows the percentage of adult indigenous populations able to digest lactose.

The ability to digest lactose is due to the presence of an enzyme (lactase) which can metabolise the sugar (lactose) present in milk. The gene responsible for producing lactase is usually permanently switched off at some time between the ages of 2 and 5 years. However, some people remain able to digest lactose throughout their lives.

With reference to evolution and DNA, provide possible reasons for the distribution shown in the map.
diagramCaption: World map showing the percentage of adult indigenous populations able to digest lactose, with high percentages (90-100%) in Northern Europe and low percentages (0-10%) in East Asia and Australia.
sampleAnswer: The map shows that ability to digest lactose varies in adult populations around the world. For example it is much lower in Australia than Northern Europe. This variation is likely to be due to natural selection where the presence of milk in the diet is the selective pressure. A mutation to a gene is likely to cause the continued production of lactase past the age of five years. Adults who possess the mutation are likely to have an increased chance of survival as they have increased nutrition in their diets. They then reproduce and pass the mutation on to their offspring, making it more common in the population. Populations that have remained largely lactose intolerant are less likely to have had milk available and thus a mutation of the lactase gene would not offer any advantage and would not become more common in the population.

What type of reproduction is budding?

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline a procedure that could be used to test the effect of temperature on reproduction in yeast.
sampleAnswer:
1. Prepare a suspension of yeast cells.
2. Prepare water baths at three temperatures (15°C, 25°C and 35°C).
3. Mix the suspension.
4. Measure and place 5 mL of suspension into 9 test tubes.
5. Place three test tubes in each water bath.
6. Incubate for 2 hours and mix.
7. Take a drop from each test tube and place on a microscope slide with a coverslip.
8. Count the yeast cells using a mm grid on low power at each temperature and record the data.
9. Compare the number of cells recorded at each temperature.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant condition caused by a mutation of a gene on chromosome 4. It causes nerve cells to break down. Stargardt disease is an autosomal recessive condition caused by a mutation of a different gene on chromosome 4. It causes damage to the retina.

A patient is heterozygous for both Huntington's (Hh) and Stargardt disease (Rr). His father's extended family has numerous cases of both of these diseases. His mother does not have either disease and is homozygous for both genes.

Complete the tables, showing the TWO alleles the patient inherited from each parent.
diagramCaption: Two blank tables for recording alleles inherited from each parent.
sampleAnswer: Alleles from father: H, r. Alleles from mother: h, R.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows the patient's homologous pair of chromosome 4 at various stages of meiosis.

Add the relevant alleles to the diagram to model the production of possible gamete combinations. Include a key and an example of crossing over.
diagramCaption: Diagram showing homologous pair of chromosome 4 at various stages of meiosis with spaces to add alleles and model crossing over.
sampleAnswer: The homologous pair before crossing over shows one chromosome with H and r alleles (from father) and the other with h and R alleles (from mother). After duplication, each chromosome consists of two identical chromatids. Crossing over exchanges segments between non-sister chromatids, producing recombinant chromatids. Possible gametes include: HR, Hr, hR, hr (where HR and hr are recombinant types produced by crossing over).

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe ONE mechanism by which plants maintain internal water homeostasis.
sampleAnswer: Plants can control movement of water out of the leaf to keep a stable internal environment with respect to water, by altering the size of the opening of the stomates. The opening and closing is under hormonal control, for example abscisic acid is a stress hormone produced by plants when internal water is low, causing stomata to close thus reducing water loss.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Experiments were conducted to obtain data on the traits 'seed shape' in plants and 'feather colour' in chickens. In each case, the original parents were pure breeding and produced the first generation (F1). The frequency data diagrams below relate to the second generation offspring (F2), produced when the F1 generations were bred together.

Graph A: Seed shape - shows a ratio of approximately 3:1 for two phenotypes.
Graph B: Feather colour - shows a ratio of approximately 1:2:1 for three phenotypes.

Explain the phenotypic ratios of the F2 generation in both the plant and chicken breeding experiments. Include Punnett squares and a key to support your answer.
diagramCaption: Two bar graphs showing phenotypic ratios: Graph A shows seed shape with ~3:1 ratio for 2 phenotypes, Graph B shows feather colour with ~1:2:1 ratio for 3 phenotypes.
sampleAnswer:
Graph A shows a ratio of 3:1 of the two phenotypes. This is typical of dominant/recessive alleles. The pure breeding parents must have been homozygous for seed shape eg RR for round and rr for wrinkled. The first generation would all have been round heterozygous Rr. When this generation was self-pollinated to provide the second generation: R r / R RR Rr / r Rr rr. KEY: R = Round seed, r = Wrinkled seed. This gives 3 round : 1 wrinkled.

Graph B shows a ratio of 1:2:1 for the three phenotypes. This is typical of co-dominant alleles. Neither allele is dominant and both are expressed. In the F1 generation, the chickens express both alleles and are heterogeneous. FB FW / FB FBFB FBFW / FW FBFW FWFW. KEY: FB = Black feathers, FW = White feathers. This results in 25% black feathers, 50% with black and white feathers, 25% white feathers.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline ONE adaptation of a specific pathogen that facilitates its entry into a host.
sampleAnswer: The bacterium Helicobacter pylori causes stomach ulcers in humans. It has a flagellum that allows it to move and penetrate the mucus lining/barrier of the stomach wall.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how the mode of transmission of pathogens influences the spread of diseases.
sampleAnswer: Some diseases are spread easily by the pathogen passing between people in droplets in the air, eg influenza virus, and therefore the rates of infection are high in populated areas. If an intermediate host or a vector is required then the spread of the disease may be slower depending on the presence of the vector.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following data shows the global distribution and burden of malaria and dengue fever.

Based on the data provided, identify trends in the global disease burden for both malaria and dengue fever.
diagramCaption: Maps and data tables showing the global distribution and burden of malaria and dengue fever over time.
sampleAnswer: The distribution of dengue fever appears to have increased markedly since 1950. There are many more parts of the world such as South America and Africa that are now affected. The number of countries with reported cases of malaria has decreased significantly. However, there is a growing number of people at risk, but representing a smaller percentage of the global population.

subject: Biology
questionText: Analyse factors that could have contributed to the change in global distribution of malaria and dengue fever.
sampleAnswer: The global distribution of both dengue fever and malaria is likely to be associated with mosquito vectors. Airline travel has increased markedly over the last century offering opportunities for both infected people and the mosquito vectors to be transported around the world. In addition, world population has increased, which will increase the density of potential hosts of both diseases. With increasing urbanisation of a larger population, new urban habitats for the mosquitoes could have emerged. These factors have led to the increased distribution of dengue fever and the increased population at risk of malaria, but the number of countries with malaria has shrunk. This suggests that the mosquito vector for malaria has been contained within known areas through pesticide spraying on water bodies to kill mosquitoes, or by strict quarantine regulations. Medical advances have the capacity to prevent or control these diseases, for example development of vaccines, especially for a viral disease such as dengue fever. From the data it would seem that medicines have had a greater effect in containing malaria than dengue fever. Vaccines and pharmaceuticals, such as antibiotics and antimalarial drugs, appear to have been more effective for malaria than for dengue fever.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Alzheimer's disease causes destruction of brain tissue, dementia and eventually death.

Amyloid beta protein is produced in the human brain throughout life. In people with Alzheimer's disease, it accumulates in excessive amounts.

Outline the main steps that brain cells use to make proteins such as amyloid beta.
diagramCaption: Diagram showing the effect of Alzheimer's disease on the brain, comparing a healthy brain with a brain affected by Alzheimer's disease showing plaques.
sampleAnswer: The DNA is unzipped and a complementary mRNA strand is transcribed. The mRNA moves to the ribosomes where translation occurs. Each codon is matched to a tRNA molecule with a complementary anticodon and carries a specific amino acid. The amino acids are joined together to form a polypeptide/protein.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The gene with the greatest known effect on the risk of developing late-onset Alzheimer's disease is called APOE. It is found on chromosome 19. The APOE gene has multiple alleles, including e2, e3 and e4.

What are multiple alleles?
sampleAnswer: Alleles are different versions of a gene. 'Multiple alleles' refers to three or more versions of a gene existing in a population.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The table shows the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease for various APOE genotypes compared to average risk in the population.

| APOE genotype | e2/e2 | e2/e3 | e2/e4 | e3/e3 | e3/e4 | e4/e4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk | 40% less likely | 40% less likely | 2.6 times more likely | Average | 3.2 times more likely | 14.9 times more likely |

Analyse the data to assess the risk of developing Alzheimer's disease associated with the e2, e3 and e4 alleles.
diagramCaption: Table showing risk of developing Alzheimer's disease for various APOE genotypes.
sampleAnswer: The presence of e2 reduces the risk of AD. When it is present in the homozygous genotype or heterozygous with e3 there is a 40% reduction in risk of AD. The e2 allele appears to mask the effect of the e3 allele (e2 is dominant over e3). When e2 is combined with e4 AD is 2.6 times more likely, suggesting that e2 cannot fully mask e4. If two e4 alleles are present the risk of AD is greatly increased and is 14.9 times more likely. This suggests that e2 reduces the risk of AD and that e4 significantly increases the risk of AD. When the individual is homozygous for the e3 allele there is an average risk of AD. However when e3 and e4 are present together the presence of e3 appears to reduce the increased risk that is the result of the e4 allele (e3/e4 makes AD 3.2 times more likely).

subject: Biology
questionText:
A large epidemiological study was conducted. It used historical data to investigate the association between Herpes simplex virus (HSV) infection and dementia. The study used the records of 8362 patients with HSV infection and 25 086 randomly selected sex- and age-matched control patients without HSV infection. Some of the patients with HSV had been treated with antiviral medication.

The graph below shows some results of the study.

Describe the trends shown in the data.
diagramCaption: Graph showing cumulative risk of dementia over years of follow-up for three groups: HSV infection without treatment, HSV infection with treatment, and without HSV.
sampleAnswer: Patients with untreated HSV infection have an increased risk of developing dementia each year compared to patients who are not infected or are treated. Both treated and untreated HSV infection groups show a sharp increase in the risk of dementia after 10 years, but the untreated HSV group increases to more than double the risk of the treated HSV group. The group that did not have HSV infection only has a very small increase in risk after 10 years.

subject: Biology
questionText: Diseases are classified as infectious or non-infectious. Evaluate whether Alzheimer's disease should be classified as an infectious disease or a non-infectious disease. In your answer, include reference to the information and data provided throughout Question 33.
sampleAnswer:
Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens that can be passed from one person to another. A pathogen is established as a cause of disease using a rigorous set of criteria known as Koch's postulates.

The data from the study provides evidence for an association between untreated viral (HSV) infection and the increased risk of development of dementia. The data also shows that treating HSV patients with antiviral medication results in a much lower risk of dementia over the following years. This provides some evidence that the disease may be infectious – a result of infection with the virus Herpes simplex.

However, this does not prove that HSV actually causes dementia, as Koch's postulates would need to be fulfilled. Additionally the historical study obtained results about dementia, and not all of the dementia cases would have been Alzheimer's disease.

Non-infectious diseases do not spread from person to person and are not caused by pathogens. They are caused by other factors such as environmental factors or inherited genes. AD is the result of the build up of amyloid protein. Protein synthesis is regulated by genes, suggesting the disease is non-infectious. The data about the APOE gene indicates that the presence of certain alleles, such as APOE-e4, result in a large increase in the risk of developing AD.

From the information provided it is not possible to classify Alzheimer's disease as infectious or non-infectious as there appears to be evidence that the risk of developing it is influenced by both a viral pathogen and genes.

sampleAnswer: Washing hands after going to the toilet removes bacteria from the skin. The purification of drinking water will kill the bacteria present in the water. Proper disposal of sewage would stop the exposure of people to the bacteria.

subject: Biology

sampleAnswer: Antibiotics can be used to treat bacterial infections as they can inhibit bacterial growth. Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is becoming increasingly common and is reducing the effectiveness of many antibiotics.

subject: Biology
questionText:
An indicator of kidney function is the volume of filtrate formed at the glomerulus in 1 minute (GFR).

GFR of healthy adult > 100 mL min⁻¹
GFR needing dialysis < 15 mL min⁻¹

A patient's kidney function was monitored and the following data recorded.

| Year | 2011 | 2012 | 2013 | 2014 | 2015 | 2016 | 2017 | 2018 | 2019 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GFR (mL min⁻¹) | 81 | 76 | 77 | 77 | 79 | 65 | 60 | 45 | 35 |

Plot the data on the grid.
diagramCaption: Grid for plotting GFR data over years 2011-2019.
sampleAnswer: A correctly plotted graph with Year on the x-axis and GFR (mL min⁻¹) on the y-axis, with all nine data points accurately plotted.

subject: Biology
questionText: Use the graph to show the year that the patient is predicted to require dialysis. Show your working and answer on the graph.
diagramCaption: Graph with plotted data requiring a line of best fit to predict when GFR falls below 15.
sampleAnswer: Using a suitable line of best fit extending the trend, the patient's GFR is predicted to reach below 15 mL min⁻¹ around 2022.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how dialysis compensates for the loss of a function of the kidneys.
sampleAnswer: Loss of kidney function may result in a failure to remove urea from blood. In dialysis, blood from the patient passes through selectively permeable dialysis tubing. Because the urea diffuses from the high concentrations in the blood to the low concentrations in the dialysate, the urea is removed from the blood.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Students tested the hypothesis that the number of eggs/young produced was greater in animals using external fertilisation than those using internal fertilisation. They obtained data from secondary sources.

The data shows:
- Internal fertilisation mean ± SD: 43 ± 55
- External fertilisation mean ± SD: 40 ± 32

What conclusion can be drawn from the data? Justify your answer.
diagramCaption: Table showing species data for internal and external fertilisation with mean ± SD values.
sampleAnswer: Since there is little difference between the mean numbers of young/eggs produced in animals using the two modes of reproduction, and the variability of the data is large as shown by the standard deviations, the students could conclude that there is no difference between the number of young produced and the mode of fertilisation.

subject: Biology
questionText: Justify an improvement to the students' experimental design to test the same hypothesis.
sampleAnswer: The students have selected only a very few species for their study and either by chance or by specific selection of these species the number of eggs/young born are similar. A much larger number of species should be included if the current hypothesis is to be reinvestigated.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain ONE advantage for animals of using external fertilisation.
sampleAnswer: Animals using external fertilisation will expend less energy on gestation, as this will occur outside the body.

subject: Biology
questionText:
One of the genes involved in determining the colour of a species of fish has two alleles: yellow and orange.

The diagram shows a pedigree chart for the inheritance of colour in the fish.

Use the pedigree chart to explain why the yellow allele is recessive.
diagramCaption: Pedigree chart showing inheritance of yellow and orange colour in fish.
sampleAnswer: The inheritance of yellow colour is recessive since both parents are orange but have yellow offspring. The yellow allele must be present in both parents but it is not expressed.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how a cross between individuals I and II could be used to determine whether the inheritance of colour in the fish is sex-linked or autosomal.
sampleAnswer:
If the inheritance is sex linked, then I and II would be XᴬY and XᵃXᵃ respectively. A cross between I and II would result in all male offspring being yellow and all female offspring being orange.

If the inheritance is not sex linked then II would have the genotype aa, whereas I would either have the genotype AA or Aa. If I was AA then all of the offspring would be orange. But if I was Aa, then 50% of the offspring would be yellow while the other 50% would be orange and the colours would be equally distributed between male and female offspring.

Therefore, the absence of an orange male fish as a result of the cross between I and II would confirm that the inheritance of colour in the fish is sex linked.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Exposure to arsenic in drinking water has been associated with the onset of many diseases. An epidemiological study involving 58 406 young adults was conducted over an 11-year period to investigate young-adult mortality due to chronic exposure to arsenic in local drinking water. Age, sex, education and socioeconomic status were taken into account during the analysis.

Identify TWO features of the method used that contributed to the validity of this study.
diagramCaption: Graphs showing survival rates for males and females over 11 years at different arsenic exposure levels.
sampleAnswer: Factors which contribute to the validity of the study could include: large sample size (58 406 individuals), and controlling for variables such as age, sex, education and socioeconomic status.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The hypothesis put forward was that exposure to arsenic in drinking water increases mortality in young adults.

Discuss the data presented in the graphs in relation to this hypothesis.
sampleAnswer: Survival is highest in those exposed to less than 90 µg L⁻¹ arsenic in both males and females. This group serves as a control showing that most young people in the study survived over the 11-year period. In both males and females, increasing doses of arsenic led to decreased survival, which suggests that arsenic is causing the decline in survival. The increasing response to increasing doses was best seen in the males. In females, all doses over 90 µg L⁻¹ led to a similar survival decrease which suggests there may be other factors that interact with the dose of arsenic. Survival declined progressively over the 11 years, which supports the idea that as arsenic exposure increases over the years, survival declines. However, although the numbers in the study were large, survival only dropped by 0.1% or less.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A student drew a diagram to model part of the process of meiosis.

The diagram shows: Homologous chromosomes paired → Crossing over → Recombinant chromatids / Non-recombinant chromatids (with Maternal and Paternal KEY)

Explain the misunderstanding of meiosis shown in this model.
diagramCaption: Student diagram modelling part of meiosis with an error in the representation of homologous chromosomes and crossing over.
sampleAnswer: The paired homologous chromosomes are incorrectly drawn. In a pair of chromosomes, one is paternal and the other is maternal. Prior to crossing over, each chromosome duplicates itself forming two chromatids and they should be identical, that is both chromatids should be either maternal or paternal and not different as shown in the model.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the effect of meiosis on genetic variation.
sampleAnswer: In meiosis, homologous chromosomes are lined up in Metaphase I in random order and orientation (independently assorted). They separate in Meiosis I, resulting in different combinations of parental chromosomes in the gametes. Crossing over is the exchange of genetic material between the chromatids of homologous chromosomes during Meiosis I. This leads to a new combination of alleles on each chromatid.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how TWO processes that affect the gene pool of populations can lead to evolution.
sampleAnswer:
A gene pool is the total genetic diversity of a population – it results in variation of phenotypes and provides the basis for natural selection. When the gene pool of a population changes evolution has occurred.

Gene pools may change as a result of mutation, gene flow and genetic drift. Gene flow is the movement of alleles into or out of a population. For example a migrant animal may add new alleles when it reproduces with individuals in the population. Genetic drift is a change in allele frequency as a result of random selection of alleles. This is especially marked in a small, remnant population. The few remaining individuals that survive carry a small sample of the alleles in the original population.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline how genetic technologies have been used to manage both infectious and non-infectious diseases. In your answer, refer to specific examples.
sampleAnswer:
Non-infectious diseases such as diabetes and cystic fibrosis are not caused by pathogens. In type 1 diabetes the pancreas no longer produces insulin. Recombinant DNA technology has produced bacteria that have a human insulin gene inserted and then produce insulin. The insulin can then be used to treat diabetic patients and keep them alive.

Replacing faulty genes in inherited diseases such as cystic fibrosis would be able to cure such conditions. It is possible to deliver a replacement gene to the lung cells via recombinant viruses. However, when lung cells are replaced the new cells (formed from stem cells) do not have the healthy version of the gene.

Infectious diseases are caused by pathogens. Crops such as corn are affected by the European corn borer. Recombinant Bt corn is produced by taking a gene from Bacillus thuringiensis. The gene codes for a protein that is toxic to the European corn borer, thus reducing disease in corn crops.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graphs show data related to the control of blood glucose in healthy humans.

Figure 1: Plasma glucose levels before and after a meal.
Figure 2: Plasma insulin levels.
Figure 3: Plasma glucagon levels.

Explain all the graphs with respect to the negative feedback control of blood glucose in healthy humans.
diagramCaption: Three graphs showing plasma glucose, insulin, and glucagon levels over time before and after a meal.
sampleAnswer:
The plasma levels measured in the first 60 minutes represent resting levels. After the meal, plasma glucose rises as a direct result of absorption of glucose from the gut into the bloodstream.

Rising blood glucose stimulates β cells in the pancreas to release the hormone insulin, which stimulates body cells to take up glucose to be used in their metabolism and the liver to take up glucose to be stored as glycogen. Therefore, the rise in insulin in Figure 2 is a direct result of the rise in plasma glucose. As the cells and liver take up glucose, they remove the glucose from the blood leading to the subsequent fall in plasma glucose levels. The falling glucose removes the stimulation of β cells and by this negative feedback mechanism, insulin levels also fall.

Glucagon is a hormone that is an important part of the negative feedback loop that controls glucose levels in the blood. If plasma glucose falls to low levels, glucagon is released from alpha cells in the pancreas and causes glucose to be released into the blood from glycogen stores in the liver and muscles to restore normal glucose levels. In Figure 1, as glucose rises the alpha cells will produce less glucagon and falling glucagon levels can be seen in Figure 3.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline differences between the control of blood glucose and the control of body temperature.
sampleAnswer: Temperature changes are detected by the hypothalamus in the brain but changes in glucose are detected by the pancreas. The response to changes in temperature is via the nervous system but glucose is regulated via hormones.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Rabies is caused by a virus that can infect many types of animals including humans. It usually takes weeks for the virus to travel via the nervous system from the site of the infection to the brain.

Identify features of the rabies virus that facilitate its transmission between hosts.
sampleAnswer: The virus is able to travel via the nervous system to the salivary glands. This can result in direct contact transmission when the infected host bites another animal.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain a feature that distinguishes the rabies virus from cellular pathogens.
sampleAnswer: The rabies virus has a small genome composed of single-stranded RNA but the genome of cellular pathogens such as bacteria is much larger and is in the form of DNA which enables complex cellular processes without a host.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows a simplified representation of the structure of the rabies virus, including viral RNA polymerase (L and P proteins).

Explain the role of RNA polymerase in the reproduction of the rabies virus.
diagramCaption: Diagram showing the simplified structure of the rabies virus with labelled components including RNA polymerase.
sampleAnswer:
The viral RNA polymerase, which is made up of the L and P proteins, is responsible for the production of viral proteins and viral RNA, which are the components of new rabies viral particles.

RNA polymerase is responsible for transcription of the viral RNA into complementary mRNA strands, which are then translated into rabies viral proteins, G, M, N, P and L, by host cell ribosomes.

The replication of rabies viral RNA is facilitated by the newly produced L and P proteins (RNA polymerase). In this process, a complementary strand of viral RNA is produced from the original viral RNA strand. This complementary strand is then used as a template for the RNA polymerase to catalyse the production of more viral RNA. The new strands produced are therefore the same as the original viral RNA.

RNA polymerase is responsible for both the viral RNA and proteins needed in reproducing the virus.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) is used to treat people who may have been infected with the rabies virus. It involves the injection of human rabies immunoglobulin (HRIG) and a course of rabies vaccine.

The graphs show:
- Viral concentration with and without PEP treatment.
- Antibody concentration with PEP treatment over time.

Using the data and your knowledge, explain how PEP prevents the disease rabies from developing after exposure to the virus.
diagramCaption: Graphs showing viral concentration over time with and without PEP, and antibody concentration with PEP treatment.
sampleAnswer:
Once the rabies virus has entered the wound it will use the patient's cells to replicate and the viral concentration increases (as seen in the first five days). Without PEP the virus will continue to replicate, migrate to the CNS and eventually cause rabies and death.

Initially the patient does not have memory cells for the virus and does not produce antibodies to it. The injection of HRIG provides the patient with human antibodies that are specific to the virus, providing passive immunity. The antibodies will combine with viral molecules and inactivate them and also facilitate their destruction by white cells such as phagocytes. The PEP graph shows these antibodies will only last for up to 21 days but have an important initial role in controlling viral numbers.

The rabies vaccine contains an inactivated, harmless version of the rabies virus and will result in the development of acquired, active immunity. This occurs when macrophages present the viral antigen to specific helper T cells. These helper T cells then stimulate specific B cells to undergo mitosis and cell differentiation. The daughter cells will either be plasma cells that produce antibodies or will be kept as memory B cells.

The PEP graph shows that by day 7 the production of antibodies begins and increases sharply over the next few days. This coincides with the rapid decrease in viral concentration. By day 11 there are no viral particles left at the infection site and progression to the CNS and the disease has been avoided.

D. Environmental exposure

B. Insulin is secreted to convert glycogen into glucose.

diagramCaption: A diagram showing two chromosomes before and after a cell division process, with chromosomes being replicated.

questionText:
In females, one X chromosome in each cell is inactivated.

What is the main consequence of this process?
A. All female cells have the same X chromosome active.
B. Females cannot be carriers of sex-linked conditions.
C. Females and males have a similar level of X-linked gene expression.
D. Females always express recessive X-linked alleles.

questionText:

What effect is this most likely to have on their hearing?
A. Damage to hair cells in the cochlea

D. Blockage of the auditory nerve

questionText:

A. Antigenic variation

questionText:
A mutation occurs in the promoter region of a gene.

This mutation is most likely to affect the
A. amino acid sequence of the protein.

D. replication of DNA.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Which of the following correctly identifies the type of microorganism and a disease it causes?
A. Virus – Tinea

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows a stage of mitosis.

Which of the following correctly identifies this stage and what is happening?
A. Prophase – chromosomes are condensing
B. Metaphase – chromosomes are lined up along the cell equator

D. Telophase – nuclear membranes are reforming
diagramCaption: A diagram showing a stage of mitosis with chromosomes visible.

Two pink flowers are crossed.

A. 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white
B. 3 red : 1 white
C. All pink
D. 1 red : 2 pink : 1 white

questionText:

A. Blood test

D. Ultrasound

questionText:
Epidemiological studies show that the incidence of melanoma is higher in Australia than in countries closer to the equator.

Which is the most likely explanation?
A. Australians have less melanin in their skin.
B. The ozone layer is thinner over Australia.
C. Australia has a higher population density.
D. Australians eat a different diet.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram represents a generalised pathogen.

Label TWO features on the diagram.
diagramCaption: A diagram of a generalised pathogen cell.
sampleAnswer: Answers could include: Plasma membrane, Absence of membrane-bound organelles, DNA.

subject: Biology
questionText: Complete the flowchart to show the Koch's postulates procedure.
diagramCaption: A flowchart with blank boxes to be filled in showing Koch's postulates.
sampleAnswer: 1. The microorganism must be found in the diseased organism. 2. The microorganism must be isolated and grown in pure culture. 3. The cultured microorganism must cause disease when introduced into a healthy host. 4. The microorganism must be re-isolated from the new host and shown to be the same.

subject: Biology
questionText: Using a named example, outline the benefits and limitations of TWO strategies used to prevent and control infectious disease.
sampleAnswer: Vaccination: Benefits include providing long-term immunity and establishing herd immunity. Limitations include that some individuals cannot be vaccinated and vaccines may not be available for all diseases. Quarantine: Benefits include preventing spread during outbreaks. Limitations include economic impact and difficulty in enforcement.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A student investigated the proportion of students who could taste PTC paper in two Year 12 classes.

Class A: 24 out of 30 could taste PTC. Class B: 15 out of 30 could taste PTC.

The allele for tasting PTC (T) is dominant.

Using the Hardy-Weinberg equation, calculate the allele frequencies for each class.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how meiosis produces genetically unique gametes.
sampleAnswer: Meiosis produces genetically unique gametes through: 1) Crossing over during prophase I, where homologous chromosomes exchange genetic material, creating new allele combinations. 2) Independent assortment during metaphase I, where homologous pairs align randomly, giving 2^n possible combinations. 3) Random fertilisation adds further variation as any sperm can fertilise any egg.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The pedigree shows the inheritance of a genetic condition.

Determine the pattern of inheritance. Justify your answer using the pedigree.
diagramCaption: A pedigree chart showing inheritance of a genetic condition across generations.
sampleAnswer: The pattern is autosomal recessive inheritance. Justification: Two unaffected parents have affected offspring, indicating both parents are carriers. The condition affects both males and females, ruling out sex-linked inheritance.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how a point mutation can lead to a change in the structure and function of a protein.
sampleAnswer: A point mutation changes a single base pair in the DNA sequence. During transcription, this altered base is copied into the mRNA. During translation, the changed codon may code for a different amino acid (missense mutation). The altered amino acid may affect the folding of the polypeptide chain, changing the 3D structure of the protein. This can alter the active site of an enzyme or the binding properties of a receptor, reducing or eliminating the protein's function.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows a cross-section of the eye.

Explain how the structures of the eye work together to focus light on the retina for a near object.
diagramCaption: A cross-sectional diagram of the human eye showing major structures.
sampleAnswer: Light enters through the cornea, which provides most of the refraction. The iris controls the size of the pupil to regulate light entry. For near objects, the ciliary muscles contract, making the suspensory ligaments slack. This allows the lens to become thicker/more convex, increasing its refractive power. The light is focused precisely on the retina at the fovea.

subject: Biology
questionText: Identify a named disorder of the eye.
sampleAnswer: Myopia (short-sightedness) – the eyeball is too long or the lens is too curved, causing light from distant objects to focus in front of the retina rather than on it, resulting in blurred distant vision.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe a technology used to correct or assist with the disorder identified in part (b).
sampleAnswer: Concave (diverging) lenses in glasses or contact lenses can correct myopia. The concave lens diverges light rays before they enter the eye, compensating for the excessive convergence by the cornea and lens, so that the image is focused on the retina. LASIK surgery can also reshape the cornea to correct the refractive error permanently.

subject: Biology
questionText: Compare the processes of mitosis and meiosis in terms of genetic outcomes.
sampleAnswer: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells (diploid), while meiosis produces four genetically unique daughter cells (haploid). Mitosis involves one division; meiosis involves two divisions. Crossing over and independent assortment occur in meiosis but not mitosis, contributing to genetic variation. Mitosis is for growth and repair; meiosis is for production of gametes.

subject: Biology
questionText:
In a species of flower, petal colour is controlled by co-dominance. Red (R) and white (W) alleles produce pink (RW) heterozygous flowers.

Predict the phenotypic ratio of offspring from a cross between two pink flowers. Show your working.
sampleAnswer:
RW × RW:
R W
R RR RW
W RW WW
Phenotypic ratio: 1 Red (RR) : 2 Pink (RW) : 1 White (WW)

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe the process of polypeptide synthesis.
sampleAnswer: Transcription: In the nucleus, RNA polymerase unwinds the DNA and reads the template strand. Complementary mRNA is synthesised using RNA nucleotides (A-U, G-C). The mRNA moves to the ribosome. Translation: At the ribosome, tRNA molecules with complementary anticodons bring specific amino acids. The ribosome moves along the mRNA, reading codons. Peptide bonds form between amino acids, creating a polypeptide chain.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how the immune system responds to a pathogen that it has previously encountered.
sampleAnswer: When the pathogen is encountered again, memory B cells and memory T cells recognise the specific antigen. Memory B cells rapidly differentiate into plasma cells, producing large quantities of specific antibodies much faster than the primary response. Memory T cells quickly activate into cytotoxic T cells that destroy infected cells. The secondary immune response is faster, stronger, and longer-lasting than the primary response, often preventing symptoms from developing.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows changes in body temperature of a person over 24 hours in two different environmental conditions.

Using the data, explain how homeostatic mechanisms maintain body temperature.
diagramCaption: A graph showing body temperature measurements over 24 hours in different environmental conditions.
sampleAnswer: The body maintains a relatively constant core temperature despite environmental changes through negative feedback mechanisms. When environmental temperature increases, thermoreceptors detect the change and signal the hypothalamus. The hypothalamus triggers responses including vasodilation (increasing blood flow to the skin surface to lose heat), sweating (evaporative cooling), and behavioural changes. When temperature decreases, vasoconstriction reduces heat loss, shivering generates heat through muscle contraction, and metabolic rate may increase.

subject: Biology
questionText: Evaluate the strategies used to prevent and control a named infectious disease in a population.
sampleAnswer: Influenza prevention and control strategies include: 1) Vaccination - annual flu vaccines are effective but must be updated due to antigenic drift/shift. Provides individual protection and herd immunity but effectiveness varies (40-60%). 2) Hygiene measures - hand washing, covering coughs reduce transmission but depend on individual compliance. 3) Quarantine and isolation of infected individuals limits spread but has economic and social costs. 4) Antiviral medications can reduce severity and duration but are not a cure. Overall, a combination of strategies is most effective, with vaccination being the primary prevention method.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The data shows the number of new cases and deaths for a non-infectious disease over a 10-year period.

Using the data, discuss the trends in the epidemiology of this disease.
diagramCaption: A data table showing new cases and deaths for a non-infectious disease over 10 years.
sampleAnswer: The data shows an increasing trend in new cases (incidence) over the 10-year period, suggesting the disease is becoming more common. The death rate shows a different pattern - while total deaths increased, the mortality rate (deaths per case) decreased, suggesting improvements in treatment. The prevalence is increasing because people are surviving longer with the disease. These trends may be influenced by changes in risk factors, improved diagnosis and screening, and an aging population.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The body temperature of a lizard and a mouse were measured at different environmental temperatures.

Using the data, compare the thermoregulation strategies of these two organisms.
diagramCaption: A table or graph comparing body temperatures of a lizard and mouse at different environmental temperatures.
sampleAnswer: The lizard is an ectotherm - its body temperature varies with the environmental temperature. It relies on behavioural adaptations (basking in sun, seeking shade) to regulate temperature. The mouse is an endotherm - it maintains a relatively constant body temperature regardless of the environment. It uses physiological mechanisms (shivering, sweating, vasoconstriction/vasodilation) and metabolic heat production. The mouse has a higher metabolic rate and energy requirement compared to the lizard.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe the process of PCR (polymerase chain reaction).
sampleAnswer: PCR amplifies specific DNA sequences through repeated cycles of: 1) Denaturation - heating to ~95°C separates the double-stranded DNA. 2) Annealing - cooling to ~55°C allows primers to bind to complementary sequences flanking the target region. 3) Extension - heating to ~72°C allows Taq polymerase to synthesise new DNA strands from the primers. Each cycle doubles the amount of target DNA, producing millions of copies.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments.
sampleAnswer: DNA samples are loaded into wells in an agarose gel. An electric current is applied - DNA fragments migrate toward the positive electrode because DNA is negatively charged (due to phosphate groups). Smaller fragments move through the gel pores faster than larger fragments. After a set time, the fragments are separated by size, with smaller fragments travelling further. The fragments can be visualised using UV light with a fluorescent dye.

subject: Biology
questionText: Using a named example, evaluate how biotechnology techniques are used to maintain biodiversity.
sampleAnswer: Transgenic salmon carry a growth hormone gene that allows faster growth. Physical isolation (land-based tanks) prevents escape to the wild. Reproductive techniques produce all-female, triploid (infertile) fish to prevent breeding with wild populations. Benefits: Increased food production, reduced fishing pressure on wild stocks, economic benefits. Risks: Potential escape and competition with wild salmon, reduced genetic diversity in aquaculture. Gene flow from GM fish could alter wild gene pools. The combination of physical and reproductive containment strategies is crucial for protecting biodiversity. Overall, the technology provides benefits but requires strict biosecurity measures.

questionText:

Which of the following might occur in this person?
A. Shivering
B. Vasodilation
C. Goosebumps
D. Pale appearance

A. Behavioural
B. Environmental

questionText:

A. Antibody

D. Antiseptic

questionText:
The diagram shows the response to an injury in a human.

What is the response shown?
A. Infection

diagramCaption: A diagram showing the inflammatory response to an injury with labels for pain, warmth, redness, and swelling.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Data for incidence of notifiable infectious diseases in Australia are shown in the table.

Disease | Percent of all cases
Influenza | 53
Sexually transmitted infections | 25
Gastrointestinal diseases | 10
Vaccine preventable diseases excluding influenza | 9
Other | 3

Which type of graph should be chosen to best represent these data?
A. A column graph because percentages are continuous.
B. A column graph because diseases are categorical.
C. A line graph because percentages are continuous.
D. A line graph because diseases are categorical.

subject: Biology

Both colours are dominant due to co-dominance. Dark purple = PP, White = WW, Violet = PW.

P W
P PP PW
W PW WW

Phenotypic ratio: Dark purple : Violet : White = 1 : 2 : 1

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline the process of artificial pollination.
sampleAnswer: This process involves the transfer of pollen from the anther of one target plant to the stigma of another targeted plant.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain a possible outcome of the use of artificial pollination on subsequent populations.
sampleAnswer: If the pollen from one plant is used to artificially pollinate a large number of plants, this will lead to a large number of offspring that are similar. Over time, if this is repeated in subsequent generations this will reduce the genetic diversity of the population.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Birth defects in humans can be caused by chromosomal abnormalities.

Draw a suitable graph of the data provided in the table. Include a suitable line of best fit.

Maternal age (years) | Prevalence (per 1000 births)
20 | 1.5
30 | 3
35 | 8
40 | 22
45 | 38
diagramCaption: A table of data showing maternal age vs prevalence of chromosomal abnormalities.
sampleAnswer: A graph with maternal age on x-axis and prevalence on y-axis, with points correctly plotted and an exponential/curved line of best fit showing rapid increase after age 35.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline the trend shown in the data.
sampleAnswer: As maternal age increases, the prevalence of chromosomal abnormalities increases. After the age of 30, there is a rapid increase in prevalence – it is increasing at an increasing rate.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the cause of a type of chromosomal mutation.
sampleAnswer: One type of chromosomal mutation is a numerical abnormality. This is where there are more or fewer chromosomes than the normal diploid cell. These abnormalities can be caused during meiosis due to non-disjunction. Non-disjunction means that a pair of homologous chromosomes fails to separate/segregate during meiosis so that some of the gametes have one chromosome too many and some have one too few.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Offspring are rarely identical to their parents.

Compare the ways in which genetic variation can arise from asexual and sexual reproduction.
sampleAnswer: Mutation during DNA replication is a source of genetic variation that can arise in offspring of both asexual and sexual reproduction. Asexual reproduction is achieved by cells of one organism dividing by mitosis, producing offspring with the same genetic code as the parent. The most likely cause of genetic variation would be mutations during mitosis. Sexual reproduction leads to much more genetic variation due to meiosis: random assignment of chromosomes (independent assortment), crossing over of genetic material between homologous pairs, and random fertilisation. Each gamete has a unique set of alleles, leading to infinite variation in offspring.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Jelly Bush honey has been used by Aboriginal Peoples to treat cuts, sores and burns. Recent studies have shown that Jelly Bush honey has a very high level of methylglyoxal which is known to help fight infection.

Design a safe procedure that the scientist could use in a laboratory to investigate the effectiveness of Jelly Bush honey as a pharmaceutical to inhibit bacterial growth.
sampleAnswer: 1. Select appropriate safety equipment including gloves and safety clothing. 2. Prepare three nutrient agar plates using sterile techniques. Seal one plate and label as control. 3. Inoculate the remaining plates with bacteria to cover the agar surface. 4. Place a sterile paper disc soaked in distilled water in the middle of an agar plate. Seal and label as Water. 5. Place a sterile paper disc soaked in Jelly Bush honey in the middle of an agar plate. Seal and label as Honey. 6. Incubate the plates at 25°C for 48 hours. 7. Remove from incubator but do not open any of the plates. 8. Observe the control plate to check for contaminating bacteria. 9. For each agar plate, measure the zone of inhibition. 10. Repeat the experiment three more times and calculate an average.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The incidence of cervical cancer in the Australian population is shown in the graph from 1985-2015. Public health campaigns commenced during this period to reduce the incidence of cervical cancer. This included a national screening program to detect pre-cancerous cells and a vaccination program against human papillomavirus (HPV) which causes most cervical cancers.

Evaluate the success of these campaigns in reducing the incidence of cervical cancer in Australian women. Include reference to the data in your answer.
diagramCaption: A line graph showing incidence of cervical cancer per 100,000 women from 1985-2015, with markers for the 1991 screening program and 2007/2013 HPV vaccine programs.
sampleAnswer: The national screening program that started in 1991 is followed by a large drop in the incidence of the disease, to about half, and therefore appears to have been effective. However, incidence was dropping before 1991 and this decrease may have continued without the program. Because HPV causes most cervical cancers, vaccinating young girls and boys could be effective. However, the data do not show a reduction since the vaccination programs began. This could be because the vaccines are delivered to young school-aged students and cervical cancer develops in older women over several years. Although it is expected that incidence will reduce as more of the population is vaccinated, the effectiveness of each campaign separately cannot be assessed from these data.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Soon after the discovery of the structure of DNA, scientists began investigating how DNA replicated. Three models were proposed.

Identify which of the models above is currently accepted.
diagramCaption: Three diagrams showing proposed models of DNA replication: Model 1 (conservative), Model 2 (semi-conservative), Model 3 (dispersive).

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe the process of DNA replication.
sampleAnswer: An enzyme unzips the DNA, creating a replication fork. On each strand, an enzyme attaches to the original DNA nucleotides and uses them as a template. It reads the bases and adds complementary nucleotides. Another enzyme glues the nucleotides together, forming a new double-stranded section of DNA.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline the ways in which the DNA of prokaryotes and eukaryotes differ.
sampleAnswer: Prokaryotic DNA is a circular molecule found in the cytoplasm, carrying a small number of genes and not tightly coiled around histone proteins. In contrast, eukaryotic DNA is a linear molecule found in the nucleus carrying a large number of genes and tightly coiled around histone and other proteins.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Bt cotton has been genetically engineered to produce an insecticide that kills cotton bollworm. It was introduced to a cotton-producing nation in 2002.

Explain ONE reason why cotton yield changed between 2002 and 2013.
diagramCaption: Graphs showing trends in cotton yield, % Bt cotton grown, and insecticide use from 2002-2013.
sampleAnswer: As the percentage of Bt cotton grown increased, so did the cotton yield. Bt cotton reduces insect attack, increasing yield.

subject: Biology
questionText: To what extent do the data support the use of Bt cotton as a method of disease control in cotton?
sampleAnswer: Initially Bt cotton resulted in less insect damage and reduced insecticide use. As Bt cotton was introduced, insecticide for bollworm decreased to zero, suggesting effective control. However, once most cotton was Bt, insecticide for hemiptera increased markedly. Bt toxin killed bollworms but not hemiptera. Removal of bollworms provided a survival advantage for hemiptera. Therefore, Bt cotton only has short-term benefits for disease control and needs to be complemented by use of pesticides.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Malaria is a disease transmitted by a mosquito vector. There has been no effective vaccine developed.

Refer to the maps below to answer Question 30.

Discuss possible reasons for the differences in the distribution of malaria and its vector. Include detailed reference to Map 1 and Map 2.
diagramCaption: Two world maps: Map 1 showing global distribution of malaria-transmitting mosquitoes, Map 2 showing global distribution of malaria cases.
sampleAnswer: A pathogen relying on a mosquito vector will occur mainly where the vector occurs. However, some areas in Map 2 show malaria where the mosquito is not found (e.g., northern Africa) - possibly due to human travel maintaining the disease. Some countries have the mosquito but no disease (e.g., Europe) - public health measures may have eliminated malaria despite the vector. Strategies like pesticides reduce mosquito populations, bed nets prevent bites, and pharmaceuticals target the parasite. Wealthier nations like the USA can use widespread pharmaceuticals. The distribution reflects both ecological factors and socioeconomic capacity for disease control.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Studies have shown that lung cancer can be linked to environmental causes. In one historical study across 29 health districts in Japan, non-smoking married women aged 40 and above were followed up for 14 years and annual mortality rates for lung cancer were assessed according to the smoking habits of their husbands.

Evaluate the method used in this epidemiological study.
diagramCaption: A table showing lung cancer mortality rates for non-smoker women with non-smoker husbands (8.7), non-smoker women with smoker husbands (15.5), and women who smoke (32.8) per 100,000.
sampleAnswer: The study used large numbers of women matched for exposure in three categories and followed them for 14 years. The numbers and time period are adequate for validity. However, the categories assume similar periods of time with a smoker and similar smoking amounts. In reality, exposure varies greatly. The large cohort should average out this effect, so overall the method appears valid.

subject: Biology
questionText: Justify conclusions that could be drawn from the results of the study.
sampleAnswer: Exposure to smoke either passively or actively increases the risk of dying from lung cancer, as seen by nearly double the number of deaths between non-smoker women with non-smoker husbands (8.7/100,000) compared to women with smoker husbands (15.5/100,000) or smoking women (32.8/100,000). Lung cancer can be caused by factors other than cigarette smoke as shown by the mortality rate of unexposed women being 8.7/100,000.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Lung cancer can be linked to genetic causes. The EGFR gene codes for EGFR protein, which is composed of one polypeptide chain.

Construct a flow chart to outline the synthesis of the EGFR protein from the EGFR gene.
sampleAnswer: EGFR gene (DNA in nucleus) → Transcription (RNA polymerase reads template strand) → mRNA produced → mRNA exits nucleus to ribosome → Translation (tRNA brings amino acids, ribosome reads codons) → Polypeptide chain formed → EGFR protein (folded into functional 3D structure)

subject: Biology
questionText:
EGFR mutations are present in about 32% of cases of Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer.

Explain how a mutation in the EGFR gene could result in changes in protein structure and function to increase the risk of lung cancer.
sampleAnswer: A mutation of the EGFR gene is a change in its DNA base sequence/codons. If this change is in a coding region, it can result in a change to the amino acid sequence. This may alter the folding and properties of the protein and may affect its function. For example, if there is a change in the enzyme region of the protein, this may alter the active site and therefore enzyme activity, leading to changes that could alter the rate of DNA replication and cell division. Lung cancer is the result of uncontrolled cell division. Since EGFR protein is associated with controlling cell division, mutations in the gene may lead to uncontrolled cell division and therefore cancer.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Researchers have identified a gene that determines the inflammatory response of lung cells to infection with a virus. An allele of this gene is associated with increased inflammation and increased chance of death from the virus.

The table shows the percentage presence of the allele in people with different ancestries.

South Asian: 60.3%, European: 15.1%, African: 2.4%, East Asian: 1.8%

Explain how mutation, natural selection, genetic drift and gene flow could have led to these differences in the gene pools of populations with differing ancestry.
sampleAnswer: Mutation is likely the underlying cause of the allele. Natural selection could reduce the frequency if the virus caused severe illness, as seen in Africans and East Asians with low frequencies. If mixing was rare, limited gene flow would maintain different frequencies. Genetic drift in small populations could change allele frequencies through chance events. In large geographic areas, chance effects would be averaged out, but in isolated populations, drift could significantly alter frequencies.

subject: Biology
questionText: Identify the components of a nucleotide.
sampleAnswer: Sugar, phosphate and base (adenine, thymine, cytosine or guanine).

subject: Biology
questionText:
A section of a leading strand of DNA has a sequence of CAGT.

Complete the diagram by labelling the complementary mRNA strand which is formed during the transcription of DNA.
sampleAnswer: The complementary mRNA strand would be: GUCA (replacing T with U, and using complementary bases: C→G, A→U, G→C, T→A).

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe how phagocytes help protect against pathogens.
sampleAnswer: They engulf or enclose a pathogen/antigen as they can identify non-self substances. Once the pathogen is engulfed, the phagocyte breaks down the pathogen or antigen.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how antibodies are produced in response to the entry of a pathogen.
sampleAnswer: Pathogens carry protein markers (antigens), so that when it enters a body, it is recognised as non-self. The immune response is then activated to destroy the pathogen. The antigen of the pathogen binds to the receptor of a B cell and activates it. This specific B cell replicates to form plasma cells. Plasma cells produce an antibody that is specific to the antigen.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following graph outlines some hormonal changes during pregnancy.

Complete the table for TWO of the hormones graphed.
diagramCaption: A graph showing relative levels of hCG, Progesterone, Relaxin, and Oestrogen over the 40 weeks of pregnancy.
sampleAnswer: hCG: Function - thickens the lining of the placenta and stops menstruation; Peak - First trimester/10 weeks. Progesterone: Function - maintains uterus lining, helps the uterus grow and triggers hormones responsible for developing the baby's organs; Peak - Third trimester/38 weeks.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how problems with the structure and function of the eye can cause a named visual disorder.
sampleAnswer: The lens of the eye refracts light from the environment onto the retina. Cataracts are a visual disorder caused by the clouding of the lens which is normally clear. Clouding stops light passing through the lens leading to blurry vision.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe ONE technology that is used to assist with the effects of a named visual disorder.
sampleAnswer: LASIK laser surgery is used to correct myopia, hyperopia and astigmatism caused by refraction errors in the eye which can be caused by problems with the shape of the cornea. In LASIK surgery a thin flap is opened on the surface of the cornea, a laser then reshapes the cornea to provide the correct refraction, and the flap is laid back into place.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Huntington's disease is an autosomal dominant genetic disease.

Using the pedigree, justify the genotype of individual 'H'. In your answer, refer to the letters on the pedigree to identify individuals.
diagramCaption: A pedigree chart showing three generations of a family affected by Huntington's disease.
sampleAnswer: Individual 'H' must be heterozygous for the Huntington's disease gene (genotype Hh). This is because she has children that do not have Huntington's disease (J and L) and children that do have Huntington's disease (I and K). Therefore she must carry both the allele for Huntington's disease (H) and the normal allele (h).

subject: Biology
questionText:
Huntington's disease is caused by a misfolded protein 'Huntingtin'. It is caused by excess repeats of the DNA sequence CAG on the coding strand of DNA.

Use the codon chart to identify the amino acid that is repeated.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The normal Huntingtin protein has 10-26 repeats of CAG. In Huntington's disease there are 37-80 repeats.

Explain the relationship between the number of CAG repeats and the age of onset of Huntington's disease.
diagramCaption: A scatter plot showing the relationship between number of CAG repeats (x-axis) and age of onset of Huntington's disease (y-axis), showing a negative correlation.
sampleAnswer: As the number of repeats increases, the age of onset of Huntington's disease decreases. For example, when there are 60 CAG repeats the age of onset is 20 years. CAG repeats lead to the alteration of the protein responsible for the disease at an earlier age.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Diagram 1 shows a pedigree of a family affected by Huntington's disease. Diagram 2 shows the results of gel electrophoresis on fragments of DNA from chromosome four.

Predict whether individuals S and U will be affected by Huntington's disease, and if so, at what age. Use data from the diagrams to justify your answer.
diagramCaption: A pedigree (Diagram 1) and gel electrophoresis results (Diagram 2) showing CAG repeat numbers for family members.
sampleAnswer: Individual S is not predicted to be affected by Huntington's disease. The gel electrophoresis shows a band at approximately 15 repeats, the same as his father (P) who does not have the disease. Individual U will most likely get Huntington's disease at around age 45. Individual U has the same number of repeats as her mother (Q) around 38 repeats.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Malaria is a potentially fatal infectious disease that is spread to humans by infected mosquitoes.

Identify the dependent variable and a controlled variable in this investigation.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing four containers (A-D) with infected/uninfected mosquitoes and worn/clean clothing.
sampleAnswer: Dependent variable: the number of mosquitoes that land on the clothing. Controlled variable: any reasonable controlled variable e.g., size of container, number of mosquitoes in each container.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The results of the investigation showing the number of times mosquitoes landed on the clothing in an hour are provided.

Justify a suitable conclusion for this investigation.
sampleAnswer: Wearing clean clothing would reduce the transmission of malaria as mosquitoes land on clean clothing on average fewer times than on worn clothing. Infected mosquitoes landed on clothing three times as often as uninfected mosquitoes. Infected mosquitoes can only pass on infection if they land on a host and bite them.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Air pollution has been linked to a variety of non-infectious neurological (brain) disorders.

500 people from each of three major cities were surveyed and monitored for 12 months.

Evaluate the method used in this epidemiological study in determining a link between air pollution and the symptoms.
diagramCaption: A table showing percentage of males and females with symptoms in three cities.
sampleAnswer: The study was not fully valid and a statistically significant relationship between cause and effect is not shown. Risk factors including age, sex, ethnic group, occupation are not all controlled. The time period of 12 months may not be long enough for symptoms to develop. No indication of the locality within each city or proximity to industry. No indication of the degree of symptoms. The study must be done on a very large population with varying socioeconomic status for trends to be reliable.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe a feature that distinguishes a viral from a bacterial pathogen.
sampleAnswer: The size of a bacterium is 1-10μm, a virus is 0.05-0.1μm so a bacterium is much larger than a virus. Bacteria are prokaryotic cellular organisms while viruses are comprised of nucleic acids and a protein coat and are not cellular.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A waterborne disease outbreak occurred after a flood.

Outline an experimental procedure that could be used to determine if the pathogen is viral or bacterial.
sampleAnswer: The presence of a bacterial pathogen could be identified by using an agar culture. A viral pathogen cannot be cultured on agar. A bacterial pathogen can be observed using a high-powered light microscope, but a viral pathogen would not be able to be observed as it is too small.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Organisms use various mechanisms to maintain their internal environment within tolerance limits.

Outline a physiological adaptation in endotherms which assists in maintaining their internal environment.
sampleAnswer: Endotherms can undergo vasoconstriction to decrease blood flow and volume. This reduces heat loss through the skin.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain TWO adaptations in plants that help to maintain water balance.
sampleAnswer: 1. Sunken stomata: Stomata may be sunken in pits in the epidermis. Moist air is trapped over the top of the stomate. The saturated air above the stomata reduces the evaporation rate. 2. Thick waxy cuticle: A thick waxy cuticle makes the leaf watertight, acting as a barrier to evaporation. The shiny surface also reflects heat, lowering leaf temperature.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Tetanus vaccines were introduced in 1953 resulting in reduced case numbers. The majority of recorded cases occurred in people aged 65 and over.

Assess the use of vaccinations and the vaccination schedule. Use the data provided to support your answer.
diagramCaption: A graph showing the recommended vaccination schedule for tetanus with antibody levels over time for 5 doses.
sampleAnswer: Vaccinations are used to initiate an immune response using a form of the pathogen/antigen that will not result in infection. From the graph, the more booster shots, the longer the period of time the person is considered immune. Cases occurring in people aged over 65 would be the result of not having a booster for a long time. The reduction in numbers of tetanus cases would suggest that vaccinations are effective while the recurring schedule ensures a person maintains immunity.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe a named genetic technology and its use in a medical application.
sampleAnswer: Human insulin is produced by recombinant DNA technology to help diabetics. Restriction enzymes are used to cut the insulin gene from a human cell. The same restriction enzyme is used to cut a section from a plasmid of E.coli to ensure the sticky ends are complementary. The plasmid is then resealed with the insulin gene inserted. The recombined plasmid is then inserted into a host to produce human insulin. The insulin is then used by patients to manage diabetes.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The mountain pygmy possum (Burramys parvus) is restricted to four regions in Australia's alpine zone. The species is listed as critically endangered with less than 2000 adults remaining.

Evaluate how bushfires and the introduction of males from other locations have affected the population size and gene pool of the Mt Buller pygmy possum population.
diagramCaption: A map showing four pygmy possum locations and a graph showing Mt Buller population size from 1996-2016 with markers for bushfires and male introductions.
sampleAnswer: Bushfires in 1998, 2000 and 2002 reduced the population size and restricted the alleles present in the gene pool. This may have led to genetic drift (bottleneck effect). In 2007 and 2012, 6 males from Mt Bogong were introduced, producing gene flow that increased genetic diversity. The number of suitable adaptations increased, leading to population growth from about 8 individuals in 2007 to 150 in 2016. The improved diversity has enabled the population to better adapt to changing conditions.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Mechanisms of reproduction for both humans and generalised fungi are shown in the diagrams.

Describe the similarities and differences of reproduction in humans and generalised fungi.
diagramCaption: Two diagrams showing the reproductive cycles of humans and generalised fungi, including meiosis, fertilisation, and mitosis stages.
sampleAnswer: Similarities: Both use meiosis (to produce gametes in humans and spores in fungi). Both involve the fusion of genetic material to produce a zygote. Differences: In humans all reproduction is sexual; fungi have both sexual and asexual reproduction. In humans, fertilisation produces a diploid zygote directly. In fungi, after mycelium fuse there is a stage where two nuclei are present in the cytoplasm before fusion.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Cattle have been domesticated by humans for approximately 10,000 years. Many biotechnologies have been employed in the farming of cattle.

With reference to the table, evaluate the effect of biotechnologies on the biodiversity of cattle.
diagramCaption: A table showing examples of biotechnologies applied to cattle: selective breeding, artificial insemination, whole organism cloning, hybridisation, and transgenic organisms.
sampleAnswer: Biotechnologies can increase, maintain and decrease biodiversity in cattle. Artificial insemination reduces biodiversity as one male can sire many offspring. Selective breeding reduces biodiversity as only individuals with desired characteristics are bred. Producing transgenic organisms may increase genetic diversity by introducing new genes. Whole organism cloning reduces biodiversity as clones are genetically identical. Hybridisation can increase biodiversity through new gene combinations. Overall, these biotechnologies have decreased the biodiversity in cattle.

subject: Biology
questionText:
5-Bromouracil (bU) is a synthetic chemical mutagen. It bonds with adenine in place of thymine in DNA. During replication, it then binds with guanine.

Identify the type of mutation that is caused by bU.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing how 5-Bromouracil incorporates into DNA and causes base pair changes during replication.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe the possible effects on a protein if this mutation occurred within a gene.
sampleAnswer: The mutation changes one base pair, potentially changing a codon. If the new codon codes for the same amino acid (degenerate code), there will be no change to the protein. If the new codon codes for a different amino acid, a different polypeptide chain will form. This could result in different folding and a non-functioning protein. If a stop codon is formed, the chain will be truncated. If the mutation occurs at the AUG start codon, translation will not begin.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Which of the following are non-cellular pathogens?
A. Bacteria
B. Fungi
C. Prions
D. Protozoa

subject: Biology
questionText:
Resin produced by spinifex grass has long been used by Aboriginal Peoples. Spinifex resin is currently used to produce medicinal creams.

What is this an example of?
A. Biotechnology
B. Selective breeding
C. Artificial insemination
D. Genetically modified organisms

questionText:

What type of mutation has occurred?
A. Deletion
B. Duplication
C. Inversion
D. Substitution

questionText: Which row of the table correctly identifies components of DNA?
diagramCaption: A table showing rows with checkmarks for different DNA components: Phosphate, Ribose, Deoxyribose, Uracil, Thymine.

questionText:

D. Gamete production – Protist

How many chromosomes will the egg of a female fruit fly have?
