diagramCaption: Karyotype diagrams for male and female fruit flies.

subject: Biology
questionText:
How do stomata maintain water balance in plants?
A. They close in hot weather to decrease transpiration.
B. They open in cold weather to decrease transpiration.
C. They open in hot weather to decrease evaporative cooling.
D. They close in cold weather to decrease evaporative cooling.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Trypanosomes (Trypanosoma brucei) are protozoans that cause African sleeping sickness in humans.

Which row of the table identifies the pathogen, vector and method of disease transmission to humans?
A. Pathogen=Trypanosomes, Vector=Tsetse fly, Method=Direct
B. Pathogen=Tsetse fly, Vector=Cow, Method=Direct
C. Pathogen=Trypanosomes, Vector=Tsetse fly, Method=Indirect
D. Pathogen=Tsetse fly, Vector=Cow, Method=Indirect
diagramCaption: A diagram showing the transmission cycle of Trypanosoma brucei through tsetse flies between livestock and humans.

subject: Biology
questionText:

A. P
B. Q
C. R
D. S
diagramCaption: A diagram of a chromosome section showing three genes (Body colour, Eye colour, Wing shape) with four mutation locations P, Q, R, S marked.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Francesco Redi challenged the idea that maggots arose spontaneously from rotting meat.

Which of the following is correct for this experimental set up?
A. The sealed jar improves the validity of the experiment.
B. The independent variable is whether the meat spoils or not.
C. The use of three jars improves the reliability of the experiment.
D. The dependent variable is the use of different covers for the jars.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing Redi's experiment with sealed jar, gauze-covered jar, and open jar with rotting meat.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The data shows the proportion of adults living in Australia who are obese.

Which of the following can be observed from the data?
A. The proportion of obese adults always increases with age.
B. There is a greater percentage of men who are obese than women in all age groups.
C. The proportion of women who are obese increases from 13% at 18-24 to 38% at 65-74.
D. The proportion of men who are obese increases from 18% at 18-24 to 35% at 45-54, then decreases to 23% at age 85 and over.
diagramCaption: A bar chart showing proportion of obese adults by age group and sex in Australia, 2017-18.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Robert Koch produced a set of criteria to establish whether a particular organism is the cause of a disease in an animal.

Which of the following correctly shows the order of steps?
A. 2, 3, 1, 4
B. 2, 4, 1, 3
C. 4, 2, 1, 3
D. 4, 3, 2, 1

subject: Biology
questionText:
Which of the following identifies plant responses to pathogens?
A. Increased phagocytosis and programmed cell death
B. Increased number of stomata and programmed cell death
C. Production of antihistamines and increased thickness of cell walls
D. Production of antimicrobial substances and increased thickness of cell walls

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows the quantity of DNA in one mammalian cell over time.

Which of the statements about the cell's activity is correct?
A. The cell is dividing during section O.
B. The cell is inactive for the first two hours.
C. The cell takes one hour to replicate its DNA.
D. The cell becomes diploid only during section N.
diagramCaption: A graph showing quantity of DNA in a cell over time with sections L, M, N, O, P labelled.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows the number of cases of Swine Flu from May to July 2009 in Australia.

What effective control measures could have been introduced between May and July to limit the spread?
A. May-June: Quarantine all people arriving in Australia; July: Encourage people to wear masks and wash hands
B. May-June: Issue antibiotics to overseas visitors; July: Isolate people with symptoms
C. May-June: Encourage people to wear masks and wash hands; July: Quarantine all arriving
D. May-June: Isolate people with symptoms; July: Vaccinate all arriving
diagramCaption: A graph showing Swine Flu cases in Australia from May to July 2009, split between locally acquired and cases from new arrivals.

subject: Biology
questionText:
In a person with a particular visual disorder, light from a distant object focuses in front of the retina.

How can this disorder be corrected?
A. Laser surgery to reshape the retina
B. Use of a diverging lens in front of the eye
C. Use of a converging lens in front of the eye
D. Laser surgery to make the cornea more curved

subject: Biology
questionText:
Over 12 months, the prevalence of a non-infectious disease will increase in a population if
A. the total population increases.
B. disease recovery time decreases.
C. the incidence rate of the disease decreases.
D. the survival time of individuals with the disease increases.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following diagram models a population of glowworms in an isolated cave.

Which row of the table correctly identifies the process and reason for the change in the gene pool?
A. Gene flow – Change in frequency of the b allele due to a mutation
B. Genetic drift – Change in frequency of the b allele due to random events
C. Gene flow – Introduction of more b alleles due to new members moving into the population
D. Genetic drift – Introduction of more b alleles due to this allele being more advantageous
diagramCaption: A diagram showing a population of glowworms with alleles B and b in an isolated cave.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram represents some experimental steps used in the production of large amounts of human growth hormone.

What makes this technology successful?
A. DNA ligase can cut human growth hormone genes from human cells.
B. Human growth hormone can cause E. coli to grow and mature rapidly.
C. Human plasmids containing the gene of interest can be inserted into bacteria.
D. Restriction enzymes can produce sticky ends on both bacterial and human DNA.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing experimental steps in producing human growth hormone using recombinant DNA technology.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Analyse the following four pedigrees.

Which row in the table correctly identifies the pedigree with the type of inheritance?
diagramCaption: Four pedigree charts showing different inheritance patterns.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A diagram of the different parts of a flower is shown.

Identify the structures where pollen and ovules are located.
diagramCaption: A diagram of a flower showing stigma, anther, filament, and ovary.

subject: Biology
questionText: Complete the table to compare features of sexual and asexual reproduction.
sampleAnswer:
Feature | Sexual | Asexual
Genetic variability | Yes | No
Number of parents | 2 | 1
Example organism | Humans | Bacteria

subject: Biology
questionText:
A student designed and conducted a practical investigation to test for the presence of microbes in water and food samples.

Justify a safety precaution required to prevent infection when conducting the investigation.
sampleAnswer: The wearing of gloves to prevent contact with skin and the subsequent entry of pathogens into skin/mouth. Keeping the petri dish closed and wiping down the benchtop with an antiseptic after viewing the microbes will kill any pathogens that may have escaped.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how the student could ensure the reliability of the investigation.
sampleAnswer: The student could repeat the experiment by using five plates to culture the same sample of food or water. This would mean the student could check that the results are similar in each test.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline how ONE type of electromagnetic radiation can cause a germline mutation.
sampleAnswer: X-rays are a type of electromagnetic radiation that damages the structure of DNA. If this damage occurs to the DNA in gametes (sex cells) then it is a germline mutation.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline the cause of a disease due to environmental exposure.
sampleAnswer: Lung cancer can be caused by exposure to inhalation of smoke from cigarettes.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how an educational program or campaign can be used to decrease the incidence of a disease caused by environmental exposure.
sampleAnswer: An educational program in schools could teach students that UV exposure causes skin cancer, and that to protect from this, they should wear hats and sunscreen when outside. This will help increase understanding of the risk and therefore increase compliance, helping to prevent the cause of skin cancer (UV rays damaging DNA in cells).

subject: Biology
questionText:
One-Eyed Jack was a rescue dog that had been injured and lost an eye before his owner adopted him. One-Eyed Jack was cloned and the clone was born with two eyes.

Explain why the cloned dog was born with two eyes.
sampleAnswer: The rescue dog had been injured and lost an eye but it still had the genetic code for two eyes which the clone inherited.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe how animals like dogs can be cloned.
sampleAnswer: The egg of a host animal is enucleated (nucleus removed). The nucleus of a body cell of the animal to be cloned is removed. The nucleus is inserted into the enucleated egg to produce a zygote. The zygote is stimulated to divide via electric charge. The zygote is then transplanted into the surrogate mother's uterus. After a period of time, a cloned animal is born.

subject: Biology
questionText: Describe a plant disease and its effect on agricultural production.
sampleAnswer: Stone fruit scab is a fungal disease that affects stone fruit such as plums, peaches and nectarines. It starts as small dark spots on the fruit which later become scabby and may cause the fruit to crack, shrivel and fall off the plant. It causes downgrading of fruit quality and decreased fruit yield, leading to economic losses.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Milk pasteurisation (heating to approximately 70°C) was gradually introduced in America from the early 1900s.

Explain the trends observed in the graph. In your response, refer to the role of Pasteur's work in pasteurisation.
diagramCaption: A line graph showing disease outbreaks from raw and pasteurised milk in America from 1900-1975.
sampleAnswer: Pasteur's work disproved spontaneous generation and showed heating kills microbes. This explains why pasteurised milk reduces disease outbreaks. The graph shows raw milk outbreaks decreased significantly after 1945, possibly due to fewer people drinking raw milk. Pasteurised milk outbreaks were consistently much lower. Some outbreaks from pasteurised milk could be due to problems during pasteurisation, storage or transport.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Cystic fibrosis is an inherited disorder. Two healthy parents, heterozygous for cystic fibrosis, have a child.

Using a Punnett square, determine the probability of their second child being born with the condition.
sampleAnswer:
R r
R RR Rr
r Rr rr
There is a 25% (1 in 4) chance of the second child having cystic fibrosis.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain how the deletion of nucleotides in the CFTR gene removes only one amino acid. Include reference to the nucleotides that code for isoleucine and phenylalanine amino acids.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing the CFTR gene mRNA sequence with isoleucine, phenylalanine and other amino acids labelled, plus a codon chart.
sampleAnswer: A triplet of mRNA nucleotides codes for one amino acid. However, the three nucleotides that are removed span across two triplet codons – the codon for isoleucine and the codon for phenylalanine. The correct coding for isoleucine is AUC, but after the deletion, the code left is AUU, which also codes for isoleucine (degeneracy of the genetic code). Thus, only the phenylalanine amino acid is removed.

subject: Biology
questionText:
An epidemiological study was conducted to model how many people will be affected by Type 2 diabetes globally.

Plot the data on the grid provided and include the line of best fit.
diagramCaption: A grid with axes for Year (1990-2040) and Percentage of population affected by Type 2 diabetes.
sampleAnswer: Points correctly plotted at (1990, 3.1), (2000, 3.7), (2010, 4.3), (2020, 5.6) with an appropriate line of best fit showing an increasing trend.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A prediction of the global population numbers suggests there will be about 9 billion people on the planet by 2040.

Predict the number of people that will be affected by diabetes in 2040.
sampleAnswer: Extrapolating the line of best fit to 2040 gives approximately 8% affected. 8% of 9 billion = 0.08 × 9,000,000,000 = 720,000,000 people (approximately 720 million).

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows a simplified version of the process of polypeptide synthesis.

Compare Process A with DNA replication.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing polypeptide synthesis with Process A in the nucleus and Process B at the ribosomes.
sampleAnswer: Both Process A (transcription) and DNA replication involve unwinding of the DNA double strand to produce a complementary strand. In replication, DNA is copied to produce two identical strands of DNA, whereas in transcription, DNA is copied to produce a single strand of mRNA.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the importance of mRNA and tRNA in polypeptide synthesis.
sampleAnswer: mRNA is synthesised from the DNA template in the nucleus during transcription. It carries the genetic instructions (codons) from the DNA to the ribosomes in the cytoplasm. Once at the ribosome, translation begins. Each tRNA molecule has an anticodon that pairs with a complementary codon on the mRNA strand. The mRNA codons are read in sequence, and as each tRNA brings its specific amino acid to the ribosome, the polypeptide is assembled.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A study monitored the changes in the body temperature of a kookaburra and a human over a 24-hour period.

At what time was the kookaburra's body temperature the lowest?
diagramCaption: A graph showing body temperatures of a kookaburra and human over 24 hours, with an inactive period marked.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Some endothermic organisms can display torpor (a significant decrease in physiological activity).

With reference to the graph, explain whether the human or the kookaburra was displaying torpor and if so, state the time this occurred.
sampleAnswer: The human did not display torpor, since their body temperature remained fairly constant throughout the time period. The kookaburra did display torpor, since they had a decrease in body temperature between 5 pm and 4 am, which indicates a decrease in physiological functioning.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline an adaptation that may lead to an increase in the kookaburra's body temperature during the inactive period.
sampleAnswer: The kookaburra can raise its temperature by fluffing up feathers which traps a layer of warm air between the feathers and the body. This retains heat.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Helicobacter pylori is a bacterium that invades the gut lining and can cause damage to the stomach.

With reference to innate and adaptive immunity, explain how the body responds after exposure to Helicobacter pylori.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing Helicobacter pylori invading the gut lining with inflammatory cells present.
sampleAnswer: Damaged cells cause chemicals to be released, causing inflammation. Dilation of blood vessels increases blood flow, facilitating movement of phagocytes. Phagocytes process bacterial antigens and present them to helper T-cells, triggering the adaptive immune response. Cytotoxic T-cells attack the bacteria while memory T-cells remain for secondary response. Plasma B-cells produce specific antibodies while memory B-cells remain for secondary response. Both innate (rapid, immediate) and adaptive (slow but specific) responses work together.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Female Jack Jumper ants have a single pair of chromosomes. During meiosis, crossing over occurs.

Outline the significance of crossing over for the Jack Jumper ants.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing homologous chromosomes with three genes undergoing crossing over.
sampleAnswer: Crossing over increases genetic variation, which gives the Jack Jumper ant a better chance to survive environmental change.

subject: Biology
questionText: Draw the chromosomes of the four possible gametes after crossing over for the Jack Jumper ants occurs. Include the alleles for each gene.
sampleAnswer: Four possible gametes: 1) D E H, 2) D e h, 3) d E H, 4) d e h (where the crossing over has swapped alleles between the chromatids).

subject: Biology
questionText: Discuss the ethical implications and impacts on society of the use of TWO biotechnologies.
sampleAnswer: Recombinant DNA technology (e.g., Bt corn/cotton): GM seeds cost money each season, leading to inequalities in access. Benefits include increased food production and greater yields. Selective breeding/hybridisation (e.g., dairy cows): Side-effects like decreased fertility affect quality of life for animals. Benefits include greater milk availability and increased profits for farmers.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows the results of a survey conducted to determine if children changed their method of communication after cochlear implantation.

With reference to the data, describe how cochlear implants work, and how they affect communication in children.
diagramCaption: A graph showing changes in communication methods in children after cochlear implantation at different ages.
sampleAnswer: Cochlear implants are electronic devices surgically inserted into the cochlea to improve hearing when the cochlea is damaged. They directly stimulate the auditory nerve and help carry sound signals to the brain. Children implanted at a young age reduce their use of sign language so that approximately 10% use sign language five years after implantation. Older children (>5 years) continue to use sign language. The data suggest cochlear implants change communication when implanted at a younger age.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Amoeba reproduce by the process shown.

Which of the following is a characteristic of the daughter cells?
A. Genetically different to parent amoeba
B. Genetically identical to parent amoeba
C. More organelles due to the cell division
D. Fewer chromosomes than the parent amoeba
diagramCaption: A diagram showing amoeba reproduction: parent amoeba → nucleus divides → cytoplasm divides → two daughter cells.

subject: Biology
questionText:

questionText:

A. Phagocytes=Engulf bacteria, Lymphocytes=Produce antibodies
B. Phagocytes=Engulf bacteria, Lymphocytes=Produce antigens

B. Natural passive
C. Acquired active
D. Acquired passive

subject: Biology
questionText:
An animal's body temperature and the air temperature of the animal's environment were measured every 4 hours.

Based on this data, which row of the table indicates what type of animal it is and why?
A. Ectotherm – Body temperature varies with air temperature
B. Ectotherm – Body temperature is always above air temperature
C. Endotherm – Body temperature is relatively constant despite changes in air temperature
D. Endotherm – Body temperature is relatively constant and air temperature is relatively constant
diagramCaption: A table showing body temperature and air temperature measurements every 4 hours over 24 hours.

subject: Biology
questionText:
When a red camellia flower is crossed with a white camellia flower, all the offspring are covered in both red and white petals.

What is the reason for this occurrence?
A. One gene is controlling multiple characteristics.
B. Environmental factors affect the phenotype of camellia flowers.
C. Alleles for both red and white colour in camellia flowers are recessive.
D. Petal colour in camellia flowers is controlled by a co-dominance pattern of inheritance.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Dialysis is used to assist people with loss of kidney function.

Which row of the table correctly describes the composition of blood before and after dialysis?
A. Before: High urea and high glucose; After: Low urea and high glucose
B. Before: High urea and high glucose; After: High urea and low glucose
C. Before: Low urea and low glucose; After: Low urea and high glucose
D. Before: Low urea and high glucose; After: Low urea and low glucose

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows the number of recorded deaths, due to measles, before and after the measles vaccine was included in the National Immunisation Program (NIP).

Which of the following is a trend shown in the graph?
A. Most people have been immunised against measles since 1975.
B. Over the last 100 years the number of deaths has consistently fallen.
C. The number of measles-induced deaths has fallen to near zero since measles vaccine was added to the NIP.
D. The National Immunisation Program was the primary reason for the great reduction in measles-induced deaths.
diagramCaption: A graph showing number of measles deaths from 1907-2012 with a marker at 1975 when measles vaccine was added to the NIP.

subject: Biology
questionText:
In 2018, the Victorian government reported the target of vaccinating more than 95% of children below the age of five had been achieved.

What is the benefit of achieving a 95% vaccination rate for an infectious disease?
A. Only 5% of individuals will catch the disease.
B. Only 5% of individuals will be protected by the vaccine.
C. This will protect the remaining 5% who are not vaccinated.
D. The vaccinated individuals can transfer immunity to the remaining 5%.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following represents a karyotype for an individual with Down syndrome.

What conclusions can be drawn about the individual from this karyotype?
A. A male with a deletion mutation
B. A female with a deletion mutation
C. A male with a chromosomal mutation
D. A female with a chromosomal mutation
diagramCaption: A karyotype showing chromosomes 1-22 plus X and Y, with trisomy 21 (three copies of chromosome 21).

subject: Biology
questionText:
The prevalence of a non-infectious disease has remained constant for over 10 years. A new treatment prolongs the life of people with the disease but does not cure them.

Which row shows the effect of the treatment on both the incidence and prevalence?
A. Incidence: None, Prevalence: Increases
B. Incidence: None, Prevalence: Decreases
C. Incidence: Increases, Prevalence: None
D. Incidence: Decreases, Prevalence: None

subject: Biology
questionText:
A pathogen that produces an immune response is shown.

Which antibody will be produced as a response to the pathogen?
diagramCaption: A diagram showing a pathogen with specific shaped antigens and four antibody options with different binding shapes.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Cochlear implants assist with hearing. The following are five steps involved in the process.

Which is the correct order for this process?
A. 1, 4, 3, 2, 5
B. 1, 3, 2, 4, 5
C. 3, 1, 5, 2, 4
D. 3, 1, 2, 5, 4

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows a process that was used to make multiple clones in sheep.

The identical offspring are clones of
A. each other.
B. only sheep B.
C. the surrogates.
D. both sheep A and B.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing the cloning process: Sheep A (male) and Sheep B (female) produce an early embryo, cells are separated, multiplied, implanted into surrogates, producing identical offspring.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Some phases of cell division in an organism are shown.

What is the correct sequence of the process?
A. Q, S, R, T
B. Q, R, S, T
C. S, T, R, Q
D. S, R, T, Q
diagramCaption: Four diagrams showing different phases of cell division labelled Q, R, S, T.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The fruit fly, Drosophila melanogaster, normally has large round eyes. A mutation of the eye shape gene, found on the X-chromosome, causes bar-eye, which is a dominant allele.

Offspring were produced when a bar-eyed male was crossed with a normal female.

Which row shows the correct percentage of male and female offspring with bar-eye?
A. Males=100%, Females=100%
B. Males=0%, Females=100%
C. Males=100%, Females=0%
D. Males=50%, Females=0%
diagramCaption: Images showing normal eye and bar-eye phenotypes in Drosophila.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The grasshopper has an average genome size of 120 million base pairs. 42% of these base pairs contain cytosine and guanine.

What is the number of DNA nucleotides with a thymine base found in each cell?
A. 25.2 million
B. 34.8 million
C. 69.6 million
D. 120 million

subject: Biology
questionText:
Researchers studying T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (T-ALL) examined a section of DNA in individuals A and B.

Based on this information, which of the following statements is the best explanation of the cause of T-ALL?
A. T-ALL is caused by an inactive Oncogene LMO 2.
B. The regulating proteins are the direct cause of T-ALL.
C. The regulating proteins have no effect as they are made on non-coding DNA.
D. The regulating proteins can activate the Oncogene LMO 2, causing the condition.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing DNA sections for Individual A (unaffected, oncogene LMO2 switched off) and Individual B (affected, regulating proteins activate the oncogene).

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows components of the innate immune system in humans.

State the role of TWO components that protect against infection.
diagramCaption: A diagram of a human body showing components of the innate immune system: nasal hair, tear glands, mucus lining, skin, stomach acid, urinary tract.
sampleAnswer: Stomach acid: The acid inhibits the growth of or kills bacteria/pathogens. Skin: Acts as a physical barrier to pathogen entry into the body.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline a process used by fungi for reproduction.
sampleAnswer: Fungi can reproduce asexually by producing spores which are released and when carried into a favourable environment they germinate and grow into a new fungal organism.

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline an adaptation in a pathogen that facilitates transmission between hosts.
sampleAnswer: After colonising a host, fungi can produce spores which can be released to infect a new host.

subject: Biology
questionText: Compare the processes of artificial insemination and artificial pollination.
sampleAnswer: A similarity is that both processes involve gametes of an organism. A difference is that artificial insemination occurs only in animals while artificial pollination occurs only in (flowering) plants.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following flow chart represents the control of body temperature in humans.

Complete the flow chart to give an example of mechanism A and an example of mechanism B.
diagramCaption: A flow chart showing negative feedback control of body temperature with blank spaces for mechanism A (cooling) and mechanism B (heating).
sampleAnswer:
Mechanism A – sweating (to decrease body temperature)
Mechanism B – shivering (to increase body temperature)

subject: Biology
questionText: Outline how mechanism B maintains homeostasis.
sampleAnswer: When the body temperature drops, skeletal muscles start shivering. This generates body heat and raises the temperature. This helps maintain homeostasis.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The graph shows the changes in UV level in a single day.

The Cancer Council suggests that sun protection is needed whenever the UV level is 3 or above. The information provided on a sunscreen product suggests that sunscreen should be used between 10 am and 4 pm.

Using the graph, evaluate the information provided on the sunscreen product with regard to the Cancer Council suggestion.
diagramCaption: A graph showing UV levels over a 24-hour period, with peaks around midday reaching approximately 12-13.
sampleAnswer: The product statement is incorrect because a person is not fully protected from UV. UV levels greater than 3 occur between approximately 8 am and 6 pm, and so for the given day there is about 2 hours before and after the recommended times where a person would have no protection from UV.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The diagram shows the steps in LASIK surgery.

Compare the LASIK technology shown with ONE other technology that can be used to treat a named visual disorder.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing four steps of LASIK surgery: numbing drops, corneal flap incision, excimer laser reshaping, and healing.
sampleAnswer: Myopia can be corrected by wearing glasses with concave lenses which refract light so the image falls on the retina rather than before it. This is similar to LASIK surgery where the cornea is reshaped to precisely refract light to reach the retina. LASIK is an intrusive and more expensive procedure whereas prescription glasses are less expensive and non-intrusive.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The table shows the number of eggs produced by females in TWO different groups of animals.

Compare the types of fertilisation that occur in group A and group B animals with reference to the data provided.
diagramCaption: A table showing Group A (Monotremes 1-3, Snakes 1-100, Birds 1-17 eggs) and Group B (Crabs 1000-2000, Sea urchins 100,000-2 million, Squid 2000-3000 eggs).
sampleAnswer: Group A animals are terrestrial with internal fertilisation. Group B animals are aquatic with external fertilisation. In both groups, fertilisation involves fusion of gametes. In Group A, fertilisation occurs inside the female body with fewer gametes needed (e.g., 1-100 in snakes). In Group B, much larger numbers of gametes are released (e.g., 100,000-2 million in sea urchins) due to lower probability of success in aquatic environments. Internal fertilisation ensures higher survival rates of fewer offspring.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Alpha-gal syndrome (AGS) is a tick-borne allergy to red meat caused by tick bites.

The flow chart shows the process of antibody production following exposure to alpha-gal.

Describe the role of X, Y and Z in the process of antibody production.
diagramCaption: A flow chart showing: Alpha-gal → Macrophage → X → Y → Z → Memory cells and Antibodies.
sampleAnswer: The tick bite transfers antigenic alpha-gal sugar molecules into the skin. Macrophages, together with Helper T cells (X), activate B-lymphocytes (Y). These cells grow and differentiate into either memory cells or plasma cells (Z) which produce antibodies against the antigen.

subject: Biology
questionText:
An allergic reaction to alpha-gal sugar is similar to a secondary immune response.

Describe the features of antibody production shown in the graph.
diagramCaption: A graph showing antibody concentration over time with two peaks - one after tick bite and a larger one after meat is eaten.
sampleAnswer: On first exposure to the alpha-gal sugar by the tick bite, antibody production takes longer and only a lower concentration of antibodies is produced. On re-exposure, antibodies are produced more quickly and a higher concentration is produced.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the role of memory cells in the immune response.
sampleAnswer: Memory cells are created by B-cells in response to a specific antigen. Memory cells remain in the lymph nodes after the infection. On re-exposure to the antigen, these memory cells quickly differentiate into plasma cells or cytotoxic T-cells, which neutralise the pathogen more rapidly to prevent disease.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The Varroa mite is an external parasite of European honey bees.

Why is Varroa mite infection considered to be an infectious disease?
sampleAnswer: Varroa mite is an infectious disease because the mite is a parasitic pathogen that is spread between bees and hives that come into direct contact with the pest.

subject: Biology
questionText:
In June 2022, the Varroa mite was detected for the first time in Australia at the Port of Newcastle.

Explain TWO procedures that could have been employed to prevent the spread of the Varroa mite in honey bees.
sampleAnswer: 1. Early identification of Varroa mite infestation ensures early detection and isolation of infected bee populations, stopping them from spreading to healthy bees. 2. Destroy infected hives – this will kill the mites and infected bees, preventing the spread of the mite to healthy hives.

subject: Biology
questionText:
PAI-1 protein is encoded by the SERPINE 1 gene in humans. Anopheles mosquitoes have been genetically modified to express PAI-1.

Describe a process that could be used to produce mosquitoes which express PAI-1.
sampleAnswer: Restriction enzymes are used to cut the SERPINE 1 gene from a human cell chromosome. A bacterial plasmid is opened using the same restriction enzymes and the SERPINE 1 gene is inserted using DNA ligase. The bacteria reproduce, producing multiple copies. The gene can then be delivered into mosquito egg cells by micro-injection, so that the mosquitoes which develop will contain and express the SERPINE 1 gene, producing PAI-1.

subject: Biology
questionText:
'Genetic technologies are beneficial for society.'

Evaluate this statement.
sampleAnswer: Recombinant DNA technology has allowed human insulin production in bacteria, benefitting diabetes sufferers with greater access and lower cost. CRISPR can control pest populations including disease-causing insects, reducing vector-borne diseases like dengue fever. These technologies have largely been beneficial, improving access to drugs and quality of life. However, concerns include unintended health effects, environmental risks, and ethical concerns about genetic modification.

subject: Biology
questionText:
Congenital amegakaryocytic thrombocytopenia (CAMT) is a rare, inherited disorder.

What type of inheritance is shown in the pedigree above? Justify your answer.
diagramCaption: A pedigree chart showing inheritance of CAMT across three generations with affected and unaffected males and females.
sampleAnswer: Type of inheritance: autosomal recessive. Individuals 3 and 4 have affected and unaffected children. Since 9 is an unaffected male, 3 would have to be heterozygous (Bb) and 4 would be homozygous recessive. For 10 to be affected, they inherited the recessive allele from both parents.

subject: Biology
questionText:
A CAMT mutation was found to produce the following amino acid sequence: Glutamine – Tyrosine – Isoleucine – Aspartic acid. The same DNA fragment has been sequenced from an unaffected individual: Template strand GTC ATA CAG CTG.

Use the codon chart to explain the type of mutation which causes CAMT.
diagramCaption: A codon chart showing all mRNA codons and their corresponding amino acids.
sampleAnswer: The amino acid sequence in the unaffected individual is Glutamine-Tyrosine-Valine-Aspartic acid. The codon GUC coding for Valine is replaced with AUC, producing Isoleucine. As a single nucleotide is changed, CAMT is a point mutation (missense/substitution).

subject: Biology
questionText:
A population lives across three regions, A, B and C. People in community B developed an environmental disease.

Design an epidemiological study that could be used to produce the results shown in the graph. Justify the features of your design.
diagramCaption: A map showing three regions A, B, C connected by a road bridge, and a graph showing risk of developing environmental disease by age at exposure over time.
sampleAnswer: Individuals from age groups 10, 20 and 30 in areas A, B and C should be surveyed over 55 years. Areas A and B are connected via a road bridge so communities can mix, but area C is isolated and serves as a control group. The study should include equal numbers of males and females. Data must be collected on diet, exercise, exposure to disease-causing agents, lifestyle and health. Correlating data to find common features would help determine risk due to age at exposure. Large sample sizes improve validity.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following diagram shows the cell division processes occurring in two related individuals.

Compare the cell division processes carried out by cells R and S in Individual 1.
diagramCaption: A diagram showing cell division processes in two individuals, with cells R, S, germ-line cells, somatic cells, sperm, egg, and zygote labelled.
sampleAnswer: Cell R undergoes mitosis which results in two genetically identical daughter cells. Cell S undergoes meiosis with half the chromosome number producing gametes. Both mitosis and meiosis require DNA replication where the genetic content doubles.

subject: Biology
questionText: Explain the relationship between Individuals 1 and 2.
sampleAnswer: Individual 1 is the parent of Individual 2 because they provide a gamete to produce the offspring which is Individual 2.

subject: Biology
questionText: A and B are two separate mutations. Analyse how mutations A and B affect the genetic information present in cells U, V, W and X.
sampleAnswer: Mutation A is a germline mutation and will not be present in Individual 1, but could be present in offspring including Individual 2. Mutation B is a somatic cell mutation and will only be present in some cells of Individual 2 but not their offspring. Cell U will not have mutations A or B. Cell V could have mutation A but not B. Cell W could have both mutations. Cell X could carry mutation A.

subject: Biology
questionText:
The following graphs show the changes in allele frequencies in two separate populations of the same species.

Explain why the fluctuations of the allele frequencies are more pronounced in the small population, compared to the larger population.
diagramCaption: Two graphs showing allele frequencies over 50 generations for populations of 20 and 2000, with the small population showing much more dramatic fluctuations.
sampleAnswer: The smaller the population size, the smaller the gene pool. Small gene pools have decreased genetic diversity, so any fluctuations in allele frequency will have a greater effect on the gene pool.

subject: Biology
questionText: Evaluate the effects of gene flow on the gene pools of the two populations.
sampleAnswer: Small populations have limited genetic diversity. Gene flow can benefit the gene pool by introducing genetic variation. In favourable conditions, the gene can spread easily. In unfavourable conditions, it will be quickly removed by natural selection. In larger populations, gene flow has less pronounced effects due to higher existing genetic diversity. The graph shows small populations have dramatic allele frequency changes while larger populations fluctuate little. Gene flow can be positive or negative depending on population size and environmental conditions.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Poem

Explain how Boomerangs in a Thunderstorm represents an intense moment.
sampleAnswer:
Samuel Wagan Watson’s poem Boomerangs in a Thunderstorm conveys an intense spiritual 
experience by using the onomatopoeia of ‘thunder cracking’ and the image of ‘rain disguised 
tears’. Watson’s intense cultural connection with his uncle and the land is bound by the act of 
throwing ‘boomerangs in a thunderstorm’ to reflect and celebrate the power of the human 
spirit and of the natural environment.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Prose fiction extract

Analyse how the experience of returning home has been shaped by the writer.
sampleAnswer:
The writer shapes this moment of returning home as an emotional and complex experience. 
The short sentence ‘He hopes’ establishes emotional suspense for the reader that gives way 
to the paradoxical ‘nauseating nostalgia’ the man feels as he approaches land. The 
cumulation of contrasting adjectives when he arrives: ‘ambitions for solitariness’ and ‘groggy’, 
‘uncertain’, ‘drabness’ show a mixed emotional response to coming back home after twenty 
years away. The reader develops empathy for the man as he admits, finally, through 
dialogue that ‘it’s twenty years . . . since I’ve been here’ and the waitress responds 
sarcastically, ‘lucky you’. Furthermore, the ‘sluicing out’ and the proclamation ‘one should 
forgive places as much as people’ suggests that from this homecoming the man may heal 
whatever wounds he has brought with him.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Prose fiction extract

How is metaphor used in Lines, Planes and Bodies to represent human experience?
sampleAnswer:
The writer uses the metaphor of ‘stages’ to represent the world as she sees it. When she 
writes ‘the windows turn into little stages on which actors act out their evenings’, the 
metaphor highlights the fragmented view of human experience. The narrative viewpoint 
positions the reader as an observer who sees the ‘moments, crumbs, fleeting configurations’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Feature article

How does the feature article explore the paradox of boredom?
sampleAnswer:
The feature article explores the ways in which boredom is both feared and essential to the 
creative human spirit through the use of irony and juxtaposition. Ryan, through his use of 
critical experts such as Mary Mann who sets up the notion that ‘boredom is a challenge’, 
expresses the idea that we must aspire to boredom when, ironically, it is a basic human trait. 
The interweaving of the anecdote of Christopher Knight’s ‘full-Robinson Crusoe’ 
disappearance creates a personal inflection that is both familiar and alien to the reader; we 
can empathise with his regret at the loss of ‘somewhere between quiet and solitude . . . 
stillness’, but baulk at the idea of ‘simply [being] alone in the forest, a solitary adventurer 
discovering what mysteries lay [beyond] boredom’. The paradox that ‘being challenged is 
often good for us’, creates the image of the necessity of boredom, itself a paradox.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Feature article and Text 1, Text 2 and Text 3

To what extent is the significance of ordinary experiences explored in the feature article and ONE other text?

Support your response with reference to the feature article and ONE other text from the Stimulus Booklet.
sampleAnswer:
The ordinary experiences in i’m bored, therefore i am are explored through the time and 
space that boredom brings to make room for self-reflection and a change in perspective, 
while Lines, Planes and Bodies examines the significance of observing others to see the 
world differently. 
The feature article represents the value of ordinary experiences through the use of irony. 
Ryan has ironically portrayed boredom, something that would not be considered an everyday 
pleasant experience, as something important, through the use of expert opinions. He quotes 
Mary Mann as saying ‘Boredom is a challenge’ and ‘But we’re in a transition now’. This 
shows the value of the ordinary experience of boredom, as challenges are something that 
humans need and, consequently, that boredom is an experience that, ironically, needs to be 
made ordinary. 
On the other hand, the reflective qualities in Lines, Planes and Bodies are more geared 
towards exploring others’ experiences in a passive way, using the ‘camera obscura’ 
metaphor. Tokarczuk relies on observing others, “Life’. Dutch painting. Moving lives.” 
Tokarczuk also subverts the passivity of observation, turning it to amusement: ‘Some say 
that this is a boring play . . . But I like it’, while Ryan’s notion of the ordinary relies on 
introspection about one’s self.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Prose Fiction — Anthony Doerr, All the Light We Cannot See

To what extent does the exploration of human experience in All the Light We Cannot See invite you to reconsider your understanding of courage?

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Internet article and Text 2 — Illustration

How do these texts use a variety of language forms and features to communicate ideas about being creative?
diagramCaption: Illustration by Julie Paschkis accompanying an internet article about creativity
sampleAnswer:
These texts use a variety of language forms and features to communicate ideas about how 
being creative not only requires dedication, effort and being open to the world, but also 
brings joy and satisfaction through the act of creating. For example, Frayn’s clear, almost 
practical voice details how writers are ‘lured on’ by ideas that come into their heads. Frayn 
conveys the idea that being creative requires hard work by describing how it is not a life of 
‘great freedom’. He details being receptive to ideas that arrive, to ‘characters [who] suggest 
themselves’ through the metaphor of being ‘led into this new world’. Frayn sees the act of 
being creative through the analogy of discovery – which he sees as something very different 
to the act of invention: being creative means discovering what is already there, following it 
and acting on it. The illustration, with its symbols of the bird and pencil, highlights how art 
and creativity bring freedom and joy to individuals. The journey of creativity, with its sense of 
discovering rich new ideas and unknown aspects of the world, is depicted in the illustration 
through the sea of flowing, poetic words that keeps the smiling, content writer afloat. The 
creativity that is evident in this illustration by Julie Paschkis captures the joy that comes from 
the act of creating something vibrant, fresh and joyful.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Poem

How does the poem explore the power of storytelling?
sampleAnswer:
Andy Kissane’s poem, It Begins with Darkness, details a father’s first encounter with his 
son’s performance as an actor, using images of light and darkness to depict the 
transformative effect the evening has on the father. The first stanza establishes the theatre 
as a place of storytelling and the father’s sense of dislocation and displacement through ‘I 
don’t know what I’m doing here,/I just know that this is theatre, my son an actor.’ The father 
is characterised through his depiction as a ‘boilermaker’ who exults in ‘the flying sparks from 
an arc welder’ and someone who recognises his son’s performance is ‘a different kind of 
trade.’ However, Kissane gradually removes the father’s sense of dislocation through 
highlighting the effect his son’s performance – and the story being told on stage – has on 
him. He moves from wanting to ‘go down and slap him about the face’ because of his 
swearing to wanting to ‘reach out/and lift him up as I did when he was two’. The final image 
of the burning scrap of paper illuminating his son’s face and revealing the 
‘unmistakable/features of my father who is ten years dead’ creates a powerful resolution that 
binds the three generations of men together through a ‘story/that takes a whole evening in 
the telling’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Nonfiction extract

Explain how this text examines the human experience of laughter.
sampleAnswer:
In On Laughter, Eagleton examines how the human reaction of laughter is something that 
has a language of its own, how it is remarkably fluid, lacks uniformity and can be completely 
divorced from humour. He paraphrases Samuel Johnson to initialise his thesis, disagreeing 
with the statement that humans ‘have always laughed in the same way’, accumulating an 
extensive list of adjectives to detail the many different ways in which humans laugh – 
‘cackling, chortling, grunting … screeching and so on.’ This almost exhaustive list is also 
designed to amuse and engage our senses through detailing the wonderful variety of ways 
that people laugh. 
Eagleton’s distinction between smiling as a visual mode and laughter as an aural mode is 
designed to communicate the complex reactions humans have to a complex world. His short 
piece concludes with examples of how one way of describing a person’s laugh can be 
performed by a person in two completely different ways. He combines the verb ‘bray’ with 
two opposing adverbs, ‘genially’ and ‘aggressively’, to demonstrate how difficult it is to pin 
down the way the language of laughter works.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 5 — Prose fiction extract

Analyse the ways this text represents the relationship between identity and place.
sampleAnswer:
Throughout this extract from Carpentaria, Alexis Wright establishes the complex relationship 
between identity and place juxtaposing the everyday and the supernatural using Normal ‘an 
old tribal man’ who lives in a ‘foreign infestation on the edge of Desperance’. 
Wright details the relationship between identity and place through the connection Normal has 
with sea and sky that has been passed down to him by his ancestors, a connection that 
allows him to ‘grab hold of the river in his mind and live with it as his father’s fathers did 
before him’. This spiritual connection with land, combined with an ability to run off into the 
night ‘trying to catch stars’ is something Wright describes to ensure Normal’s identity is 
connected with both the present and the past. 
While Wright’s use of third person narrative has the potential to distance us from Normal, it 
also allows her to shift the point of view so that we see him from a number of different 
perspectives – through the eyes of the Pricklebush mob, the people of the town of 
Desperance and the eyes of ‘everyone’. Wright uses this third person perspective to give us 
a wider view of how people view Normal as he drives ‘north to meet the river’s edge’. It also 
allows her to focus in on the places that we have heard about, but no-one else has gone to: 
‘his gun pointing all over the place in a turmoil of water … until he made a direct hit between 
the eyes of the reptile caught in an instant of moonlight’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Module C: The Craft of Writing
questionText:
"Once there were brook trout in the streams in the mountains. You could see them standing in the amber current where the white edges of their fins wimpled softly in the flow... On their backs were vermiculate patterns that were maps of the world in its becoming. Maps and mazes. Of a thing which could not be put back. Not be made right again. In the deep glens where they lived all things were older than man and they hummed of mystery." — Cormac McCarthy, The Road

(a) Use this extract as a stimulus for a piece of imaginative or discursive writing that explores what is truly valuable in a world of change.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Poem

Identify TWO language features used in Text 1 to represent human experience.
sampleAnswer:
Gray uses a wide range of sensory imagery to share his experience of obtaining new books 
with his readers, such as ‘brushing fingers over embossed titles’, ‘devour their smell’ and 
‘luscious scrape of cardboard on floor’. These phrases invite the reader to imagine how the 
physical experience of touching and smelling books is an integral part of Gray’s reading 
experience.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Nonfiction extract

Explain how the writer of Text 2 uses language to convey ideas about a human experience.
sampleAnswer:
The poet uses imagery to portray the persona’s memorable experience with B at Plaza 
Blanca. The quartz is an effective image as it is a part of the ancient landscape that 
symbolises ‘the enormous past’. There are also evocative contrasting images when the 
persona realises how momentary they are in comparison to the landscape. For example, the 
simile that describes ‘the white formations’ as being ‘ancient, as sleep is ancient’ is sharply 
contrasted with the metaphors describing their lives as ‘winks in a deep night, wrinkles/on a 
long green sea’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Prose fiction extract

How does the writer of Text 3 use narrative voice to represent a human experience?
sampleAnswer:
Vuong’s image of Little Dog plucking out Lan’s grey hairs is used to establish the close 
nature of their relationship: it is an intimate physical act where Little Dog ‘would kneel on a 
pillow behind her, the tweezers ready in my grip’. The length of this close relationship is 
communicated through Vuong’s use of figurative language when describing Lan’s ritual of 
telling cultural stories. Lan’s stories ‘spiralled out from her mind, only to return the next week’ 
and Little Dog would ‘mouth along with the sentences, as if watching a film for the umpteenth 
time – a movie made by Lan’s words and animated by my imagination’. This representation 
of a collaborative weekly ritual allows the reader to understand their deep creative 
relationship.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Prose fiction extract

Analyse how Text 4 represents a significant moment in a human experience.
sampleAnswer:
In this extract, Caia deliberately structures her writing to emphasise the transformative 
moment when the ‘woods became instantaneously new’. She recounts the events of her hike 
in the first paragraph then signals her return to the moment of realisation in the second 
paragraph with the short opening sentence: ‘I’ve left out a step.’ She also incorporates 
emotive language to reflect on the stages of her revelation, from her initial ‘swirl of frustration’ 
when realising she is lost to the subsequent ‘rush of exhilaration’ as the world’s hidden 
beauty is revealed. In the final theatrical metaphor (‘the woods were the stage. I was the 
audience.’), Caia imagines herself interacting with the landscape, which highlights the 
personal nature of her discovery.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 and ONE other text

To what extent does Text 4, along with ONE other text from the Stimulus Booklet, explore the human need for connection?

In your response, make detailed reference to Text 4 and your chosen text.
sampleAnswer:
The writer effectively uses the begrudging yet admiring narrative voice of Katherine O’Dell’s 
daughter to depict her mother as an actress in both her public and private worlds. The 
narrator vividly describes O’Dell’s actions in her everyday world as a performance. In 
response to the common question asked of her ‘what was she like?’ the narrator recounts the 
domestic act of O’Dell ‘eating toast’ to dramatise even this simple task as a performance. 
The narrator cynically describes how O’Dell ‘holds the slice of toast to her mouth, bites and 
chews, then bites again. Swallows’. This deliberately portrays her mother as a star ‘not just 
on screen or on the stage, but at the breakfast table also.’ Additionally, Enright successfully 
uses the adult narrator to reflect on a bittersweet childhood anecdote which describes how 
her mother fed ‘the dog who waits under the table, like a dog in the movies, for scraps and 
crumbs.’ The inclusion of this detail highlights how the resigned narrator perceives that 
Katherine O’Dell prioritised her role as a radiant actress over her motherly duties.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Poem

In what ways does Azzam celebrate togetherness?
sampleAnswer:
Azzam celebrates togetherness through the personification of the nine spices ‘careening’ and 
‘tangling’ together to represent a collective human experience. By detailing the origins of the 
spices from all around the world, such as the ‘West Indies’ and ‘Indonesia’, Azzam 
establishes the diversity of spices and, therefore, humanity. However, Azzam employs the 
collective terms ‘comrades’ and ‘unique union of nine’ to highlight that like spices, people can 
overcome their cultural differences to flourish together with ‘a vivacious global salute’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Prose fiction extract

Analyse how Fforde captures the narrator's experience of awe and wonder.
sampleAnswer:
The rich and detailed description by the first-person narrator of the ‘elegant’ and ‘endless’ 
library at the beginning of the extract creates a setting that shows their awe-struck response 
to the space. The focus shifts to the narrator’s description of the living qualities of the books, 
using the senses of touch and sound: the books are ‘warm’, and produce a ‘distant hum’ of 
the realities that lie within their pages. Fforde conveys the importance of the narrator’s 
epiphany by using heightened language to gather momentum, as ‘the clouds slid back from 
[their] mind’ and the new and awe-filled understanding ‘shone upon [them]’. The exclamatory 
revelation, ‘these books [are] alive!’ brings the experience of wonder to its climax.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Feature article extract

Explain how Gemmell explores the paradoxes of human behaviour in this extract.
sampleAnswer:
Gemmell uses alliteration and tricolon to evocatively highlight how humans are ‘plunderers, 
predators, polluters’ and yet observes, paradoxically, that we have left Antarctica ‘untouched’ 
except in the ‘pursuit of scientific inquiry’. Further, the inclusive language of ‘we’ and ‘our’ 
invites the reader to share her perspective about the paradoxical nature of humanity. 
Humans have the capacity to preserve ‘our beautiful planet’ when we ‘act with consensus 
and without greed’ like we have in Antarctica.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Memoir extract

Analyse how Saramago conveys the value of memory in this extract.
sampleAnswer:
Saramago conveys memory as valuable because of its ‘reconstructive powers’ which allow 
people to revisit the past. He switches between past, present and future tense to highlight 
how memory helps us to relive past experiences. Through memory Saramago claims he can, 
‘at any moment’, ‘rebuild’ his childhood house, ‘replant the olive tree’ and ‘go into the pigsties 
and watch the piglets’. These cumulative descriptions emphasise the value of memory, 
enabling him to return to his ‘true home’ even though it no longer exists.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 5 — Nonfiction extract and Text 6 — Photograph

Compare how Falconer and Paine represent interactions between humans and the natural world.
diagramCaption: Photograph by Paine showing interaction between humans and nature
sampleAnswer:
Both texts express human interaction with local landscapes. While Falconer focuses on 
humans using nature to construct a beautiful urban environment, Paine shows humans 
embracing the raw power of the natural world. 
In Sydney, the human desire to create natural beauty within a constructed urban 
environment is clearly represented through Falconer’s opening anecdote about how ‘each 
mother of a newborn’ was given ‘a jacaranda seedling’, establishing these trees as a symbol 
of fertility and the cycle of life. Through her vivid description of the trees as ‘glowing corridors’ 
that ‘flare violet’, she emphasises the aesthetic beauty of this constructed natural world. 
A different perspective is represented in Paine’s photograph where the central figure of a 
young girl, Daisy, sits on her bike in the middle of a road in a vast red and dry rural 
landscape. Immersed in her natural surroundings, she revels in the elements as she smiles 
into the wind. Her protective goggles represent her only acknowledgement of the dust storm 
cloud in the background of the shot. Daisy, resilient and fearless in the face of the dust 
storm, is in awe of its destructive power.

questionText:
"Her mind wandered. Last night had been something. Alive. Yes. She had felt alive. Kindled. Lit. Blazing. She had forgotten the way being around other people could feel... The sharing of food. The sharing of stories. The gathering around a fire. Elemental. And no one had required anything of her. She had been allowed to just listen. To absorb. She'd forgotten how much she was capable of absorbing." — Vanessa McCausland, The Beautiful Words

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Prose extract

Why does Dank prefer 'that gravel and dust comfort, away from that other place'?
sampleAnswer:
Dank prefers the familiarity of ‘that gravel and dust comfort’ because it represents the 
fulfilment of home. In contrast, the ‘strange new pressure of wet pebbles’ and ‘velvety, 
texture’ of ocean sand is confronting and ‘foreign’ to the ‘dry, dusty earth’ to which she feels 
a deep connection. Dank’s inability to find the same ‘comfort’ and connection at the beach is 
evident as the ‘sand and shells and seaweed remained just what they were … There was no 
story talking to my bones’. The contrasting feeling of the ‘sharp edges of the gravel and soft 
dust’ is where Dank feels grounded, providing a comforting and enriching ‘way into new 
stories’ that is soul-affirming.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Memoir extract

Analyse Langbroek's representation of the emotional impact of new places.
sampleAnswer:
Langbroek reveals the positive emotional impact of exploring new places by comparing this 
with the experience of falling in love. The reader is immersed in Langbroek’s experience 
through the use of a personal, reflective tone and inclusive language: we don’t know how a 
country will ‘open up to us, and us to it ’. The metaphorical relationship is established through 
positive, vivid images of life in Italy: ‘glittering seas’ and ‘golden light ’ which she associates 
with ‘falling in love’. Langbroek evokes an emotional response from the reader as we find 
familiarity and inclusion in the experience of new places and the feeling of love through the 
use of second person narration: ‘You feel alive … You are open and happy and free … You 
laugh …’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Feature article extract

How does Hamblin expand the reader's understanding of the paradoxes of consumerism?
sampleAnswer:
Hamblin expands the reader’s understanding of the paradoxes of consumerism by showing 
that ‘we stop appreciating things … They deteriorate or become obsolete’. Paradoxically, we 
usually think that material goods bring happiness but the connection from ‘trips, concerts, 
movies … tend to trump material purchases’ and provide ‘more enduring happiness’. The 
adjective ‘enduring’ suggests that experiential purchases form memories that connect us. 
The direct address ‘when you can’t live in a moment … live in anticipation of an experience’ 
and inclusive language ‘our memories and stories … get sweeter’ invites the reader to share 
Hamblin’s perspective on the paradoxical nature of consumerism.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Nonfiction – opinion piece

How does Robertson challenge the trend towards 'self-narrativisation' in modern culture?
sampleAnswer:
Robertson’s satirical piece challenges the reader to consider the trend towards ‘self­
narrativisation’ in modern culture as limiting for self-expression. Robertson’s humorous voice 
highlights the prolific use of the hero’s journey in reality television, which has come to dictate 
the way individuals construct their public identity; ‘nobody is simply chilling. They are 
triumphing over [insert hardship real or imagined]’. The reader is challenged by the irony of 
‘self-narrativisation’ in modern culture where individuals, in pursuit of an interesting personal 
narrative, metaphorically ‘sand off all the rough edges’, removing anything about their lives 
that makes them interesting. Robertson’s parody culminates in her ‘embarking on a journey 
to ban the word ‘journey’ , prompting the reader to reconsider the way in which we construct 
and share our personal stories.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 5 — Poem

Analyse how O'Sullivan captures the idea of being in the moment.
sampleAnswer:
O’Sullivan captures the idea of being in the moment through depicting the simple activity of 
two people collecting and eating apricots, leading to the speaker’s contemplation of the 
contentment that can come from ordinary experiences. The speaker’s rhetorical questions 
frame the idea that we should appreciate the elements of nature ‘being themselves, for 
themselves’. The visual images of the bees ‘arcing and mining’ and the ‘laden apricot tree’ 
prompt the speaker’s reflection on why we expect ‘an emblem at every turn’. The couple’s 
simple joy of this shared moment is represented as one holds out their hands ‘like the 
opened pages of a book’ and the other piles ‘apricots on them stacked three / deep’. The 
apricots ultimately ‘become a symbol’ of appreciating the moment, emphasising that just by 
‘being here’ in the present an individual can experience a fulfilment which will also become a 
lasting memory.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Nonfiction extract

How does Green invite the reader to consider aspects of the human sense of smell?
sampleAnswer:
Green invites the reader to consider how smell is the one human sense that cannot yet be 
replicated or reproduced. He shares a personal anecdote of his virtual reality experience on 
a roller coaster, describing how the ride ‘felt breath-stealingly real’ but the inaccurate 
imitation scent ‘wrenched’ him from his ‘state of joyfully suspended disbelief’. He expands on 
this point by challenging the reader to consider how humans are unable to accurately 
imagine and write about smell and ‘based on those stories’ even AI would get it 
‘spectacularly wrong’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Nonfiction extract

Analyse how Vincent connects the significance of measurement to human experience.
sampleAnswer:
Vincent begins with an accumulation of reasons designed to persuade the reader of the 
importance of measurement to the human experience. This includes figurative phrases like 
‘cornerstone of cognition’ and ‘tool of social cohesion and control’ that capture the role it 
plays in human thought and culture. He further connects its significance to all of humanity 
through using the plural first person in ‘If we could not measure, then we could not observe 
the world around us’. Vincent shares a personal anecdote about reporting on the redefinition 
of the kilogram, then reflects on the transformative nature of his discoveries on his worldview 
through the simile ‘I felt like I had opened the door of my flat… on to the surface of an alien 
planet’. Finally, Vincent captures the broad range of human endeavours that are shaped by 
and connected to measurement through the metaphorical description of it as ‘a banquet of 
historical, scientific, and sociological wonder’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 and Text 4 — Prose fiction extract and Photograph

Compare how Text 3 and Text 4 offer a perspective on the ways individuals perceive their surroundings.
diagramCaption: Photograph used alongside prose fiction extract for comparison
sampleAnswer:
In My Name is Lucy Barton, Strout represents the character’s profound engagement with the 
natural world by using a single run-on sentence to capture a 360-degree perspective of the 
horizon. In contrast, the photograph suggests that an individual’s perspective is filtered by 
technology, providing an altered version of their surroundings through the blurred landscape 
in the background and clear focus on the devices in the foreground. These visual choices 
highlight the ways an individual may have a limited view of the natural world because they 
are distracted by and over-reliant on technology. By comparison, Strout’s narrator makes 
detailed observations of their home landscape — ‘the few trees, the quiet field of cover crops’ 
— which results in their introspective revelation that ‘All life amazes me’. Therefore, Strout’s 
prose suggests a spiritual awakening and deep connection to the physical environment, 
whereas the photograph suggests some individuals’ preoccupation with connecting to the 
world through technology disconnects them f rom t he reality of their surroundings.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 5 — Poem

Analyse how Wilson represents the relationship between discomfort and joy.
sampleAnswer:
Wilson represents the individual’s relationship between discomfort and joy through the 
contrast between the enjoyment of the freedom of running and the surrounding dangers of 
the landscape. This is established through the use of contrasting natural imagery seen by the 
speaker while running through a bush track, of positive ‘yellow fireworks/… opalescent 
pearls’ and the negative ‘thunder claps/… burnt black’. The paradoxical relationship between 
physical discomfort and the rewarding emotional experience of the track is further 
emphasised by the use of the final enjambment ‘Soaked with rain/scared… I am 
immeasurably happy’.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 6 — Prose fiction extract

Assess how Diaz's use of language illuminates the dynamics within the Brevoort family.
sampleAnswer:
Diaz uses language to effectively illuminate the unconventional dynamics of the Brevoort 
family that are primarily made evident through the interactions between father and daughter. 
Leopold’s rejection as a writer which leaves him ‘Embittered’ is juxtaposed with his 
experience of joy as he ‘delighted in every facet of her personality’ when he takes charge of 
Helen’s education. This provides the reader with an insight into the positive dynamic within 
the Brevoort family. Diaz enhances the closeness between Leopold and Helen by their ‘walks 
along the [river]… discussing the natural phenomena around them’ and their ‘collection of 
fabricated, outrageous myths’ where the detailed imagery suggests an equal partnership in 
her education. Diaz’s use of Helen’s memories of her ‘studies under her father’s tutelage’ 
that would ‘remain the happiest of her life’ shows, through third person narrative point of 
view, the close bond that exists between the father and daughter. Diaz also skilfully 
establishes how the mother, Catherine, while ‘having a knack for’ entertaining and 
conversation, exists outside of Helen and Leopold’s dynamic. Diaz portrays her ‘[breaking] 
into their study to stop them laughing “like horses”’ which reinforces her exclusion from the 
father−daughter relationship

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 1 — Memoir extract

Explain how Jansson celebrates the process of creativity.
sampleAnswer:
Jansson celebrates the process of creativity, firstly through an appreciation of physical 
materials, ‘pretty rocks’ and ‘beautiful, heavy lumber’ as the raw materials of future projects. 
Jansson lists a range of unusual stone creations: ‘mosaics, bulwarks, terraces, supports, 
smoke ovens’, delighting in their variety ranging from decorative, useful or temporary, ‘which 
the sea will carry away’. This ultimately shows how Jansson privileges the creative process 
over the product. By contrasting Tooti’s creations in wood, ‘the smallest salt spoon ever 
made’ with ‘building big’, Jansson celebrates that creativity comes in different forms and 
sizes.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 2 — Prose fiction extract

How does Ferrante portray the ways that family relationships shape identity?
sampleAnswer:
Ferrante portrays contrasting relationships with different parts of the narrator’s family, leading 
to shifts in her identity. Her identity is initially significantly influenced by her father’s views, 
exemplified in the blunt and critical tone of ‘deprived me of the affection of people certainly 
without education, not smooth talkers’, emphasising her physical separation from lower 
classes of people, including her own extended family. This belief is challenged when 
Giovanna meets her relative, Vittoria, who teaches her the importance of ‘the heart’ which 
symbolises a person’s character as the most essential element of identity. This experience 
causes a deeper understanding of herself seen through the metaphorical shift from the 
formal ‘Giovanna’ to the affectionate nickname Giannina, which signifies her new ‘relaxed 
and funny’ identity.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 3 — Poem

Analyse how Kooser shares insights about the experience of change.
sampleAnswer:
Kooser frames the poem in letter form to signal that he wishes to share his experiences 
regarding the inevitability of change. The personification of the light to ‘walk down the hill’ 
enables him to share his delight in how the lake lights up to reveal ‘a garden/of trees that 
grew as if by magic’. In contrast the personification of ‘winter’ in its ‘jacket’, bringing its ‘black 
horse’, depicts winter bringing darkness. He metaphorically compares summer’s light, which 
allows one to ‘keep looking out’ with winter’s darkness which offers only a mirror that forces 
introspection. The imperative ‘must’ captures his final insight where one ‘must now keep 
looking in’ to examine how the passage of time requires change.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 4 — Prose fiction extract

How does Kingsolver expand the reader's understanding of the relationship between past and present?
sampleAnswer:
Kingsolver enables the reader to see the present through the lens of the past. The setting on 
Zebulon Mountain, coupled with the passing down of the name, Garnett Walker, from 
grandfather to grandson, portray a sense of heritage and conveys the relationship between 
Garnett’s family history on the mountain and his current experiences on his farm. Garnett’s 
revelation as the eighty-year-old protagonist that it was his grandfather who ‘felled that tree’, 
later used by the ‘useless mischief of boys’, cleverly layers past and present experiences 
together of Garnett as a child and adult and of his grandfather as a young man. Kingsolver 
resolves her piece with the wise dialogue ‘however fondly we might recall the simple times of 
old, they had their limits.’ She successfully portrays the complexity of current experiences so 
the reader understands them as the totality of what has come before.

subject: English Advanced
topic: Common Module: Texts and Human Experiences
questionText:
Text 5 — Nonfiction extract

Evaluate Macfarlane's representation of the connections between humans and the landscape.
sampleAnswer:
Macfarlane beautifully represents the ancient ties between humans and the landscape; ties 
which collapse time and move above and below the surface of the earth. The ash, as the 
central metaphor, binds sky, earth, and underland and links humans with the land: ‘Its crown 
flourishes skyward’, ‘its roots reach far underground’ while the ‘riven trunk’ is the portal to the 
underland. Although the rock art was made 35,000 years ago, Macfarlane provocatively 
narrates its creation in the present tense, skilfully conflating a sense of immediacy, intimacy 
and longevity: it is the past and the present. The ‘hand against the cave wall’ is a timeless 
symbol of human and landscape interaction. Macfarlane successfully captures the mystery of 
the underland through rhetorical questions about the prints as signs ‘Of joy? Of warning? 
Of art? Of life in the darkness?’ This deliberate acknowledgement of the unknowable is part 
of the ancient relationship between humans and the underland. The passive voice enables 
Macfarlane to emphasise the role the landscape plays; it moves humans through it: ‘the 
passage is taken’, ‘the passage turns’, ‘A chamber is entered.’ There is a sublime 
relationship between humans and this landscape.

History and Geography are two of the subjects students may decide to study. For a group of 40 students, the following is known.
• 7 students study neither History nor Geography
• 20 students study History
• 18 students study Geography

(a) A student is chosen at random. By using a Venn diagram, or otherwise, find the probability that the student studies both History and Geography.
sampleAnswer:
40 − 7 = 33 study at least one
20 + 18 − 33 = 5 study both
P(both) = 5/40 = 1/8

questionText: Sketch the graph of the curve y = −x³ + 3x² − 1, labelling the stationary points and point of inflection. Do NOT determine the x intercepts of the curve.
sampleAnswer:
y = −x³ + 3x² − 1
dy/dx = −3x² + 6x = 3x(2 − x)
Stationary points when dy/dx = 0: x = 0 (y = −1) and x = 2 (y = 3)
Stationary points: (0, −1) minimum, (2, 3) maximum
d²y/dx² = −6x + 6
Inflection when d²y/dx² = 0: x = 1, y = 1
Point of inflection: (1, 1)
Sketch shows cubic curve with local min at (0, −1), local max at (2, 3), and inflection at (1, 1).

questionText: Prove that sec θ − cos θ = sin θ tan θ.
sampleAnswer: LHS = sec θ − cos θ = 1/cos θ − cos θ = (1 − cos²θ)/cos θ = sin²θ/cos θ = sin θ × (sin θ/cos θ) = sin θ × tan θ = RHS

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: At what rate is the tea cooling 4 minutes after it has been poured?
sampleAnswer:
dT/dt = 70(1.5)^(−0.4t) × (−0.4) ln(1.5)
When t = 4: dT/dt = 70(1.5)^(−1.6)(−0.4) ln(1.5) = −5.934...
The tea is cooling at approximately 5.9°C per minute.
