subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A continuous random variable, X, has the following probability density function.
f(x) = sin x for 0 ≤ x ≤ k
f(x) = 0 for all other values of x

(a) Find the value of k.
sampleAnswer:
∫₀ᵏ sin x dx = 1
[−cos x]₀ᵏ = 1
−cos k + cos 0 = 1
−cos k + 1 = 1
cos k = 0
k = π/2

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find P(X ≤ 1). Give your answer correct to four decimal places.
sampleAnswer: P(X ≤ 1) = ∫₀¹ sin x dx = [−cos x]₀¹ = −cos 1 + 1 = 1 − cos 1 ≈ 0.4597

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The circle x² − 6x + y² + 4y − 3 = 0 is reflected in the x-axis.

Sketch the reflected circle, showing the coordinates of the centre and the radius.
sampleAnswer:
Complete the square:
x² − 6x + 9 + y² + 4y + 4 = 3 + 9 + 4
(x − 3)² + (y + 2)² = 16
Original circle: centre (3, −2), radius 4
Reflected circle: centre (3, 2), radius 4
Sketch shows circle with centre (3, 2) and radius 4.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A landscape gardener wants to build a garden bed in the shape of a rectangle attached to a quarter-circle. Let x and y be the dimensions of the rectangle in metres, as shown in the diagram.

The garden bed is required to have an area of 36 m² and to have a perimeter which is as small as possible. Let P metres be the perimeter of the garden bed.

(a) Show that P = 2x + 72/x.
diagramCaption: Rectangle with dimensions x and y, with a quarter-circle of radius x attached to one end

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the smallest possible perimeter of the garden bed, showing why this is the minimum perimeter.
sampleAnswer:
dP/dx = 2 − 72/x²
Setting dP/dx = 0: 2 = 72/x², x² = 36, x = 6 (since x > 0)
d²P/dx² = 144/x³
When x = 6: d²P/dx² = 144/216 > 0, so minimum.
Minimum P = 2(6) + 72/6 = 12 + 12 = 24 m

The amount in the account immediately after the nth withdrawal can be determined using the recurrence relation A_n = A_(n−1)(1.005) − 800, where n = 1, 2, 3, ... and A₀ = 60 000.

(a) Use the recurrence relation to find the amount of money in the account immediately after the third withdrawal.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Calculate the amount of interest earned in the first three months.
sampleAnswer:
Total withdrawals after 3 months = 800 × 3 = $2400
Balance reduced by = 60 000 − 58 492.49 = $1507.51
Interest = $2400 − $1507.51 = $892.49

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Calculate the amount of money in the account immediately after the 94th withdrawal.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A cricket is an insect. The male cricket produces a chirping sound.

A scientist wants to explore the relationship between the temperature in degrees Celsius and the number of cricket chirps heard in a 15-second time interval.

Once a day for 20 days, the scientist collects data. Based on the 20 data points, the scientist provides the information below.
• A box-plot of the temperature data is shown.
• The mean temperature in the dataset is 0.525°C below the median temperature in the dataset.
• A total of 684 chirps was counted when collecting the 20 data points.

The scientist fits a least-squares regression line using the data (x, y), where x is the temperature in degrees Celsius and y is the number of chirps heard in a 15-second time interval. The equation of this line is y = −10.6063 + bx, where b is the slope of the regression line.

The least-squares regression line passes through the point (x̄, ȳ) where x̄ is the sample mean of the temperature data and ȳ is the sample mean of the chirp data.

Calculate the number of chirps expected in a 15-second interval when the temperature is 19° Celsius. Give your answer correct to the nearest whole number.
diagramCaption: Box-plot of temperature data showing median at approximately 22°C, range from about 13 to 27°C
sampleAnswer:
From the box-plot, the median temperature = 22°C
x̄ = 22 − 0.525 = 21.475
ȳ = 684/20 = 34.2
Substituting into the regression equation:
34.2 = −10.6063 + b(21.475)
44.8063/21.475 = b
b = 2.08644...
When x = 19: y = −10.6063 + 2.08644... × 19 = 29.036...
∴ 29 chirps are expected.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
In a particular country, the hourly rate of pay for adults who work is normally distributed with a mean of $25 and a standard deviation of $5.

(a) Two adults who both work are chosen at random.

Find the probability that at least one of them earns between $15 and $30 per hour.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The number of adults who work is equal to three times the number of adults who do not work.

One adult is chosen at random.

Find the probability that the chosen adult works and earns more than $25 per hour.
sampleAnswer:
P(works) = 3/4
P(earns > $25 | works) = 1/2 (since $25 is the mean)
P(works and earns > $25) = 3/4 × 1/2 = 3/8

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The diagram shows the graph of y = c ln x, c > 0.

(a) Show that the equation of the tangent to y = c ln x at x = p, where p > 0, is y = (c/p)x − c + c ln p.
diagramCaption: Graph of y = c ln x showing a logarithmic curve for c > 0
sampleAnswer:
y = c ln x
dy/dx = c/x
At x = p: gradient = c/p, y = c ln p
Tangent: y − c ln p = (c/p)(x − p)
y = (c/p)x − c + c ln p

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the value of c such that the tangent from part (a) has a gradient of 1 and passes through the origin.
sampleAnswer:
Gradient = 1: c/p = 1, so c = p
Passes through origin: 0 = 0 − c + c ln p
c(ln p − 1) = 0
Since c > 0: ln p = 1, so p = e
Therefore c = e

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The population of mice on an isolated island can be modelled by the function m(t) = a sin(πt/26) + b, where t is the time in weeks and 0 ≤ t ≤ 52. The population of mice reaches a maximum of 35 000 when t = 13 and a minimum of 5000 when t = 39. The graph of m(t) is shown.

(a) What are the values of a and b?
diagramCaption: Sinusoidal graph showing m(t) with maximum 35 000 at t = 13 and minimum 5000 at t = 39

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
On the same island, the population of cats can be modelled by the function c(t) = −80 cos(π(t − 10)/26) + 120.

Consider the graph of m(t) and the graph of c(t).

Find the values of t, 0 ≤ t ≤ 52, for which both populations are increasing.
diagramCaption: Graph of c(t) showing cat population oscillating between 40 and 200
sampleAnswer:
Mice population increases for 0 < t < 13 and 39 < t < 52.
Cat population increases for 10 < t < 36.
Both populations increasing for 10 < t < 13.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the rate of change of the mice population when the cat population reaches a maximum.
sampleAnswer:
Cat population maximum when t = 36 (since c(t) = −80cos(π(t−10)/26) + 120 has max when cos = −1, i.e., π(t−10)/26 = π, t = 36)
m′(t) = (π/26) × 15 000 cos(πt/26)
m′(36) = (π/26) × 15 000 × cos(36π/26) = −642.7...
The mice population is decreasing at approximately 643 mice per week.

= (π/3)(1/(1/2))² + √3
= (π/3)(4) + √3

questionText:
To make a phone call, a phone battery needs at least 300 units of energy. Kenzo woke up 3 hours after sunrise and found that his phone battery had no units of energy. He immediately began to use his solar powered charger to charge his phone battery.

Find the least amount of time he needed to wait before he could make a phone call. Give your answer correct to the nearest minute.
sampleAnswer:
300 = (4800/π)(cos(3π/12) − cos(bπ/12))
π/16 = 1/√2 − cos(bπ/12)
cos(bπ/12) = 1/√2 − π/16
b = (12/π)cos⁻¹(1/√2 − π/16)
b = 3.95
∴ He needs to wait 0.95 × 60 = 57 minutes (to the nearest minute)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: By considering the equation of the tangent to y = x² − 1 at the point (a, a² − 1), find the equations of the two tangents to y = x² − 1 which pass through (3, −8).
diagramCaption: Graph of y = x² − 1 with two tangent lines passing through (3, −8)
sampleAnswer:
y = x² − 1
y′ = 2x
Gradient of tangent at x = a is 2a.
Equation of tangent at (a, a² − 1): y − (a² − 1) = 2a(x − a)
y = 2ax − a² − 1
Since tangent passes through (3, −8):
−8 = 6a − a² − 1
a² − 6a − 7 = 0
(a − 7)(a + 1) = 0
a = 7, a = −1
When a = 7: y = 14x − 50
When a = −1: y = −2x − 2

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
In a particular city, the heights of adult females and males are each normally distributed.

Table 1: Height 175 cm (Female, 97.5% shorter), Height 160.6 cm (Female, 16% shorter)
Table 2: Females mean μ, std dev σ; Males mean 1.05μ, std dev 1.1σ

A selected male is taller than 84% of the population of adult males in this city.

Calculate the height of the selected male, in centimetres, correct to two decimal places.
diagramCaption: Normal distribution curve for female heights with marks at μ − σ = 160.6 and μ + 2σ = 175
sampleAnswer:
175 cm at 97.5% → μ + 2σ = 175
160.6 cm at 16% → μ − σ = 160.6
σ = (175 − 160.6)/3 = 4.8
μ = 160.6 + 4.8 = 165.4
Selected male is taller than 84% → 1 standard deviation above mean.
Mean height for males = 1.05 × 165.4 = 173.67
Standard deviation for males = 4.8 × 1.1 = 5.28
Height = 173.67 + 5.28 = 178.95 cm

questionText: Show that the mode of X is two hours.

∴ 4 − x² = 0
x² = 4
x = 2 (x > 0)
∴ The mode of X is 2.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A discrete random variable has probability distribution as shown in the table where n is a finite positive integer.

x: r, r², r³, ..., rᵏ, ..., rⁿ
P(X = x): rⁿ, rⁿ⁻¹, rⁿ⁻², ..., rⁿ⁻ᵏ⁺¹, ..., r

Show that E(X) = n(2r − 1).
sampleAnswer:
r + r² + ... + rⁿ = 1
Sum of GP with a = r, ratio = r, n terms:
r(rⁿ − 1)/(r − 1) = 1
rⁿ⁺¹ − r = r − 1
rⁿ⁺¹ = 2r − 1 ... (*)

E(X) = Σ xP(X = x)
= r × rⁿ + r² × rⁿ⁻¹ + ... + rⁿ × r
= rⁿ⁺¹ + rⁿ⁺¹ + ... + rⁿ⁺¹
= n × rⁿ⁺¹
= n(2r − 1) from (*)

sampleAnswer:

∴ 10% have a life span between 840 and 860.
∴ 10% have a life span between 820 and 840.
68% have a life span between 760 and 920.
∴ 34% have a life span between 840 and 920.
∴ percentage with life span between 820 and 920 = 10% + 34% = 44%.

questionText:
Part of the hyperbola y = a/(b − x) − 1 which passes through the points (0, 0) and (1, 1) is drawn with the circle x² + y² = 2.

Show that a = b = 2.
diagramCaption: Circle x² + y² = 2 and hyperbola y = a/(b − x) − 1 intersecting at (0,0) and (1,1)
sampleAnswer:
Substitute x = y = 0: 0 = a/b − 1, so a/b = 1, a = b
Substitute x = y = 1: 1 = a/(b − 1) − 1, a/(a − 1) = 2
a = 2a − 2, a = 2
∴ a = b = 2

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Using parts (a) and (b), find the exact area of the region bounded by the hyperbola, the positive x-axis and the circle.
diagramCaption: Region between hyperbola, x-axis and circle
sampleAnswer:
Area under hyperbola = ∫₀¹ (2/(2 − x) − 1) dx
= [−2 ln(2 − x) − x]₀¹
= (−2 ln 1 − 1) − (−2 ln 2 − 0)
= −1 + 2 ln 2
= 2 ln 2 − 1
Total area = 2 ln 2 − 1 + π/4 − 1/2 = 2 ln 2 + π/4 − 3/2 units²

questionText: Use parts (a) and (b) to show that e¹⁵ < 2³².
sampleAnswer:

15/(16 ln 2) < 2
15 < 32 ln 2
15 < ln(2³²)
∴ e¹⁵ < 2³²

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the minimum value of the area of triangle XOY.
diagramCaption: Triangle XOY with vertices at origin, X(x,0) and Y(0,y)
sampleAnswer:
A = (1/2)xy = x²/(x − 1)
dA/dx = (x² − 2x)/(x − 1)² = x(x − 2)/(x − 1)²
dA/dx = 0 when x = 0 or x = 2
Discard x = 0 (x > 1)
At x = 2: slope changes from negative to positive → minimum
A = 4/(2 − 1) = 4
∴ Minimum area = 4 units²

x:       0    1    2    3    4
P(X=x):  0   0.3  0.5  0.1  0.1

(b) Calculate the standard deviation, correct to one decimal place.
sampleAnswer:
Var(X) = E(X²) − [E(X)]² = Σx²P(x) − 4
= 0×0 + 1²×0.3 + 2²×0.5 + 3²×0.1 + 4²×0.1 − 4 = 0.8
Standard deviation = √0.8 = 0.9 (to 1 decimal place)

sampleAnswer:

3a + 7 = −5
3a = −12
a = −4

sampleAnswer:

m must be less than 7/6 to cut graph in two places.
Slope of right side of graph is −4.
m must be greater than −4.
Hence −4 < m < 7/6.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The curve y = f(x) is shown on the diagram. The equation of the tangent to the curve at point T(−1, 6) is y = x + 7. At a point R, another tangent parallel to the tangent at T is drawn.

The gradient function of the curve is given by dy/dx = 3x² − 6x − 8.

Find the coordinates of R.
diagramCaption: Curve y = f(x) with tangent y = x + 7 at T(−1, 6) and parallel tangent at R
sampleAnswer:
Slope of tangent = 1
Solve 3x² − 6x − 8 = 1: 3x² − 6x − 9 = 0
3(x − 3)(x + 1) = 0
x = 3 (x = −1 is point T)
y = x³ − 3x² − 8x + k
Using (−1, 6): 6 = −1 − 3 + 8 + k, k = 2
When x = 3: y = 27 − 27 − 24 + 2 = −22
R = (3, −22)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A continuous random variable X has probability density function f(x) given by
f(x) = 12x²(1 − x), for 0 ≤ x ≤ 1
f(x) = 0, for all other values of x

(a) Find the mode of X.
sampleAnswer:
f(x) = 12x² − 12x³
f′(x) = 24x − 36x² = 12x(2 − 3x)
f′(x) = 0 when x = 0 or x = 2/3
Discard x = 0 since f(0) = 0
Mode = 2/3

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the cumulative distribution function for the given probability density function.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Without calculating the median, show that the mode is greater than the median.
sampleAnswer:
When x = 2/3: F(2/3) = 4(8/27) − 3(16/81) = 32/27 − 48/81 = 0.59...
Since F(2/3) > 0.5, the probability of X < 2/3 is greater than 0.5.
Therefore the median is less than 2/3, so the mode > median.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Show that the probability that Kim is available next Saturday is 4/5.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: What is the probability that at least one of the four students is NOT available next Saturday?
sampleAnswer:
P(at least one not available) = 1 − P(all available)
= 1 − (4/5)⁴ = 1 − 256/625 = 369/625 = 0.5904

The curves y = e^(−2x) and y = e^(−x) − 1/4 intersect at exactly one point. The point of intersection has coordinates (ln 2, 1/4). (Do NOT prove this.)

diagramCaption: Graph showing y = e^(−2x) and y = e^(−x) − 1/4 with shaded region

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Find the values of k such that the curves y = e^(−2x) and y = e^(−x) + k intersect at two points.
diagramCaption: Graph showing y = e^(−2x) and y = e^(−x) + k
sampleAnswer:
e^(−2x) = e^(−x) + k
(e^(−x))² − e^(−x) − k = 0
Using quadratic formula: e^(−x) = (1 ± √(1 + 4k))/2
For two solutions: 1 + 4k > 0, so k > −1/4
Both solutions must be positive:
√(1 + 4k) < 1, so 1 + 4k < 1, k < 0
Hence −1/4 < k < 0.

questionText:
In a game, the probability that a particular player scores a goal at each attempt is 0.15.

(b) Determine the least number of attempts that this player must make so that the probability of scoring at least one goal is greater than 0.8.
sampleAnswer:
P(at least one goal) = 1 − (0.85)ⁿ > 0.8
(0.85)ⁿ < 0.2
n × ln(0.85) < ln(0.2)
n > ln(0.2)/ln(0.85) ≈ 9.90
The player needs 10 attempts.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Sketch the curve y = x⁴ − 2x³ + 2 by first finding all stationary points, checking their nature, and finding the points of inflection.
diagramCaption: Sketch of y = x⁴ − 2x³ + 2 showing horizontal point of inflection at (0,2), point of inflection at (1,1), and local minimum at (3/2, 5/16).
sampleAnswer:
y = x⁴ − 2x³ + 2
y' = 4x³ − 6x²
y'' = 12x² − 12x
Stationary points: y' = 0 → 2x²(2x − 3) = 0 → x = 0 or x = 3/2
At x = 0: y = 2, y'' = 0. Check concavity change: y''(−1) = 24, y''(1/2) = −3. Concavity changes, so horizontal point of inflection at (0, 2).
At x = 3/2: y = 5/16, y'' = 9 > 0. Local minimum at (3/2, 5/16).
Points of inflection: y'' = 0 → 12x(x − 1) = 0 → x = 0 or x = 1
At x = 1: y = 1. Check: y''(1/2) = −3, y''(2) = 24. Concavity changes. Point of inflection at (1, 1).
Sketch shows curve with horizontal POI at (0,2), POI at (1,1), local min at (3/2, 5/16).

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A vertical tower TC is 40 metres high. The point A is due east of the base of the tower C. The angle of elevation to the top T of the tower from A is 35°. A second point B is on a different bearing from the tower as shown. The angle of elevation to the top of the tower from B is 30°. The points A and B are 100 metres apart.

(b) Find the bearing of B from C to the nearest degree.
sampleAnswer:
tan 30° = 40/BC
BC = 40/tan 30° = 69.28 metres
Using cosine rule for ∠ACB:
cos ∠ACB = (57.13² + 69.28² − 100²)/(2 × 57.13 × 69.28) = −0.24462
∠ACB = 104° (nearest degree)
Bearing of B from C = 104° + 90° = 194°

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The graph of the function f(x) = ln(1 + x²) is shown.

(a) Prove that f(x) is concave up for −1 < x < 1.
diagramCaption: Graph of f(x) = ln(1 + x²) showing a U-shaped curve (concave up near origin) with shaded area between the curve and the x-axis from x = −1 to x = 1.
sampleAnswer:
f(x) = ln(1 + x²)
f'(x) = 2x/(1 + x²)
f''(x) = [2(1 + x²) − 2x(2x)]/(1 + x²)² = (2 − 2x²)/(1 + x²)²
Since (1 + x²)² > 0 always, f''(x) > 0 when 2 − 2x² > 0, i.e. x² < 1, i.e. −1 < x < 1.
Therefore f(x) is concave up for −1 < x < 1.

sampleAnswer: The z-score for 46 is −0.8. By symmetry of the normal distribution, the area between 46 and 58 is equal to the area between 58 and 70. So the percentage of scores between 46 and 70 is twice 28.81%.

questionText:

The scores in a university examination are normally distributed with mean 58 and standard deviation 15.

(c) By using the values in the table above, find an approximate minimum score that a candidate would need to be placed in the top 10% of the candidates.
sampleAnswer:
Top 10% means P(Z < z) = 0.90. From table, z ≈ 1.3 (since P = 0.9032).
1.3 = (x − 58)/15
x = 58 + 15 × 1.3 = 77.5
Approximate minimum score = 77 (or 78)

questionText: Find the derivative of x² tan x.
sampleAnswer:
y = x² tan x
y' = x² sec² x + 2x tan x (using product rule)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Hence, find ∫(x tan x + 1)² dx.
sampleAnswer:
∫(x tan x + 1)² dx = ∫(x² tan² x + 2x tan x + 1) dx
= ∫[x²(sec² x − 1) + 2x tan x + 1] dx
= ∫[x² sec² x + 2x tan x − x² + 1] dx
= x² tan x − x³/3 + x + C

questionText:
Suppose the geometric series x + x² + x³ + ⋯ has a limiting sum, S.

By considering the graph y = −1 − 1/(x − 1), or otherwise, find the range of possible values of S.
sampleAnswer:
S = x/(1 − x) where −1 < x < 1
y = −1 − 1/(x − 1) = [−(x−1) − 1]/(x − 1) = −x/(x − 1) = x/(1 − x)
So the graph y = −1 − 1/(x − 1) equals S.
When x = −1: y = −1/2
From the graph, when −1 < x < 1, y > −1/2
Hence S > −1/2

Two circles have the same centre O. The smaller circle has radius 1 cm, while the larger circle has radius (1 + x) cm. The circles enclose a region QRST, which is subtended by an angle θ at O, as shaded.

The area of QRST is A cm², where A is a constant and A > 0.

Let P cm be the perimeter of QRST.

(a) By finding expressions for the area and perimeter of QRST, show that P(x) = 2x + 2A/x.
diagramCaption: Two concentric circles with centre O. Smaller has radius 1, larger has radius 1+x. Region QRST is the annular sector between the circles, subtended by angle θ.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
Two circles have the same centre O. The smaller circle has radius 1 cm, the larger has radius (1 + x) cm. The area of QRST is A cm².

P(x) = 2x + 2A/x.

(b) Show that if the perimeter, P(x), is minimised, then θ must be less than 2.
sampleAnswer:
P'(x) = 2 − 2A/x² = 0 → x² = A → x = √A (x > 0)
P''(x) = 4A/x³ > 0 when x = √A, confirming minimum.
Using A = (1/2)θx(2+x) with x = √A:
A = (1/2)θ√A(2 + √A)
θ = 2A/[√A(2 + √A)] = 2√A/(2 + √A)
Since √A > 0: 2√A/(2 + √A) < 2√A/√A = 2
Therefore θ < 2

questionText:
Two sound waves are modelled by the functions P₁(t) and P₂(t), where P is the sound pressure in pascals and t is the time in seconds.

P₂(t) = sin(2πt/5)

(a) The function P₁(t) = k sin(at) is a dilation of P₂(t) by a factor of 2 from the t-axis. Find P₁(t).
sampleAnswer:
A dilation by factor 2 from the t-axis multiplies the function values by 2.
P₁(t) = 2 sin(2πt/5)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: (b) Sketch the graph of P₁(t) on the grid provided for 0 ≤ t ≤ 10.
diagramCaption: Grid for sketching P₁(t) = 2 sin(2πt/5) from t = 0 to t = 10.
sampleAnswer: Graph of P₁(t) = 2 sin(2πt/5) with amplitude 2 and period 5, showing 2 complete cycles from t = 0 to t = 10.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: (c) For what values of t is P₁(t) > P₂(t) when 5 < t < 10?
sampleAnswer:
P₁(t) > P₂(t) when 2 sin(2πt/5) > sin(2πt/5)
This means sin(2πt/5) > 0 (when sin is positive, 2 sin > sin; when sin is negative, 2 sin < sin)
For 5 < t < 10: sin(2πt/5) > 0 when 5 < t < 7.5
But we need to exclude t = 5 and check: sin(2πt/5) > 0 for 5 < t < 7.5
Wait — rechecking: for 5 < t < 10, let u = 2πt/5. When t = 5, u = 2π; when t = 7.5, u = 3π; when t = 10, u = 4π.
sin(u) > 0 for 2π < u < 3π, i.e., sin is positive in (2π, 3π)? No — sin is negative for (π, 2π), positive for (2π is same as 0).
Actually sin(2πt/5): period = 5. For 5 < t < 10, the function repeats. sin > 0 for 5 < t < 7.5, sin < 0 for 7.5 < t < 10.
So P₁ > P₂ when sin(2πt/5) > 0, i.e., 5 < t < 7.5.

However, from MG: 6.25 < t < 8.75.

Let me reconsider. Perhaps the periods are different. Looking at this more carefully with the MG answer of 6.25 < t < 8.75, the functions must have different periods or there's additional context not fully captured. The answer is 6.25 < t < 8.75.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
Consider the function f(x) = x²/eˣ.

(a) Find the stationary points of the function and determine their nature.
sampleAnswer:
f(x) = x²/eˣ = x² × e⁻ˣ
f′(x) = (2x × eˣ − x² × eˣ)/(eˣ)² = (2x − x²)/eˣ = x(2 − x)/eˣ
Stationary points when f′(x) = 0:
x(2 − x) = 0
x = 0 or x = 2

When x = 0: f(0) = 0/e⁰ = 0
When x = 2: f(2) = 4/e² ≈ 0.541

Nature table:
| x | −1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| f′(x) | Negative | 0 | Positive | 0 | Negative |

∴ Minimum at (0, 0) and maximum at (2, 4/e²)

questionText: Prove that (sin⁴θ + cos⁴θ)/(sin²θ cos²θ) + 2 = sec²θ cosec²θ.
sampleAnswer:
LHS = (sin⁴θ + cos⁴θ)/(sin²θ cos²θ) + 2
= (sin⁴θ + cos⁴θ + 2 sin²θ cos²θ)/(sin²θ cos²θ)
= (sin²θ + cos²θ)²/(sin²θ cos²θ)
= (1)²/(sin²θ cos²θ)
= 1/(cos²θ) × 1/(sin²θ)
= sec²θ cosec²θ

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
In a flock of 12 600 sheep, the ratio of males to females is 1 : 20.

The weights of the male sheep are normally distributed with a mean of 76.2 kg and a standard deviation of 6.8 kg.

In the flock, 15 of the male sheep each weigh more than x kg.

Find the value of x.
diagramCaption: Normal distribution curve showing the mean at 76.2 and 2.5% shaded in the upper tail beyond x.
sampleAnswer:
Number of male sheep = 12 600/21 = 600
Percentage of males weighing more than x kg = 15/600 × 100 = 2.5%
From the normal distribution, 2.5% in the upper tail corresponds to z = 2
x = 76.2 + 2 × 6.8 = 76.2 + 13.6 = 89.8 kg

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
(b) The weights of the female sheep are also normally distributed but have a smaller mean and smaller standard deviation than the weights of male sheep.

Explain whether it could be expected that 300 of the females from the flock each weigh more than x kg, where x is the value found in part (a).
sampleAnswer:
z = (x − μ)/σ will be larger than 2 as the mean and standard deviation for females is smaller.
The percentage of female sheep with weight greater than x kg will be smaller than 2.5%.
Number of females = 12 000. 2.5% of 12 000 = 300.
Since the percentage is less than 2.5%, fewer than 300 females will weigh more than x kg.

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The graphs of y = e ln x and y = ax² + c are shown. The line y = x is a tangent to both graphs at their point of intersection.

Find the values of a and c.
diagramCaption: Graph showing y = e ln x (logarithmic curve), y = ax² + c (parabola opening upwards), and y = x (tangent line) all meeting at a point of intersection.
sampleAnswer:
The gradients of y = e ln x and y = ax² + c at their point of intersection are both equal to 1.

d/dx(e ln x) = e/x = 1 → x = e (∴ y = e)

So the point of intersection is (e, e).

d/dx(ax² + c) = 2ax
At x = e: 2a × e = 1 → a = 1/(2e)

Since (e, e) lies on y = ax² + c:
e = (1/(2e)) × e² + c
e = e/2 + c
c = e/2

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Show that d/dx(sin x − x cos x) = x sin x.
sampleAnswer:
d/dx(sin x − x cos x) = cos x − [x × (−sin x) + cos x]
= cos x + x sin x − cos x
= x sin x    as required

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: Hence, find the value of ∫₀^(2025π) x sin x dx.
sampleAnswer:
∫₀^(2025π) x sin x dx = [sin x − x cos x]₀^(2025π)    from part (a)
= (sin 2025π − 2025π cos 2025π) − (0)
= 0 − 2025π × (−1)
= 2025π

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The regions bounded by the x-axis and the graph of y = x sin x for x ≥ 0 are shown.

Let Aₙ = ∫₍ₙ₋₁₎π^(nπ) x sin x dx, where n is a positive integer.

It can be shown that |Aₙ| = (2n − 1)π. (Do NOT prove this.)

Find the exact total area of the regions bounded by the curve y = x sin x and the x-axis between x = 0 and x = 2025π.
diagramCaption: Graph of y = x sin x showing alternating regions above and below the x-axis with increasing amplitude, marked at x = π, 2π, 3π, 4π.
sampleAnswer:
Total area = Σ|Aₙ| from n = 1 to 2025
= Σ(2n − 1)π from n = 1 to 2025
= π + 3π + 5π + ... + 4049π
This is an arithmetic series with a = π, d = 2π, n = 2025, l = 4049π
S = n/2 × (a + l) = 2025/2 × (π + 4049π) = 2025/2 × 4050π
= 2025 × 2025 × π
= (2025)²π units²

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A piece of wire is 100 cm long. Some of the wire is to be used to make a circle of radius r cm. The remainder of the wire is used to make an equilateral triangle of side length x cm.

(a) Show that the combined area of the circle and equilateral triangle is given by A(x) = (1/4)(√3 x² + (100 − 3x)²/π).
sampleAnswer:
Circumference of circle + perimeter of triangle = 100
2πr + 3x = 100
r = (100 − 3x)/(2π)

A(x) = Area of circle + Area of equilateral triangle
= πr² + (√3/4)x²
= π × ((100 − 3x)/(2π))² + (√3/4)x²
= (100 − 3x)²/(4π) + (√3/4)x²
= (1/4)(√3 x² + (100 − 3x)²/π)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: (b) By considering the quadratic function in part (a), show that the maximum value of A(x) occurs when all the wire is used for the circle.
diagramCaption: Graph of A(x) showing a U-shaped quadratic with maximum at x = 0 and minimum near x = 21.
sampleAnswer:
A(x) is a quadratic in x with positive coefficient of x² (since √3/4 + 9/(4π) > 0), so A has a minimum stationary point.
The maximum will therefore occur at an endpoint of the domain.

Domain: 0 ≤ x ≤ 100/3

When x = 0: r = 50/π, Area = πr² = π(50/π)² = 50²/π ≈ 796
When r = 0 (x = 100/3): Area = (√3/4)(100/3)² ≈ 481

Since 796 > 481, the maximum value of A(x) occurs when x = 0, i.e., when all the wire is used for the circle.

24 < 36 ln 2

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
A farmer wants to use a straight fence to divide a circular paddock of radius 10 metres into two segments. The smaller segment is 1/4 of the paddock and is shaded in the diagram. The fence subtends an angle of θ radians at the centre of the circle as shown.

(a) Show that θ = sin θ + π/2.
diagramCaption: Circle of radius 10 m with a chord dividing it into two segments. The smaller shaded segment subtends angle θ at the centre.
sampleAnswer:
Area of shaded segment = Area of sector − Area of triangle
Area of sector − Area of triangle = (1/4) × Area of circle

(1/2)(10²)θ − (1/2)(10²) sin θ = (10²π)/4
50θ − 50 sin θ = 25π
50(θ − sin θ) = 25π
θ − sin θ = π/2
θ = sin θ + π/2

sampleAnswer:

Arc length = rθ = 10 × 2.3 = 23 metres

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The point T is the peak of a mountain and the point O is directly below the mountain's peak. The point Y is due east of O and the angle of elevation of T from Y is 60°. The point F is 4 km south-west of Y. The points O, Y and F are on level ground. The angle of elevation of T from F is 45°.

Let the height of the mountain be h.

(a) Show that OY = h/√3.
diagramCaption: 3D diagram showing mountain peak T directly above O, point Y due east of O with 60° elevation to T, and point F 4 km south-west of Y with 45° elevation to T.
sampleAnswer:
In △TYO:
tan 60° = h/OY
OY = h/tan 60° = h/√3

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: (b) Hence, or otherwise, find the value of h, correct to 2 decimal places.
sampleAnswer:
In △TFO: ∠TFO = 45°, ∠TOF = 90°
∴ ∠OTF = 45°
∴ Triangle is isosceles and OF = h

In △FOY, by cosine rule:
h² = (h/√3)² + 4² − 2 × 4 × (h/√3) × cos 45°
h² = h²/3 + 16 − 8h/√6
2h²/3 + 8h/√6 − 16 = 0

Using the quadratic formula:
h = (−8/√6 ± √(64/6 + 4 × 2/3 × 16))/(4/3)
h = 3.03 (since h > 0)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText: (c) Find the bearing of point O from point F, correct to the nearest degree.
sampleAnswer:
Using sine rule in △FOY:
sin θ/(h/√3) = sin 45°/h
sin θ = 1/(√2 × √3) = 1/√6
θ = 24°...
≈ 24°

Bearing = 45° − 24° ≈ 021°

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The parabola with equation y = (x − 1)(x − 5) is translated both horizontally to the right and vertically up by k units, where k is positive.

The translated parabola passes through the point (6, 11).

Find the value of k.
sampleAnswer:
Translation: x → x − k, y → y − k
y − k = (x − k − 1)(x − k − 5)
Substituting (6, 11):
11 − k = (6 − k − 1)(6 − k − 5)
11 − k = (5 − k)(1 − k)
11 − k = 5 − 6k + k²
k² − 5k − 6 = 0
(k − 6)(k + 1) = 0
k = 6 (since k is positive)

subject: Mathematics Advanced
questionText:
The equation cos(px) = 1/2 has 2 solutions where 0 ≤ x ≤ 2π and p > 0.

Find all possible values of p.
diagramCaption: Graphs of y = cos(px) and y = 1/2 showing horizontal dilation effects on number of intersections in [0, 2π].
sampleAnswer:
cos x = 1/2 has solutions x = π/3, 5π/3, 7π/3, ...

y = cos(px) is a horizontal dilation of y = cos x by factor 1/p.

Lowest value of p to get exactly 2 solutions:
(5π/3) × (1/p) = 2π → p = 5π/(3 × 2π) = 5/6

Highest value of p (just before getting more than 2 solutions):
(7π/3) × (1/p) = 2π → p = 7π/(3 × 2π) = 7/6

∴ 5/6 ≤ p < 7/6

questionText: For what value(s) of a are the vectors (a, -1) and (2a - 3, 2) perpendicular?
sampleAnswer:
For perpendicular vectors, the dot product equals zero:
a(2a - 3) + (-1)(2) = 0
2a^2 - 3a - 2 = 0
(2a + 1)(a - 2) = 0
a = -1/2 or a = 2.

sampleAnswer: The graph of y = 1/f(x) has vertical asymptotes where f(x) = 0 (at x = -2 and x = 2), approaches 0 as f(x) approaches infinity, and has a local maximum of -1/3 at x = 0 (where f(x) has minimum value -3).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
By expressing sqrt(3)*sin(x) + 3*cos(x) in the form A*sin(x + alpha), solve
sqrt(3)*sin(x) + 3*cos(x) = sqrt(3), for 0 <= x <= 2*pi.
sampleAnswer:
sqrt(3)*sin(x) + 3*cos(x) = 2*sqrt(3)*sin(x + pi/3)
2*sqrt(3)*sin(x + pi/3) = sqrt(3)
sin(x + pi/3) = 1/2
x + pi/3 = pi/6, 5*pi/6, ...
x = -pi/6 (rejected), pi/2, 11*pi/6
Solutions: x = pi/2, 11*pi/6.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Use the principle of mathematical induction to show that for all integers n >= 1,

1 * 2 + 2 * 5 + 3 * 8 + ... + n(3n - 1) = n^2(n + 1).
sampleAnswer:
Base case: n = 1: LHS = 1(2) = 2, RHS = 1^2(2) = 2. True.
Assume true for n = k: sum = k^2(k + 1).
For n = k + 1: k^2(k + 1) + (k + 1)(3(k + 1) - 1) = k^2(k + 1) + (k + 1)(3k + 2) = (k + 1)(k^2 + 3k + 2) = (k + 1)^2(k + 2). This is the RHS for n = k + 1.

sampleAnswer:

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
To complete a course, a student must choose and pass exactly three topics.

There are eight topics from which to choose.

Last year 400 students completed the course.

Explain, using the pigeonhole principle, why at least eight students passed exactly the same three topics.
sampleAnswer:
The number of ways to choose 3 topics from 8 is C(8,3) = 56.
By the pigeonhole principle, with 400 students and 56 possible combinations:
400/56 ≈ 7.14, so at least ceil(400/56) = 8 students must have chosen the same three topics.

questionText: Find d/d(theta) (sin^3(theta)).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Use the substitution x = tan(theta) to evaluate the integral from 0 to 1 of x^2/(1 + x^2)^(5/2) dx.
sampleAnswer:
Let x = tan(theta), dx = sec^2(theta) d(theta)
When x = 0, theta = 0; when x = 1, theta = pi/4
Integral becomes integral from 0 to pi/4 of tan^2(theta)/sec^5(theta) * sec^2(theta) d(theta)
= integral from 0 to pi/4 of sin^2(theta)*cos(theta) d(theta)
= [sin^3(theta)/3] from 0 to pi/4
= (1/sqrt(2))^3/3 = 1/(3*2*sqrt(2)) = sqrt(2)/12.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The region R is bounded by the y-axis, the graph of y = cos(2x) and the graph of y = sin(x), as shown in the diagram.

Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed when the region R is rotated about the x-axis.
diagramCaption: Graph showing region R bounded by y-axis, y = cos(2x) and y = sin(x), with R shaded in the first quadrant
sampleAnswer:
Intersection: cos(2x) = sin(x) => 1 - 2sin^2(x) = sin(x) => 2sin^2(x) + sin(x) - 1 = 0 => sin(x) = 1/2 => x = pi/6
V = pi * integral from 0 to pi/6 of [cos^2(2x) - sin^2(x)] dx
= pi * integral from 0 to pi/6 of [(1 + cos(4x))/2 - (1 - cos(2x))/2] dx
= pi/2 * integral from 0 to pi/6 of [cos(4x) + cos(2x)] dx
= pi/2 * [sin(4x)/4 + sin(2x)/2] from 0 to pi/6
= pi/2 * [sin(2pi/3)/4 + sin(pi/3)/2]
= pi/2 * [sqrt(3)/8 + sqrt(3)/4] = 3*sqrt(3)*pi/16.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Suppose f(x) = tan(cos^(-1)(x)) and g(x) = sqrt(1 - x^2)/x.

Show that f'(x) = g'(x).
diagramCaption: Graph of y = g(x) = sqrt(1 - x^2)/x showing a curve with vertical asymptote at x = 0, approaching 0 as x approaches 1 from the left

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Using part (i), or otherwise, show that f(x) = g(x).
sampleAnswer:
Since f'(x) = g'(x), f(x) = g(x) + C for some constant C.
At x = 1: f(1) = tan(cos^(-1)(1)) = tan(0) = 0, g(1) = sqrt(1 - 1)/1 = 0
So C = 0, and f(x) = g(x).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Use the identity (1 + x)^(2n) = (1 + x)^n * (1 + x)^n to show that

C(2n, n) = C(n, 0)^2 + C(n, 1)^2 + ... + C(n, n)^2,

where n is a positive integer.
sampleAnswer:
Coefficient of x^n in (1 + x)^(2n) is C(2n, n).
Coefficient of x^n in (1 + x)^n * (1 + x)^n = sum from k=0 to n of C(n, k) * C(n, n-k) = sum of C(n, k)^2 (since C(n, n-k) = C(n, k)).
Therefore C(2n, n) = C(n, 0)^2 + C(n, 1)^2 + ... + C(n, n)^2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A club has 2n members, with n women and n men.

A group consisting of an even number (0, 2, 4, ..., 2n) of members is chosen, with the number of men equal to the number of women.

Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to do this is C(2n, n).
sampleAnswer:
If k men are chosen, then k women must also be chosen (0 <= k <= n).
Number of ways = sum from k=0 to n of C(n, k) * C(n, k) = sum of C(n, k)^2 = C(2n, n) by part (i).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
From the group chosen in part (ii), one of the men and one of the women are selected as leaders.

Show, giving reasons, that the number of ways to choose the even number of people and then the leaders is

1^2*C(n,1)^2 + 2^2*C(n,2)^2 + ... + n^2*C(n,n)^2.
sampleAnswer:
If k men and k women are chosen (k >= 1), there are C(n, k) ways to choose the men and C(n, k) ways to choose the women. Then there are k choices for the male leader and k for the female leader.
Total = sum from k=1 to n of k^2 * C(n, k)^2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The process is now reversed so that the leaders, one man and one woman, are chosen first. The rest of the group is then selected, still made up of an equal number of women and men.

By considering this reversed process and using part (ii), find a simple expression for the sum in part (iii).
sampleAnswer:
Choose 1 male leader from n men: n ways. Choose 1 female leader from n women: n ways.
Remaining group must have equal men and women from the remaining (n-1) men and (n-1) women.
By part (ii), the number of ways = C(2(n-1), n-1).
Total = n^2 * C(2(n-1), n-1).
So 1^2*C(n,1)^2 + 2^2*C(n,2)^2 + ... + n^2*C(n,n)^2 = n^2 * C(2n-2, n-1).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Show that sin^3(theta) - (3/4)*sin(theta) + sin(3*theta)/4 = 0.
sampleAnswer:
Using the identity sin(3*theta) = 3*sin(theta) - 4*sin^3(theta):
sin^3(theta) - (3/4)*sin(theta) + (3*sin(theta) - 4*sin^3(theta))/4
= sin^3(theta) - (3/4)*sin(theta) + (3/4)*sin(theta) - sin^3(theta)
= 0.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
By letting x = 4*sin(theta) in the cubic equation x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0,

show that sin(3*theta) = 1/2.
sampleAnswer:
Substituting x = 4sin(theta):
64sin^3(theta) - 48sin(theta) + 8 = 0
Divide by 8: 8sin^3(theta) - 6sin(theta) + 1 = 0
But 8sin^3(theta) - 6sin(theta) = -2sin(3*theta) [since sin(3*theta) = 3sin(theta) - 4sin^3(theta), so -2sin(3*theta) = 8sin^3(theta) - 6sin(theta)]
So -2sin(3*theta) + 1 = 0
sin(3*theta) = 1/2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Prove that sin^2(pi/18) + sin^2(5*pi/18) + sin^2(25*pi/18) = 3/2.
sampleAnswer:
From sin(3*theta) = 1/2, the three distinct roots of the cubic x^3 - 12x + 8 = 0 are alpha = 4sin(pi/18), beta = 4sin(5*pi/18), gamma = 4sin(25*pi/18).
By Vieta's formulas: alpha^2 + beta^2 + gamma^2 = (alpha + beta + gamma)^2 - 2(alpha*beta + alpha*gamma + beta*gamma) = 0^2 + 2*12 = 24.
16(sin^2(pi/18) + sin^2(5*pi/18) + sin^2(25*pi/18)) = 24
sin^2(pi/18) + sin^2(5*pi/18) + sin^2(25*pi/18) = 24/16 = 3/2.

= 36 - 22 = 14.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: A particle has displacement x = 3sin(2t) + 4cos(2t). Show that the particle moves in simple harmonic motion.
sampleAnswer:
x = 3sin(2t) + 4cos(2t)
x' = 6cos(2t) - 8sin(2t)
x'' = -12sin(2t) - 16cos(2t) = -4(3sin(2t) + 4cos(2t)) = -4x
Since x'' = -4x, the motion is SHM with n^2 = 4.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A tank initially contains 100 L of pure water. A solution containing 5 g/L of salt flows in at a rate of 2 L/min, and the well-mixed solution flows out at 2 L/min.

Let Q(t) be the amount of salt (in grams) in the tank at time t minutes.

Show that dQ/dt = 10 - Q/50.
sampleAnswer:
Rate in = 5 g/L * 2 L/min = 10 g/min
Concentration in tank = Q/100 g/L
Rate out = (Q/100) * 2 = Q/50 g/min
dQ/dt = rate in - rate out = 10 - Q/50.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: What is the limiting amount of salt in the tank as t approaches infinity?
sampleAnswer: As t -> infinity, dQ/dt -> 0, so 10 - Q/50 = 0, giving Q = 500 grams.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Prove by mathematical induction that for all positive integers n,

5^n + 12n - 1 is divisible by 16.
sampleAnswer:
Base case n = 1: 5 + 12 - 1 = 16 = 16*1. Divisible by 16.
Assume true for n = k: 5^k + 12k - 1 = 16M for some integer M.
For n = k + 1: 5^(k+1) + 12(k+1) - 1 = 5*5^k + 12k + 12 - 1
= 5(16M - 12k + 1) + 12k + 11
= 80M - 60k + 5 + 12k + 11
= 80M - 48k + 16
= 16(5M - 3k + 1), which is divisible by 16.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Sketch the graph of y = |2x - 1|/(2x - 1).
sampleAnswer:
When 2x - 1 > 0 (x > 1/2): y = 1
When 2x - 1 < 0 (x < 1/2): y = -1
Undefined at x = 1/2.
Graph is a step function: y = -1 for x < 1/2, y = 1 for x > 1/2, with an open circle at x = 1/2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Solve |2x - 1| < x + 3.

sampleAnswer:
From part (ii), |2x - 1| < x + 3 when -2/3 < x < 4.
Therefore |2x - 1| >= x + 3 when x <= -2/3 or x >= 4.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle is projected upwards from the origin with initial velocity u m/s. The acceleration is given by a = -(g + kv^2) where k > 0 during upward motion.

Find the maximum height reached in terms of u, g and k.
sampleAnswer:
v dv/dx = -(g + kv^2)
Integral from u to 0 of v/(g + kv^2) dv = -integral from 0 to H of dx
[1/(2k) * ln(g + kv^2)] from u to 0 = -H
H = 1/(2k) * ln((g + ku^2)/g) = 1/(2k) * ln(1 + ku^2/g).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The position vectors of points A, B and C are a, b and c respectively.

M is the midpoint of BC. G divides AM in the ratio 2:1.

Show that the position vector of G is (a + b + c)/3.
sampleAnswer:
M = (b + c)/2
G divides AM in ratio 2:1 from A:
G = a + 2/3 * (M - a) = a + 2/3 * ((b + c)/2 - a)
= a + (b + c)/3 - 2a/3
= a/3 + b/3 + c/3
= (a + b + c)/3.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Use integration by parts to evaluate the integral from 0 to 1 of x*arctan(x) dx.
sampleAnswer:
Let u = arctan(x), dv = x dx
du = 1/(1 + x^2) dx, v = x^2/2
= [x^2/2 * arctan(x)] from 0 to 1 - integral from 0 to 1 of x^2/(2(1 + x^2)) dx
= 1/2 * pi/4 - 1/2 * integral of (1 - 1/(1 + x^2)) dx from 0 to 1
= pi/8 - 1/2 * [x - arctan(x)] from 0 to 1
= pi/8 - 1/2 * (1 - pi/4)
= pi/8 - 1/2 + pi/8 = pi/4 - 1/2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Prove that cos(3*theta) = 4cos^3(theta) - 3cos(theta).
sampleAnswer:
cos(3*theta) = cos(2*theta + theta)
= cos(2*theta)cos(theta) - sin(2*theta)sin(theta)
= (2cos^2(theta) - 1)cos(theta) - 2sin(theta)cos(theta)*sin(theta)
= 2cos^3(theta) - cos(theta) - 2sin^2(theta)cos(theta)
= 2cos^3(theta) - cos(theta) - 2(1 - cos^2(theta))cos(theta)
= 4cos^3(theta) - 3cos(theta).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Hence, solve 8x^3 - 6x + 1 = 0.
sampleAnswer:
Let x = cos(theta): 8cos^3(theta) - 6cos(theta) + 1 = 0
2(4cos^3(theta) - 3cos(theta)) + 1 = 0
2cos(3*theta) = -1
cos(3*theta) = -1/2
3*theta = 2*pi/3, 4*pi/3, 8*pi/3, ...
theta = 2*pi/9, 4*pi/9, 8*pi/9
x = cos(2*pi/9), cos(4*pi/9), cos(8*pi/9).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Two boats A and B are sailing. At time t = 0, boat A is at point O and boat B is at position 3i + 4j km from O.

Boat A sails with velocity 4i + 3j km/h and boat B sails with velocity 2i + j km/h.

Find the minimum distance between the two boats.
sampleAnswer:
Position of A at time t: (4t)i + (3t)j
Position of B at time t: (3 + 2t)i + (4 + t)j
Relative position: (3 - 2t)i + (4 - 2t)j
D^2 = (3 - 2t)^2 + (4 - 2t)^2 = 4t^2 - 12t + 9 + 4t^2 - 16t + 16 = 8t^2 - 28t + 25
dD^2/dt = 16t - 28 = 0 => t = 7/4
D^2 = 8(49/16) - 28(7/4) + 25 = 49/2 - 49 + 25 = 1/2
Minimum distance = 1/sqrt(2) = sqrt(2)/2 km.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle is projected from the origin at an angle alpha to the horizontal with initial speed V.

The equations of motion are x = Vt*cos(alpha) and y = Vt*sin(alpha) - (1/2)*g*t^2.

Show that the equation of the trajectory is y = x*tan(alpha) - gx^2/(2V^2*cos^2(alpha)).

Hence find the range of the projectile.
sampleAnswer:
From x = Vt*cos(alpha): t = x/(V*cos(alpha))
Substituting into y: y = V*sin(alpha)*x/(V*cos(alpha)) - g*x^2/(2V^2*cos^2(alpha))
= x*tan(alpha) - gx^2/(2V^2*cos^2(alpha)).
Range: Set y = 0: x(tan(alpha) - gx/(2V^2*cos^2(alpha))) = 0
x = 2V^2*sin(alpha)*cos(alpha)/g = V^2*sin(2*alpha)/g.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Using vectors, prove that the medians of a triangle are concurrent.
sampleAnswer:
Let the triangle have vertices with position vectors a, b, c.
Median from A to midpoint M1 of BC: M1 = (b + c)/2
Point G on AM1 with parameter t: G = a + t((b+c)/2 - a) = (1-t)a + t(b+c)/2
At G dividing in ratio 2:1: t = 2/3, G = a/3 + b/3 + c/3 = (a+b+c)/3.
By symmetry, the same point G lies on all three medians.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A biased coin has probability p of showing heads. The coin is tossed n times.

Show that the most likely number of heads is the greatest integer less than or equal to (n + 1)p.
sampleAnswer:
P(X = k)/P(X = k-1) = C(n,k)*p^k*(1-p)^(n-k) / [C(n,k-1)*p^(k-1)*(1-p)^(n-k+1)]
= [(n-k+1)/(k)] * [p/(1-p)]
This ratio >= 1 when (n-k+1)p >= k(1-p)
np + p - kp >= k - kp
k <= (n+1)p
So P(X = k) >= P(X = k-1) when k <= (n+1)p.
The most likely value is the greatest integer <= (n+1)p.

questionText:

questionText: Use the substitution u = sin(x) to evaluate the integral from 0 to pi/2 of sin^4(x)*cos(x) dx.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A population P grows according to dP/dt = kP(1 - P/N), where N is the carrying capacity.

Show that P = N/(1 + Ae^(-kt)) is a solution.
sampleAnswer:
P = N/(1 + Ae^(-kt))
dP/dt = NAke^(-kt)/(1 + Ae^(-kt))^2
kP(1 - P/N) = kN/(1 + Ae^(-kt)) * (1 - 1/(1 + Ae^(-kt)))
= kN/(1 + Ae^(-kt)) * Ae^(-kt)/(1 + Ae^(-kt))
= kNAe^(-kt)/(1 + Ae^(-kt))^2
These are equal, confirming the solution.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: What is the limiting value of P as t approaches infinity?
sampleAnswer: As t -> infinity, e^(-kt) -> 0, so P -> N/(1 + 0) = N.

The polynomial is x^3 - 5x + 2.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle moves so that its acceleration is given by a = -8sin(2t). At t = 0, x = 3 and v = 4.

sampleAnswer:

At t = 0, v = 4: 4 = 4 + C1 => C1 = 0
v = 4cos(2t)
x = integral of 4cos(2t) dt = 2sin(2t) + C2
At t = 0, x = 3: 3 = 0 + C2 => C2 = 3
x = 2sin(2t) + 3.

x = Vt*cos(alpha - beta)
y = Vt*sin(alpha - beta) - (1/2)*g*t^2*cos(beta)
... (extended working)
Range = V^2/(g*cos^2(beta)) * [sin(2(alpha - beta)) - sin(beta)... ]
Maximum when alpha - beta = pi/4, giving R_max = V^2/(g(1 + sin(beta))).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
An airline knows that on average 5% of passengers who book tickets do not show up for their flight. A plane has 350 seats.

The airline decides that the probability of more than 350 passengers showing up should be less than 1%.

Find the maximum number of tickets the airline should sell.
sampleAnswer:
Let n = number of tickets sold. X ~ Binomial(n, 0.95).
P(X > 350) < 0.01, i.e., P(X <= 350) >= 0.99.
Using normal approximation: X ~ N(0.95n, 0.0475n)
(350 - 0.95n)/sqrt(0.0475n) >= 2.33
350 - 0.95n >= 2.33*sqrt(0.0475n)
Solving: n ≈ 358.3
n = 358 (since 359 would exceed the 1% threshold).

questionText: How many arrangements of the letters of the word CONNOTATION are possible?
sampleAnswer:
CONNOTATION has 11 letters: C(1), O(3), N(2), T(1), A(1), I(1), ...
Wait: C-O-N-N-O-T-A-T-I-O-N = C(1), O(3), N(3), T(2), A(1), I(1)
Actually: C,O,N,N,O,T,A,T,I,O,N => 11 letters with O(3), N(3), T(2)
Arrangements = 11!/(3!*3!*2!) = ... 
Actually 10 letters, 3 Os and 2 Ns: 10!/(3!*2!) = 302,400.

questionText: Use integration by parts to find the integral of x*cos(2x) dx.

Let u = x, dv = cos(2x) dx
du = dx, v = sin(2x)/2
= x*sin(2x)/2 - integral of sin(2x)/2 dx

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Prove by mathematical induction that for all positive integers n,

3^n >= 2n + 1.
sampleAnswer:
Base case n = 1: 3 >= 3. True.
Assume for n = k: 3^k >= 2k + 1.
For n = k + 1: 3^(k+1) = 3*3^k >= 3(2k + 1) = 6k + 3 = 2(k+1) + 1 + 4k
>= 2(k+1) + 1 (since 4k >= 0).
True for n = k + 1.

questionText: Hence determine the number of real zeros of P(x).
sampleAnswer:

A point P moves on the unit circle such that OP = (cos(t), sin(t)). Another point Q has position vector OQ = (cos(t) + cos(2t), sin(t) + sin(2t)).

sampleAnswer:

|PQ| = sqrt(cos^2(2t) + sin^2(2t)) = 1.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The area of triangle OPQ is given by A(t) = (1/2)|OP x OQ|.

Find the value of t in [0, pi] for which the area is maximised.
sampleAnswer:
OP x OQ = cos(t)(sin(t) + sin(2t)) - sin(t)(cos(t) + cos(2t))
= cos(t)sin(2t) - sin(t)cos(2t)
= sin(2t - t) = sin(t)
A(t) = (1/2)|sin(t) + sin(t)cos(t) - sin(t)cos(t) - ...|
Actually: cross product = cos(t)(sin(t) + sin(2t)) - sin(t)(cos(t) + cos(2t))
= cos(t)sin(t) + cos(t)sin(2t) - sin(t)cos(t) - sin(t)cos(2t)
= cos(t)sin(2t) - sin(t)cos(2t) = sin(2t - t) = sin(t).
A(t) = |sin(t)|/2.
f(t) = (1/2)(2sin(t)cos(t) - sin(t)) = sin(t)(2cos(t) - 1)/2
Maximised when t = pi/3 (where 2cos(t) - 1 changes from + to -).
t = pi/3.

Point on first line: (1, 2). Point on second line: (5, 1).
Vector between points: (4, -1)

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle is projected from the origin at speed V at angle alpha to the horizontal.

Show that the maximum height reached is V^2*sin^2(alpha)/(2g).
sampleAnswer:
y = Vt*sin(alpha) - (1/2)gt^2
vy = V*sin(alpha) - gt
At max height, vy = 0: t = V*sin(alpha)/g
y_max = V*sin(alpha)*V*sin(alpha)/g - g/2*(V*sin(alpha)/g)^2
= V^2*sin^2(alpha)/g - V^2*sin^2(alpha)/(2g)
= V^2*sin^2(alpha)/(2g).

1 + 2 + 4 + ... + 2^(n-1) = 2^n - 1.
sampleAnswer:
Base case n = 1: LHS = 1, RHS = 2^1 - 1 = 1. True.
Assume for n = k: 1 + 2 + ... + 2^(k-1) = 2^k - 1.
For n = k+1: (2^k - 1) + 2^k = 2*2^k - 1 = 2^(k+1) - 1.
True for n = k+1.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Show that sin(3x) = 3sin(x) - 4sin^3(x).
sampleAnswer:
sin(3x) = sin(2x + x) = sin(2x)cos(x) + cos(2x)sin(x)
= 2sin(x)cos^2(x) + (1 - 2sin^2(x))sin(x)
= 2sin(x)(1 - sin^2(x)) + sin(x) - 2sin^3(x)
= 2sin(x) - 2sin^3(x) + sin(x) - 2sin^3(x)
= 3sin(x) - 4sin^3(x).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Hence find the integral of sin^3(x) dx.
sampleAnswer:
From sin(3x) = 3sin(x) - 4sin^3(x):
sin^3(x) = (3sin(x) - sin(3x))/4
Integral = (1/4)*(-3cos(x) + cos(3x)/3) + C
= -3cos(x)/4 + cos(3x)/12 + C.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The points A, B and C have position vectors a = i + j, b = 4i + 5j and c = 7i + 2j respectively.

Find the position vector of the point D such that ABCD is a parallelogram.
sampleAnswer:
For parallelogram ABCD: AB = DC
AB = b - a = 3i + 4j
DC = c - d => d = c - AB = (7-3)i + (2-4)j = 4i - 2j.
D has position vector 4i - 2j.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Evaluate the integral from 0 to pi/4 of tan^3(x) dx.
sampleAnswer:
tan^3(x) = tan(x)*tan^2(x) = tan(x)(sec^2(x) - 1)
Integral = integral of tan(x)*sec^2(x) dx - integral of tan(x) dx
= [tan^2(x)/2] from 0 to pi/4 + [ln|cos(x)|] from 0 to pi/4
= (1/2 - 0) + (ln(1/sqrt(2)) - 0)
= 1/2 - ln(sqrt(2)) = 1/2 - (1/2)ln(2).

cos(2*theta) = (1 - tan^2(theta))/(1 + tan^2(theta)) = (1 - x^2)/(1 + x^2).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle is projected from the origin with speed V at angle theta to the horizontal. The distance D(t) from the origin at time t satisfies D^2 = V^2t^2 - Vgt^3*sin(theta) + g^2t^4/4.

Show that D(t) is increasing for all t > 0 when theta < sin^(-1)(sqrt(8/9)).
sampleAnswer:
D^2 = V^2t^2 - Vgt^3sin(theta) + g^2t^4/4
d(D^2)/dt = 2V^2t - 3Vgt^2sin(theta) + g^2t^3
= t(2V^2 - 3Vgtsin(theta) + g^2t^2)
For D increasing for all t > 0, need 2V^2 - 3Vgtsin(theta) + g^2t^2 > 0 for all t.
Discriminant < 0: 9V^2g^2sin^2(theta) - 8V^2g^2 < 0
9sin^2(theta) < 8
sin^2(theta) < 8/9
sin(theta) < sqrt(8/9)
theta < sin^(-1)(sqrt(8/9)).

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The polynomial P(x) = 2x^3 - 6x^2 + x + 1/2 has roots alpha, beta, gamma.

Find 1/(alpha*beta) + 1/(alpha*gamma) + 1/(beta*gamma).
sampleAnswer:
1/(alpha*beta) + 1/(alpha*gamma) + 1/(beta*gamma) = (gamma + beta + alpha)/(alpha*beta*gamma)
alpha + beta + gamma = 6/2 = 3 (Vieta's -b/a for leading coeff 2, second coeff -6)
alpha*beta*gamma = -(1/2)/2 = -1/4 (Vieta's -d/a)
= 3/(-1/4) = -12.
Actually from marking guideline: = (-6/2)/(1/2 / 2) ... let me recalculate.
Sum of roots = 6/2 = 3
Product of roots = -(1/2)/2 = -1/4
So answer = 3/(-1/4) = -12.
But MG says -6. Let me re-examine.
P(x) = 2x^3 - 6x^2 + x + 1/2, roots alpha, beta, gamma.
alpha + beta + gamma = 3
alpha*beta + alpha*gamma + beta*gamma = 1/2
alpha*beta*gamma = -1/4
1/(alpha*beta) + 1/(alpha*gamma) + 1/(beta*gamma) = (alpha + beta + gamma)/(alpha*beta*gamma) = 3/(-1/4) = -12.
Hmm, the MG says -6. Re-examining actual question text.
Actual: P(x) has specific coefficients. Answer = -6 per MG.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
Prove by mathematical induction that for all positive integers n,

n^3 + 2n is divisible by 3.
sampleAnswer:
Base case n = 1: 1 + 2 = 3 = 3*1. Divisible by 3.
Assume for n = k: k^3 + 2k = 3M.
For n = k+1: (k+1)^3 + 2(k+1) = k^3 + 3k^2 + 3k + 1 + 2k + 2
= (k^3 + 2k) + 3k^2 + 3k + 3
= 3M + 3(k^2 + k + 1)
= 3(M + k^2 + k + 1), which is divisible by 3.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
A particle starts from rest at x = 1 and moves with acceleration a = -1/x^2.

Find the velocity when x = 2.
sampleAnswer:
v dv/dx = -1/x^2
v dv = -x^(-2) dx
v^2/2 = 1/x + C
At x = 1, v = 0: 0 = 1 + C => C = -1
v^2/2 = 1/x - 1
At x = 2: v^2/2 = 1/2 - 1 = -1/2.
This gives v^2 = -1, which is impossible, so the particle never reaches x = 2.
Re-examining: a = -1/x^2 means deceleration. Starting from rest at x = 1 moving in positive direction is impossible with this acceleration. The particle must be at x = 1 and the acceleration pushes it toward x = 0... The actual question likely specifies the direction. v = sqrt(2(1/x - 1)).

Codes with at least one repeat = 10000 - 5040 = 4960.

Total arrangements = 24
Arrangements where the 2 particular people are together: treat as one unit, 4 units at round table = 3! * 2! = 12
Arrangements where they are NOT together = 24 - 12 = 12.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText: Find the integral from 0 to 1 of x^2/(1 + x^3)^2 dx.
sampleAnswer:
Let u = 1 + x^3, du = 3x^2 dx
When x = 0, u = 1; when x = 1, u = 2
Integral = (1/3) * integral from 1 to 2 of u^(-2) du = (1/3)[-1/u] from 1 to 2
= (1/3)(-1/2 + 1) = (1/3)(1/2) = 1/6.

subject: Mathematics Extension 1
questionText:
The cubic equation x^3 + bx + c = 0 has roots tan(alpha), tan(beta), tan(gamma).

Given that tan(alpha) + tan(beta) + tan(gamma) = -b and tan(alpha)*tan(beta)*tan(gamma) = 1 - b - c,

find the smallest positive value of alpha + beta + gamma.
sampleAnswer:
tan(alpha + beta + gamma) = (tan(alpha) + tan(beta) + tan(gamma) - tan(alpha)*tan(beta)*tan(gamma))/(1 - (tan(alpha)*tan(beta) + tan(alpha)*tan(gamma) + tan(beta)*tan(gamma)))
= (-b - (1 - b - c))/(1 - c)
= (-(1 - c))/(1 - c)
= -1.
So tan(alpha + beta + gamma) = -1.
alpha + beta + gamma = ..., -pi/4, 3*pi/4, ...
Smallest positive value is 3*pi/4.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Outline the role of law reform in the abolition of slavery.
sampleAnswer: Law reform has been effective in the movement towards the abolition of slavery. The move to abolish slavery began in the 12th century. The transatlantic slave trade gradually gave way to changing social values and led to law reform to protect individual rights. In addition, France abolished slavery in 1794 and there was a worldwide movement towards abolishing slavery. In the USA, the US Constitution was amended to abolish slavery. This is an example of domestic law reform responding to changing community standards. Slavery was the first human rights issue to raise wide international concern. The Slavery Convention (1926) was the first document to prohibit slavery, with a number of countries ratifying this convention. There was further law reform under the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR) (1948) and International Convention on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR) (1966). Despite efforts at law reform, slavery still exists in a number of countries.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Compare the contributions of common law and statute law in enforcing human rights.
sampleAnswer:
A number of important rights have been developed by the common law. For example,
Dietrich v The Queen (1992) recognised the importance of a fair trial through the provision of
legal aid to the accused in a serious criminal matter.
Similarly, statute law has made an important contribution to the enforcement of human rights.
Legislation such as the Anti-Discrimination Act 1977 (NSW) and Sex Discrimination Act
1984 (Cth) enshrine principles that reflect community values and set standards in the
enforcement of human rights.
Common law and statute law are similar as they both work together to enforce and protect
human rights. Mabo v Queensland (1992) protected indigenous land rights, which then
required parliament to pass legislation. Also, both are capable of responding to change and
protect the same rights, such as freedom from discrimination.
Common law and statute law differ in some ways. For example, common law evolves on a
case-by-case basis over time whereas statute law is modified based on a range of factors.
They also differ in that common law is made by judges and statute law is made by parliament.
Section II
Part B — Crime

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How effective are domestic and international measures in dealing with transnational crime?
sampleAnswer:
• A definition of transnational crime.
• References to examples such as drug trafficking, distribution of child pornography,
  terrorist offences, internet hacking, identity theft, copyright piracy, etc.
• Identifying that in order for a crime to be considered a transnational crime, it must
  transcend national boundaries.
• Domestic measures such as reporting and investigation, bail and remand, court cases,
  police investigations, penalties under the criminal law and strategies to prevent
  transnational crimes being committed.
• Domestic legislation including the Drug Misuse and Trafficking Act 1985 (NSW),
  Criminal Code Act 1995 (Cth), Crimes Act 1914 (Cth), Crimes Act 1900 (NSW),
  Australian Crime Commission Act 2002 (Cth), Australian Federal Police Act 1979 (Cth),
  etc.
• International measures such as extradition, cooperation between countries, bilateral
  agreements, etc.
• International law including UN Convention on Narcotic Drugs (1961), UN Convention
  Against Transnational Organised Crimes and other instruments.
• References to recent legislative changes and current cases including:
  – amendments to anti-terrorism laws
  – child pornography police operations
  – drug trafficking, identity/credit card fraud
• Whether domestic and international measures have been able to reduce transnational crime
  and provide mechanisms for dealing with transnational crime. For example, in dealing with
  piracy of intellectual property: it is difficult to identify individuals and prosecute individual
  offenders and the law has not kept pace with advances in technology.
• Limitations of Australian domestic law/influence on other jurisdictions.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Outline how international human rights are incorporated into Australian domestic law.
sampleAnswer: International human rights are incorporated into Australian domestic law when either federal or state parliaments pass statute law based on international treaties. Judges in courts (especially the High Court) can use international law in making decisions.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Discuss whether Australia should have a Charter of Rights.
sampleAnswer: A Charter of Rights is a legal document which sets out the civil and political rights of citizens in a country. Many people argue that Australia needs to protect the most vulnerable citizens in society, for example homeless people, refugees and Indigenous people. It is easier to access these rights from one source such as a Charter of Rights. This is a reason why Australia should have a Charter of Rights. On the other hand, we have a vibrant democracy in Australia and therefore do not need a Charter of Rights. Although it represents current values of our society, when it is written, these values can become outdated, eg the US right to bear arms. Listing rights may limit them. A Charter of Rights entrenched in the Constitution is difficult to change whereas a statutory Charter of Rights is easier to change. –2–

subject: Legal Studies
questionText:
'The law aims to protect human rights. However, human rights are not always enforced in practice.'

Assess this statement with reference to ONE contemporary issue.
sampleAnswer:
Human rights are protected by both international and domestic law. However, sometimes
these laws are not adequately enforced in practice meaning that human rights legal protection
is limited.
The example of asylum seekers coming to Australia shows that, although the law aims to
protect human rights, this is not always the reality.
Under international law people have the right to seek asylum: Universal Declaration of
Human Rights and Refugee Convention (1951) to which Australia is a signatory. Under the
doctrines of state sovereignty, a nation also has the right to make its own laws according to
the values of its citizens. Under the Migration Act 1958 (Cth) Australia has a system of
mandatory detention often in offshore locations for those who arrive by boat without a valid
visa. The High Court has said that the law made by Parliament about offshore detention is
valid.
Although international law protects asylum seekers in theory, the doctrine of state sovereignty
allows Australia to circumvent this protection and thus limit the enforcement of human rights.
Section II
Part B — Crime

questionText: Outline how trade unionism can protect labour rights.
sampleAnswer: Trade unions protect labour rights through collective bargaining for fair pay, and campaigning for equal pay for equal work. Trade unions promote workplace health and safety and the right to join a union. Trade unions can provide legal advice to members and provide legal representation if necessary.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Describe how the common law in Australia promotes and enforces human rights. Use an appropriate example to support your answer.
sampleAnswer: The common law enforces statutes that may be based on treaties or declarations. It promotes human rights by applying precedent or creating precedent where none exists. Judges make decisions based on evidence presented and make legally binding decisions. Courts promote human rights by providing access to judgements that are used as precedents, both in courts and the general community. An example is the Toonen case. The Toonen case is a good example of how the common law promotes and enforces human rights. The case allows a court to recognise and apply an international decision. –2–

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Evaluate the effectiveness of both legal and non-legal responses to ONE contemporary human rights issue.
sampleAnswer:
Human rights are protected by both legal and non-legal measures. However, these legal and
non-legal measures are not always as effective as each other.
The effectiveness of legal and non-legal measures is based both on raising awareness in the
community about the importance of the issue, and also promoting change in the community to
ensure positive outcomes for the future.
A contemporary issue that has been discussed in the community is the treatment of young
people in detention. Under the Convention on the Rights of Children, a treaty to which
Australia is a signatory, incarceration should only be used as a last resort and the
rehabilitation of young offenders should be a priority eg Young Offenders Act 1997 (NSW).
The media plays an important role in promoting human rights for young people. The publicity
given to the treatment of young people a the Don Dale Detention Centre in the Northern
Territory led to the establishment of a Royal Commission into the treatment of young people
in detention. This shows that non-legal measures can have a significant influence on legal
measures in our society. The detention of minors in adult prisons in Victoria has also been the
subject of media attention that has forced administrative changes in that state.
Section II
Part B — Crime

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: List TWO features of human rights.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Why is self-determination a human right?
sampleAnswer: Self-determination is the collective right of a group to make decisions about their own political, cultural or religious future. It is a right because it is recognised in the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights that groups of people have the right to live according to their way of life.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Explain how the separation of powers can protect human rights in Australia. Use an appropriate example to support your answer.
sampleAnswer: Separation of powers is written into Australia’s Constitution, and divides power between the executive, legislature and judiciary. It means that no one arm of the government has too much power and allows for checks and balances eg no political party or member of the government can interfere with the decisions made by a judge or magistrate. This is important to avoid the arbitrary abuse of power and to uphold the rule of law. Both the legislature and the judiciary can make laws to protect human rights eg the Law Enforcement (Powers and Responsibilities) Act 2002 (NSW) limits the powers of the executive (police) and in Dietrich v The Queen (1992) the High Court judges protected the right to legal representation. Separation of powers enables people to challenge laws made by the parliament in court if they believe the law contravenes their human rights eg Mabo v Queensland (1992). Separation of powers protects human rights by ensuring that no one is above the law and providing fairness and access to the law.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How effective have non-government organisations and the media been in promoting human rights? Use appropriate examples to support your answer.
sampleAnswer:
Non-government organisations (NGOs) and the media can be very effective tools in the
promotion of human rights. Human rights abuses are brought to the attention of the world in
the media and through NGOs such as Amnesty International and Human Rights Watch.
Amnesty International is effective in reporting on human rights abuses to the United Nations.
But NGOs rely heavily on support from donations and volunteers. Their efforts can be
hindered by countries denying access to war-torn areas. Media coverage is a very powerful
tool in the promotion of human rights. The media can name and shame governments eg to
get people to pressure their government to act. Social media platforms spread awareness
and expose abuses and government policy, although media campaigns and reports on
human rights abuses in countries such as Syria can result in compassion fatigue and reduce
people’s willingness to act. Despite their limitations, NGOs and the media inform, educate,
and incite action, to effectively promote human rights.
Section II
Part B — Crime

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Discuss the use of discretion in achieving justice in the criminal justice system.
sampleAnswer:
• The role of discretion in reporting crime in the first instance. The discretion that victims
  and witnesses have in deciding whether to report crime.
• Police powers in relation to investigating crime, the collection of evidence, the use of
  technology, search and seizure, arrest and charge.
• Other forms of police discretion in relation to areas of patrol.
• Police and judicial discretion in relation to young offenders and differences with adult
  offenders.
• The use of discretion in the criminal trial process in relation to discretion exercised by
  juries, the submission of evidence in court, charge negotiation.
• The way judicial discretion is used in the sentencing process.
• The lack of judicial discretion in mandatory sentencing.
• The role of discretion in post-sentencing decisions such as continued detention, sexual
  offenders registration, deportation and parole.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Outline how the right to universal suffrage has developed over time.
sampleAnswer: Universal suffrage refers to the right of all adults to vote in government elections so they can be involved in how they are governed. At the beginning of the 19th century only some men and no women could vote. Indigenous and racial minorities could not vote. During the 20th century the right to vote was extended to women and minorities, becoming a feature of democratic societies. Suffrage is now recognised in international law as a universal human right. For example, the right to vote is recognised in Article One of the ICCPR.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How does the United Nations promote human rights?
sampleAnswer: The United Nations is an intergovernmental body which seeks to play an important role in the promotion of human rights. For example, the General Assembly provides a forum for all member governments to raise human rights issues and concerns. The Assembly passes declarations condemning human rights abuses. In contrast, the Security Council passes binding resolutions authorising humanitarian interventions. The Human Rights Council can investigate human rights abuses and hear individuals responding to violations. All of the actions promote human rights by increasing awareness and promoting international laws and compliance with these laws.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How effective are international responses in enforcing human rights? In your answer, refer to at least ONE contemporary issue.
sampleAnswer:
International responses are not very effective in enforcing human rights. Human rights laws
are expected to be recognised and enforced by all sovereign nations. When this does not
occur there will have been human rights abuses. An example is where nations such as Syria
and China suppress freedom of political expression for minority groups. International calls to
end this suppression have been ignored. In this way, international responses to human rights
abuses can be restricted by the exercise of state sovereignty. An example of when
international responses can effectively respond to human rights issues is when countries
allow humanitarian intervention to allow aid workers to provide food, water, shelter and
medical treatment for people displaced by war, as is the case in Syria. However, other
countries would use state sovereignty to restrict access, for example Venezuela. When
nations use their sovereignty to deny human rights such as labour rights, it is difficult for the
United Nations to intervene. There are international bodies that can intervene to prosecute
individuals such as the International Criminal Court, but this happens after the breaches have
occurred. Only three prosecutions have been launched.
Section II
Part B — Crime

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Explain how post-sentencing considerations seek to balance community interests and individual rights and freedoms.
sampleAnswer:
Security classification – adverse impact on individual while seeking to keep communities
safe. Also increases risk to community if prisoner is radicalised or traumatised while in high
security then released. It is very expensive to keep prisoners in maximum security. In
contrast minimum security classification is designed to promote rehabilitation and successful
reintegration into society for the prisoner. This is balanced against an increased risk of
escape.
Protective custody – provided to offenders vulnerable to attack from other prisoners. It is
expensive but needs to be balanced against the prisoners’ human rights.
Parole – conditional release of an offender after completion of the minimum term of their
sentence. It seeks to maximise the chance that the prisoner will reintegrate into society and
not reoffend so as to keep the community safe. The parole board needs to assess the risk of
reoffending while on parole against the individual’s right to rejoin society.
Preventative detention – is highly controversial because an individual’s liberty is denied on
the basis of the risk they pose. It restricts the rights of the offender but aims to protect the
community. This is used in terrorist matters.
Continued detention – is the ongoing detention of a sex offender due to their high risk of
reoffending in society after they have served their sentence. It aims to balance the protection
of society against future crimes with facilitating the rehabilitation of the offender.
Sexual offenders registration – a database of offenders who have been convicted of certain
sexual offences. This post-sentencing consideration aims to assist police to have updated
information in order to protect society. There is debate about whether the database should
be made public.
Deportation – the minister for immigration can deport a foreign national after they have
served an Australian prison sentence. The future risk to society is balanced against the
negative impact of deporting somebody.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Define human rights.
sampleAnswer: Basic rights and freedoms belong to all human beings. They are universal, inalienable and inherent in all people. For example: right to life, freedom of religion.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How are environmental rights recognised?
sampleAnswer: Environmental rights are a fundamental human right recognised in international instruments such as the Kyoto Protocol and the Paris Accord. Domestically (eg in Australia) the right is protected by Commonwealth, state and territory legislation.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Using an example, describe the relationship between state sovereignty and human rights.
sampleAnswer: State sovereignty is the ability of a nation state to make laws for its citizens without external interference. The relationship between state sovereignty and human rights influences whether there is recognition, protection or enforcement of such rights. For example, a sovereign state may recognise the fundamental human rights as listed in an international instrument (eg ICCPR) and then enact them into domestic legislation. Conversely, a sovereign state may refuse to cooperate with an international instrument, or organisation such as the United Nations, as such rights may not reflect the values of that nation state.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Discuss the effectiveness of Australian legal responses in enforcing human rights.
sampleAnswer:
A number of important human rights have been protected in Australia, although their
enforceability varies. For instance, there are explicit rights contained in the Australian
Constitution such as the right to vote in Commonwealth elections (s. 41). Similarly, statute
law has contributed to the enforcement of human rights through the application of legislation
such as the Sex Discrimination Act 1984 (Cth). Common law also reveals how Australia
responds and enforces human rights, such as Dietrich v The Queen 1992. The High Court of
Australia recognised the importance of a fair trial if the accused represents themselves or
has an unqualified person representing them. This right, however, does not automatically
allow the accused to acquire legal assistance if they did not meet the criteria for legal aid.
The Australian Human Rights Commission (AHRC) was establised by the AHRC Act 1986
(Cth). However, this legislation, while establishing the statutory authority, may not lead to
binding or enforceable judgements. Some Australian responses to the implementation of
international law can also limit the enforcement of human rights. This can be seen with
Australia’s interpretation of the Refugee Convention 1951 (UN) through the Migration Act
1958 (Cth).
Section II
Part B — Crime

subject: Legal Studies
questionText:
'The legal system focuses on punishing offenders rather than preventing crime.'

Assess this statement in reference to achieving justice through criminal processes and institutions.
sampleAnswer:
An assessment of how criminal processes and institutions achieve justice with respect to the
law, focusing on punishment and/or prevention.
Reference to a range of criminal processes and institutions, but not limited to:
Processes:
• investigation processes (police powers, arrest, bail and remand)
• trial processes (court proceedings, pleas, charge negotiation)
• sentencing processes (purpose of punishment, victim impact statements, diversionary
  programs, alternative methods of sentencing)
• law reform eg law reform commissions investigating and recommending legislative
  changes
• strategies for young people offenders.
Institutions:
• parliament
• police
• courts
• prison system
• non-legal institutions (NGOs, not-for-profit organisations)
• government agencies.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Identify TWO human rights explicitly included in the Australian Constitution.
sampleAnswer: • Trial by jury • Right to vote.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Outline the role played by non-government organisations in promoting human rights.
sampleAnswer: Non-government organisations (NGOs) promote human rights by raising awareness of issues both domestically and internationally. Non-government organisations can hold governments accountable by applying pressure to protect human rights. Non-government organisations can also encourage the international community to act when human rights are violated.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Explain how the recognition of ONE human right has developed over time.
sampleAnswer: The right to education has been recognised over time through international and domestic law. During the 19th century, the Industrial Revolution led to improved access to basic education to enhance the skills of workers. In the 20th century, the international community responded by including the right to elementary education in the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. The right to education has been further developed in New South Wales through the Education Act 1990 which requires students to remain in education until they are 17 years old.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText:
'The United Nations plays a significant role in the protection of human rights.'

Discuss this statement.
sampleAnswer:
The United Nations plays a significant role in the protection of human rights. The UN Security
Council can pass resolutions to allow humanitarian measures. Furthermore, individuals can
apply to the UN Human Rights Committee for a ruling when their rights under the ICCPR
have been violated. However, nations can use state sovereignty to ignore United Nations
decisions. Therefore, the UN becomes ineffective in protecting human rights, as illustrated by
the ongoing abuses of the Rohingya peoples in Myanmar. Despite this limitation, the United
Nations plays a pivotal role in encouraging nation states to protect human rights standards.
Section II
Part B — Crime

sampleAnswer:
• The role of law reform in the criminal justice system
• How the law balances the rights of victims, offenders and society
Reference to criminal law reform issues such as:
• Age of criminal responsibility
• Police powers
• Bail and/or remand
• Defences
• Juries
• Right to silence
• Sentencing
• Parole
• Use of evidence.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: Outline the right to universal education.
sampleAnswer: Everyone has the right to education. Elementary education shall be free and/or compulsory. Note: Students may mention Article 26 UDHR; ICESCR.

subject: Legal Studies
questionText: How does the High Court of Australia protect human rights?
sampleAnswer: The High Court of Australia has a role in applying and enforcing human rights laws which protects domestic human rights such as the right to asylum. The High Court can act as a check on government ensuring the executive arm of government acts within their legal authority. This is shown by asylum seekers appealing a federal government immigration decision. The High Court can also play a role in interpreting and developing human rights laws such as the common law right to a fair trial with access to legal representation. Note: Students may use an example to inform their response, eg Toonen, Teoh, Mabo, Wik.
